VZIAey6SW8k.author: @DrAlexWibberley
VZIAey6SW8k.text:
Hello everyone and thank you for watching! I really hope you found this video useful. I read all of your comments and try to reply to as many as I can every day across the channel. The community on this channel is absolutely fantastic.... people sharing their stories, supporting each other, and learning together. It's amazing to see. 

It means a lot to me that this has become a space where people are interested in the simple things that keep us healthy and prevent problems before they ever reach the hospital. If there’s a topic you’d like me to explain in a future video, please let me know in the comments.

If you’d like to support me, the best thing you can do is click the like button and subscribe. It really helps these videos reach more people who might benefit from them, and it allows me to keep making them! 

My goal with these videos is simple: to help as many people as possible stay healthier and avoid ending up in the emergency department if we can. 

Wishing you all the best, and thanks again!

Dr Alex 😊

KY9w7aPXLzw.author: @WiseAgingHub
KY9w7aPXLzw.text: Hello Dr. Marcus! This video is a true gift for those of us over 60. I was deeply impressed by the fact that we can regain 40% of our muscle strength in just 12 weeks. Seeing real success stories like Dorothy’s is so inspiring—it proves it’s never too late to start! The 3 exercises (Wall sits, Step-ups, and Single leg stands) seem simple, but the science behind them, especially regarding bone density, is incredible. Thank you for detailing the breathing techniques and proper foot placement. I’m starting my Wall sits today! Wishing you and your team great health as you continue helping our community. ❤🌿💪

KY9w7aPXLzw.author: @NobleAgingSpirit
KY9w7aPXLzw.text:
Dear Dr. Marcus, this video is an absolute masterpiece for the senior community! 🌟 I’m deeply impressed by the scientific evidence you provided, especially the 2023 meta-analysis regarding leg strength and mobility risks. 🚶‍♂ The way you explained the benefits of 'isometric tension' in wall sits and the 'unilateral training' of step-ups is so clear and professional. It’s truly inspiring to hear Dorothy and Robert’s success stories—it gives us so much hope! 📈

I’ve started my 30-second single leg stands today and can already feel the 'full body symphony' you mentioned! 🎻 Thank you for emphasizing that 'consistency beats intensity'—that's the golden rule I needed to hear. Your dedication to helping us maintain independence and even reverse bone loss is life-changing. I’ve subscribed and hit the notification bell to stay updated with your brilliant research-backed tips. Keep up the amazing work, Doctor! 🙌💎🩺

Q2oLST5Vn4I.author: @erikacomfort3343
Q2oLST5Vn4I.text: This video is literally so helpful for me right now. I’ve been experiencing consistent health anxiety for ten months following what we think was a bad viral reaction. The fact that I’m still not back to normal ten months after being sick leads my anxiety to think the doctors missed something or that there’s something deeper and harder to detect going on (even though all of my symptoms still reasonably align with post viral illnesses). My anxiety will take over when my symptoms get worse, or even if something triggers it randomly, and my brain will mistake that for intuition and think ‘I just know I’m dying, I can just feel it.’ Then I urgently want every medical test ever made. But if I give it a day or two, or even a few hours sometimes, I will return mostly back to normal once I calm down, especially if my symptoms subside again. This has been hell and this video(in addition to the health anxiety video) are lifesavers. It’s just my anxiety. It’s not my intuition.

EloLnKTw9BQ.author: @DrAlexWibberley
EloLnKTw9BQ.text:
Hello everyone and thank you for watching! I really hope you found this video useful. I read all of your comments and try to reply to as many as I can every day across the channel. The community on this channel is absolutely fantastic.... people sharing their stories, supporting each other, and learning together. It's amazing to see. 

It means a lot to me that this has become a space where people are interested in the simple things that keep us healthy and prevent problems before they ever reach the hospital. If there’s a topic you’d like me to explain in a future video, please let me know in the comments.

If you’d like to support me, the best thing you can do is click the like button and subscribe. It really helps these videos reach more people who might benefit from them, and it allows me to keep making them! 

My goal with these videos is simple: to help as many people as possible stay healthier and avoid ending up in the emergency department if we can. 

Wishing you all the best, and thanks again!

Dr Alex 😊

EloLnKTw9BQ.text: I'm 73 and have been resistance training for six  years (3x 10 minute workouts a week) while also walking 5-10Km a day.  I'm pharma-free.  No blood pressure meds, no blood-sugar meds, no analgesic meds.  I take a few simple supplements but nothing that is essential to my survival.   It saddens me to see so many of my peers chugging down a huge handful of pills each day when many of the conditions they suffer could be reduced or managed by simple lifestyle changes.

-oMr1fOoQtI.author: @SeniorHealthPrime
-oMr1fOoQtI.text: I’m 72 and that opening picture of standing at the bottom of the stairs honestly made my chest tighten, because that’s been my reality for a while now. I didn’t realize how much fear I’ve quietly built around those steps until I heard it described so clearly. What gave me hope was hearing that it’s not just “weak legs” or aging, but lost movement patterns that can actually be retrained. The stories about Margaret and Ruth really moved me, especially the part about getting back to their own bedrooms. I’ve been sleeping downstairs for over a year myself. This video made me feel like I might not be stuck here forever. Has anyone my age actually tried these exercises and seen changes yet?

-oMr1fOoQtI.author: @healthanchor-u5c
-oMr1fOoQtI.text: Can 5 simple moves really fix years of joint wear and tear, or are we just masking a bigger issue? I feel like without addressing underlying inflammation, these exercises might do more harm than good for some of us. Has anyone actually tried this without feeling more knee pain?

If stairs feel harder than they used to, you’re not alone — and it’s NOT just aging.

👉 Save this video and practice 20 minutes every other day for 8 weeks.

Small, consistent movements lead to big independence gains.

fWXSG6qIbOI.text:
1. Sitting for extended periods | Solution: Low level frequent activity
2. Too much visceral fat. Your waist should be less than half your height. 
3. Uncontrolled high BP - increases the risk of bleeding in the brain 
4. Consuming ultra processed foods
5. Drinking sugary drinks regularly - causes insulting resistance
6. Chronic sleep deprivation - causes cravings, impairs cognitive function, weakens immunity.
7. Chronic (i.e. long term) psychological stress - promotes abdominal fat accumulation
8. Processed meat consumption - group 1 carcinogens
9. Chronically low fiber intake
10. Poor dental hygiene 
11. Lacking cardiovascular fitness

wD96kN6At4A.author: @Wtwlr7230
wD96kN6At4A.text:
I’ve had three doctors literally throw up their hands and tell me they are out of ideas. I FINALLY got in to the Mayo Clinic, only to have my insurance company fighting me tooth and nail to avoid having to cover it. They want me to go everywhere, including universities in three surrounding states before they will consider it. My doctor did a peer-to-peer review with the insurance company doctor and, this will shock no one, the doctor who is paid by the insurance company refused my treatment. 

It could take me two years to get in to all of these places they want me to go if I have to exhaust one before starting the next. Meanwhile my quality of life is rapidly declining, but by golly, the insurance company will make sure they are massively profitable.

I watched your episode about menopause and autoimmune disease and how menopause can bring on autoimmune issues. I feel this is the case with me. I am going to see my gynecologist tomorrow to see if he needs to prescribe hormone replacement therapy for me. I have always been a pretty healthy active person all my life until I had my uterus taken out. I'm hoping the hormone replacement might help. Thankyou for having these discussions to help us better understand our conditions❤

LO97JGVdorM.author: @DrAlexWibberley
LO97JGVdorM.text:
Hello everyone and thank you for watching! I really hope you found this video useful. I read all of your comments and try to reply to as many as I can every day across the channel. The community on this channel is absolutely fantastic.... people sharing their stories, supporting each other, and learning together. It's amazing to see. 

It means a lot to me that this has become a space where people are interested in the simple things that keep us healthy and prevent problems before they ever reach the hospital. If there’s a topic you’d like me to explain in a future video, please let me know in the comments.

If you’d like to support me, the best thing you can do is click the like button and subscribe. It really helps these videos reach more people who might benefit from them, and it allows me to keep making them! 

My goal with these videos is simple: to help as many people as possible stay healthier and avoid ending up in the emergency department if we can. 

Wishing you all the best, and thanks again!

Dr Alex 😊

LO97JGVdorM.author: @elainehiggins713
LO97JGVdorM.text: My skin looked great until I was 68 years old. People were amazed when they found out how old I was. I attributed it mostly to avoiding sun exposure, never smoking or doing drugs, and drinking very little alcohol. Then came Covid. The acute phase only lasted a couple of weeks and it was not too bad. Unfortunately, I developed Long Covid (diagnosed by a rheumatologist) which lasted almost two years. I gained almost 25 lbs., going from 118 lbs. to 142 lbs. I am 5' 3". When I started feeling better, I began restricting carbs and eating within a 6-7 hour window. I lost the weight within six months. My skin did not react well and became loose and crepey, especially on my arms and legs. Ugh! I can no longer wear short sleeves or shorter skirts without looking at my awful skin. There is nothing I can do about this, short of plastic surgery which costs big $. I will simply live with it. I am seventy now and not really happy with my skin because aesthetics are really important to me. Oh, well. Watch out for rapid or large scale weight loss. It can wreak havoc on your skin.

LO97JGVdorM.author: @jelenajm1160
LO97JGVdorM.text: Bone loss also has a significant impact on the appearance of aging skin, but if often completely overlooked. Interestingly, there was a study done of changes in facial bones that happened over time that showed the the biggest age-related aging change in the bone was a reduction in the height of the lower jaw bone and the overall shrinking of the lower jaw. Often when this happens, people can develop increased skin laxity and jowling and are recommended facelifts and other cosmetic intervention that don't address one of the root causes of the issue  Another study that supports the impact of bone health on aging was a study done in Asia that tested the aging of Asian women who were shown to have less skin laxity and signs of aging. The researchers hypothesized that this was due to an increased intake of vitamin K2 from natto, a food commonly eaten in Asian. Vitamin K2 helps the body better absorb and utilize calcium, decreasing age related bone loss, thereby reducing laxity caused by bone loss

And when indicated, in a loving manner be honest about my experience in response to their mindlessness. They cannot and will not accept that honesty is an act of love. If I can't be honest with someone, I'm left in a position feeling vulnerable. Both my ANS and adrenals find that to be a fools game to engaging in the mental gymnastics required in the face of people who are emotionally blind. I believe strongly in what you shared, it will help immensely in other situations.

cZU_YnFp0is.author: @DrAlexWibberley
cZU_YnFp0is.text:
Hello everyone and thank you for watching! I really hope you found this video useful. I read all of your comments and try to reply to as many as I can every day across the channel. The community on this channel is absolutely fantastic.... people sharing their stories, supporting each other, and learning together. It's amazing to see. 

It means a lot to me that this has become a space where people are interested in the simple things that keep us healthy and prevent problems before they ever reach the hospital. If there’s a topic you’d like me to explain in a future video, please let me know in the comments.

If you’d like to support me, the best thing you can do is click the like button and subscribe. It really helps these videos reach more people who might benefit from them, and it allows me to keep making them! 

My goal with these videos is simple: to help as many people as possible stay healthier and avoid ending up in the emergency department if we can. 

Wishing you all the best, and thanks again!

Dr Alex 😊

cZU_YnFp0is.author: @margod5455
cZU_YnFp0is.text: I started watching your videos at the beginning of January and gave up sugar. Since then, I have also been doing intermittent fasting in hopes of reversing insulin resistance. Everyday you have given me encouragement and a wealth of information.  I was involved in a medical research project years ago where an Apple Watch was provided to me when they were beginning some biometrics, but it ended 3 years ago.  I just down loaded the app and now have my HRV info plus numerous other data.  I’m actually doing quite well for @ 72 year old and am motivated to keep it up - because what you spoke of yesterday was right on- I am much more worried about being a burden than I am of death itself.  THANK YOU!

cZU_YnFp0is.author: @lesski1965
cZU_YnFp0is.text: Last summer, I cycled from England to Bulgaria. I cycled 3202 miles in total over 7 weeks and 5 days.I was self supporting so I was carrying all of my equipment  - clothes, tent, tools etc. I was carrying a lot of weight. It took a couple of months after I`d finished this ride to get my HRV back into the green. My HRV was in the "dangerously low" range for quite a while. Most of the ride was done at zone 2, I don`t smoke or drink and I was eating very healthy options due to having limited cooking facilities. I returned to running at zone 2 pace after a few days rest.

cZU_YnFp0is.author: @andrewrevell9099
cZU_YnFp0is.text: HRV is one of my main training metrics. If my HRV is low then I either modify my training or take a days rest. Other sites I have visited suggest that HRV is highly individual, even amongst top athletes.

Hck2yBQPW7A.author: @jirislavicek9954
Hck2yBQPW7A.text:
This channel is becoming S-Tier 👍
We just need to rediscover the wisdom of our ancestors and apply it to modern world. Ancestors instinctlively understood the importance of fermentation,  maturing, ripening and preservation. Asian cultures never consumed soy directly, but always in fermented form. Sauerkraut was staple food of cold climate Europe, hunters and butchers matured venison and beef to release enzymes and make the meat more digestible and tastier. Farm houses made yoghurt and cheese, wine was drink of choice for the wealthy. Fruit was dried or fermented.
People need to understand that they are part of nature and cannot isolate themselves from it and cannot exist without it. Bacteria and viruses are part of nature, both, the good and the bad ones. To be healthy means to maintain equilibrium in the body rather than obsessing about healthy diet and minimizing exposure to bacteria.

tWcDQtfPmag.author: @suzieq7649
tWcDQtfPmag.text: I'm practically in tears watching this. You nailed my biggest fear - being dependent. I'm in my early 60s and live alone. I have lived a pretty active lifestyle (runner, hiker, on-my-feet-all-day-teacher ) until the last few years following retirement. I can tell my body has declined significantly because even though I still workout regularly, I am not as active during the day - I sit too much. But listening to you, I feel like I can turn this around and get back on track before it becomes irreversible. Your videos carry so much important information for people of all ages. The words "thank you" are not enough.

tWcDQtfPmag.author: @winterberry-wildlife-video
tWcDQtfPmag.text: Lots of doctors do tell patients to exercise more, eat better and lose weight. Problem is that most patients don’t do it and want an easy fix. It’s not doctors it’s our culture.

tWcDQtfPmag.author: @biolbd
tWcDQtfPmag.text: Well said. A close relative was in good health, very active, spry, etc. until at age 87, he was diagnosed with prostate cancer. The decision was made (by some family members) to have him undergo proton radiation therapy, which caused massive problems with urinary incontinence, and curtailed a lot of his activities. He then entered into the sick care system: meds for the incontinence, meds for depression, meds for cholesterol, etc. Even had to go to a cardiologist despite having zero heart problems. By age 90, he began having neurological issues that resembled a form of Parkinson’s and is now in assisted living and severely depressed about his situation. He has lost the ability to swallow or talk well, and is confined to a wheelchair yet his mind is still sharp, so that makes his situation even worse. One wonders if he might have been better off living with the cancer until his death.

tWcDQtfPmag.author: @cathyphillips679
tWcDQtfPmag.text: At age 70, I recognize that my body is not the same as it was. I am healthy, fit, and active. I am a healthy weight and take no meds. All that being said, I am still 70 years old! I think that a lot of decline is mostly neglect. Prioritize good sleep, eat real food, and keep moving! Someone told me that walking is just maintenance,  but strength training will keep you independent. I think that's true.

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @francoisleveille409
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: If you have autism and have no help you are confronted on a daily basis to one incredible hypocrisy ; you have poor social skills, you have no help, society tells you its all in your head yet anyone at any time can exploit this weakness to cause you incredible harm. This certainly can cause a lot of anxiety.

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @eledatowle8767
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: When this video was recommended in my feed, I looked at the title and honestly thought, "Oh, really?  Sounds like someone trying to tell me it's all in my head," and I started the video with my arms crossed, ready for a fight.  I was completely surprised by your take on this... and comforted that we're beginning to be understood!  What you've said here is all true.  I would, however, like to add that C-PTSD has a common co-occurrence with autism, so for some of us, it's both forms, all the time.  And because of the hypervigilence AND disregulation combination, my body often reacts like we're in danger, seemingly out of the blue, even when doing something blandly enjoyable in a safe space, with no added stressors.  Once the body reacts with racing heart and the feeling of electricity running through it, no amount of rationalizing or even de-stimulating stops it, but both are part of coping with it to keep it under control. I add this only to support others like me for whom everything is a little more "complex."

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @4wolves239
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: "Force tends to increase fragility rather than resilience" omg yes you summed up why all my life ive been able to "push" or "force" myself to do things and instead of it making me better I just feel worse, now that im older and am aware of my needs i find myself regressing , being unable to handle things i used to and beating myself up for it , i love these videos its like you know me

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @MentalHealthForMetalheads
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: "Lack of early warnings" ...yup, that sums up why I've suddenly flipped from fine to meltdown in 2 seconds for years

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @JAYSCHULBERG-b9u
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: When you mentioned the word "uncertainty", you had my full attention. Uncertainty is a major obstacle for me. A few years ago my wife and I were expecting visitors who said they would be at our home at 2:00 in the afternoon. They didn't show up until after 3:00. By the time they finally arrived, I was in no condition to do much visiting. I was completely overwhelmed by my wait and I had to excuse myself after the initial greetings. I have had similar experiences over the years, but I think that I became more susceptible to becoming overwhelmed by uncertainty since my middle 60's. Your video is very relatable. I will watch it again. Thank you!

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @bennielaars
KhHOtIiPhRI.text:
Speaking for myself, I have a small comfort zone and acting outside that for a long time (like at work) does wear you out.
You need to be able to spend a large part of your day back in your comfort zone to recharge.

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @itisdevonly
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: This is such a good, succinct, and accurate video on the topic. All mental health professionals should have this information, because it is so important to understand when dealing with autistic clients! The strategies that help with anxiety in non-autistics can actively harm autistic people, and the last thing you want to be doing as a therapist is making the problem worse instead of helping.

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @sigmainfjbulgaria4478

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @Larisa2005
KhHOtIiPhRI.text:
Thank you for this video! 
I fully agree with your point in here. 
But I think that lack of gradual tiredness signal is the other side of constant concious putting efforts to overcome our warning system to function in the normal world at all. 
There is no interaction with chaotic behaviour and uncertainity that DOES NOT trigger autistic warning system. 
We just forcibily shut it down to force ourselves to interact with the reality at all. 
Second, about 'weak break' for alert system. It's not the case: our alert system stays 'on' because even in the quiet refuge of my flat the main stress factor never stops: chaos I was not able to cope with now is still out there. 
Many years ago I quited my job which gave me complete shutdown (to the point I stopped to understand my native language in the peak stress) and I had been living for 2 years on my savings and with my sister's help. I tried to find my next job earlier but I started to cry in a job interview. They asked really insane questions to establish authority over a possible employee. The second time I was able to work only for a week before I got nervoius chest pain and difficulty of breathing in the workplace. 
I was able to mostly collect myself in these 2 years (and I have been working for 8 years since), but forcing myself to go out in the place where I have to solve the tasks I don't personally interested about with other people who don't care about these tasks either and who don't care about the effects of their own words and actions - it's forcing myself to take part in a constant war. 
How can I relax at home if my body and mind are screaming it is insanity to keep going this way?

KhHOtIiPhRI.author: @lorio7764
KhHOtIiPhRI.text: I have been telling my husband who's an engineer/farmer that my circuits are overloaded when my nervous system goes into meltdown or shutdown. I'm going to ask him to watch your video.

ElUfYaHrzyU.author: @MaryShephard-d3j
ElUfYaHrzyU.text: I’m a registered dietitian that specializes in CSID/sucrose intolerance, and I’m SO HAPPY to see more people talking about this! So many of my clients have gone years being told “it’s just IBS” or “it’s all in your head.” I was diagnosed at age 10, and there were absolutely zero resources to support the necessary diet changes. I’ve published cookbooks, meal plans, and other services to support this diet. It’s so satisfying getting to help people find symptom resolution after they’ve struggled for many years! Thank you for spreading awareness!

ElUfYaHrzyU.author: @Steve-the-Lion556
ElUfYaHrzyU.text: Just some info. I've been suffering with IBS/SIBO for about 23 years, but for the first time I've managed to have a bit of a breakthrough. Some weeks ago I started using magnesium oil. In the past I've taken magnesium powder and I never noticed any improvement. So a few weeks ago I started using about 25 sprays of magnesium oil around my chest and stomach, and after a few weeks I noticed that I was having less gas. I would say my gas has reduced by about 40-50%  So I'm not cured, but I can now eat carbs without getting a huge amount of gas. It has made a big difference. What I have learned is that the vagus nerve, as well as the nerves in the intestines, are very dependant on magnesium to work properly. My next step is to supplement with thiamine (vitamin B1) as a lack of B1 can also impair the vagus nerve. But to anyone who is suffering with IBS/SIBO, try the magnesium oil.

AtFHN88EGsM.author: @ni1disponiblecarays
AtFHN88EGsM.text:
A trick that works for me to eat more vegetables (and for my husband to eat them too _especially that last part_) is to make a universal base for vegetable soups. I use onion, leek, celery, carrot, etc. I cook it, blend it, and freeze it in 250 g portions. When you want a vegetable soup, put the base from the container into a pot, add the vegetables you want (or any leftovers you have at home), and cook for 8 to 15 minutes. I also use a universal spice mix to season the soup. Then blend it, add cream, yogurt, or olive oil plus the toppings you like, and it’s ready. For me, 250 ml of base makes about 1 liter of soup, and it saves me a lot of time.

Another trick is to have one jar of spices for each thing (homemade).

Another one is to season salmon portions with salt and pepper, wrap them in foil, and put them in the freezer. When you want salmon, take one packet (better if you defrost it the day before, but it’s not required), put it in the air fryer for 15 minutes just like that, without unwrapping it, then open it and eat. It turns out super juicy and tender.

I also keep all kinds of bags of vegetables in the freezer and boiled eggs in the fridge, but you already said that.

QCpZjpfHKCk.author: @DrDrayzday
QCpZjpfHKCk.text:
Hi guys. Thanks for watching.

Menopause supplements are everywhere right now, and many of them are marketed specifically for skin health. Some of these products do have emerging evidence behind them, but the benefits tend to be modest and the marketing often exaggerates what they can realistically do.

In this video I walk through five of the most commonly marketed supplements and what the science actually shows.

I’m curious to hear from you. Have you tried collagen peptides, hyaluronic acid, biotin, or any menopause “glow” supplements? What has your experience been?

Also let me know what menopause and skin health topics you would like me to cover in future videos. There is a lot of confusion around this stage of life and I’m happy to do deeper dives into the science.

Thanks again for being here!

GUsQnklKljk.author: @pavlinaambrozova7630
GUsQnklKljk.text:
These three probiotics have really helped me with leaky gut and IBS. I currently take a D-lactate–free probiotic powder (Lactobacillus rhamnosus, Lactobacillus salivarius, Bifidobacterium lactis, Bifidobacterium bifidum, Bifidobacterium infantis, Bifidobacterium longum) together with Microbiome Labs RestorFlora™ PD (Saccharomyces cerevisiae var. boulardii, Bacillus clausii, Bacillus subtilis).

 I was on a carnivore diet for about a year and a half, and I’ve now switched to a low-FODMAP diet, which continues to improve my symptoms.

After struggling with Clostridium difficile for a year, I finally recovered within one week. Saccharomyces cerevisiae var. boulardii, vancomycin, grape seed extract, and lumbrokinase enzymes made a huge difference for me — it honestly felt like a miracle.

ACmDxS1blnA.author: @aminotmerciful4353
ACmDxS1blnA.text: Simple carbs are single chain mono- or di- saccharides such as sucrose, lactose, fructose etc whereas complex carbs are polysaccharides such as those found in starches. Simple carbs can be absorbed into the bloodstream almost immediately whereas complex carbs need to be enzymatically broken down before they can be absorbed. To say white rice and brown rice are different in carbohydrate complexity is false, the difference is that brown rice retains more fibre and other nutrients as the whole grain is eaten as opposed to just the starchy part.

dG4IE2rgw2A.author: @DJ-sv7xf
dG4IE2rgw2A.text:
Freeze Response/Depressed
1.  Get upright/more vertical. 
2.  Get morning light in the first hour (outdoors preferably).
3.  Get heat (gradual)/cold (faster).
4.  Vagus Nerve:  Hum or long sigh.  Massage your face.  
5.   Structure your morning: Exercise/Walk dog/Open blinds/Upbeat music.
6.  Positivity:  Gratitude, list 3 things.   Help someone. 
7.  Too busy:  Balance with a hike or a nap.  No tv/phone.
8.   Orange zest/peppermint.

This morning I took in 10 minutes of cold fresh morning air and light, made myself a cup of hot lemon water, forced myself to go outside for lunch and actually not only buy food but eat it (my fridge has been bare and I have been avoiding stores, people, the outside, life in general). This evening I’m going to structure my time after work. I’m already feeling better, lighter, more optimistic. Thank you. This stuff really works. ❤

Sometimes it helps me to activate, or at least to make a little bit fun of me.

Thanks so much for watching. I know diet and hidradenitis suppurativa is a huge topic online and there is a lot of confusing advice out there. I’d love to hear from you. Have you noticed any connection between certain foods and your HS flare-ups? Did cutting back on dairy, sugar or trying a low FODMAP approach make any difference for you?

Share your experience in the comments. Your insight can really help others who are navigating the same condition. And if you have questions you want me to cover in future HS videos, drop those below too.

Don’t forget to check out the HS lifestyle and skincare video linked in the description. And as always, wear sunscreen and subscribe. 💙

Sw8ntW7LkPE.author: @amphoteric
Sw8ntW7LkPE.text: personally i don't have any trigger foods for my HS. the only thing that has helped me is hibiclens + an unscented anti-bacterial body wash (i use the dove brand). fragrance/scented body products will always cause big flare ups so i just avoid those at all costs. i've been using the hibiclens + anti-bacterial body wash combo for 2+ years now and have had minimal issues since

Sw8ntW7LkPE.author: @roxannecote1028
Sw8ntW7LkPE.text: Hello, I have HS and other inflammatory diseases (rosacea, lichen sclerosis, irritable bowel syndrome) and I clearly see a link with the amount of carbohydrates I eat. I am also diabetic and when I eat too many carbohydrates and my blood sugar rises, the inflammation also increases and I have more symptoms for all these diseases at the same time.

Sw8ntW7LkPE.author: @DouglasBagnall-o3s
Sw8ntW7LkPE.text: My fiancee had HS before much was known about the disease.  Her diagnosis came after surgery to remove the flesh killed through it.  In the early 2000s HS was usually discovered by surgeons who were brought in when infections became serious.  Dermatologists were not consulted.  At that time it was believed that people with very pale skin were most susceptible so I was surprised when it became clear that people of African descent were also suffering from this terrible disease.  I often wonder if current treatments would have helped her.  She did not die from HS, but it certainly contributed to her frailty.  Thank you so much for this video and so many of your other videos as well.

85gnTCbA_kI.author: @johanneshalberstadt
85gnTCbA_kI.text: 06:45 Also: too little choline in the brain, may not have to do with how much you eat. You can eat enough, or more than enough, choline and still have less available in the brain, because after eating -like all nutrients- it has to pass the intestinal wall first, which can already be impaired or facilitated in different people. Then it goes from your intestines into your blood, where special vessels take it to your liver first, then there is processing in the liver, before it even goes into your general blood stream, and once it is there, it still needs to pass the blood-brain-barrier and needs to end up exactly where it is needed in the brain. That means that there are multiple steps and mechanisms that don't have to do with your take-in, that could determine how much is available in your brain. And also the transformation of choline into acetyl choline may be much more determined by the availability and activity of specialized anzymes that tramsfom it, rather than just enough choline being there. As an analogy: of you have to few builders to build your house (the enzymes), giving them more bricks (choline) does not get your house built faster. You need more workers. This is, in my opinion, a fundamental mosunderstanding that people have regarding mamy things to do with nutrition. Often times it is not the supply of raw materials (nutrients) that determines a desired outcome, but your internal machinery. Now people temd to feel like giving your body more of a certain raw material would somehow "tell your body" to make more of the endproduct. But thats just not how it works. Your enzymes amd cells arent sentient conscious beings capable of symbolic communication. I believe people tend to think that if they give more of a certain starting material, your body would then "understand" that that "means" yoou want it to make more of a certain endproduct. But giving more of the starting material is not what triggers higher productivity. It just means there is enough there if and when mechanisma are kicked into action. Bit what kicks them into action is dependet on your bodys internal regulatory control loops, not on presence or absence of starting materials.

s5b4vaakSGc.author: @ZeroFallout1
s5b4vaakSGc.text: My conclusion after becoming diagnosed with a rare digestive condition is intolerance. For some reason we become intolerant to some foods. I had gerd, acid reflux for years. After changing my diet im free of acid blockers. My diet was very strict and very regiment. I was able to tell how long food should stay in my stomach. Trying out different foods and seasoning ive found out my body doesnt like some things. They sit in my stomach longer than normal. This is what i believe is the cause of most acid reflux. Medication is just a bandaid but the damage is still ongoing. Over time the stomach gets eroded by food sitting for to long. Ive noticed if my stomach is painfull under diaphragm my digestion slows down to. My guess is years of damage making vagus nerve more sensitive.

w3qc6GYnAU0.author: @WoundstoWisdomBible
w3qc6GYnAU0.text:
Here's a strange thing that came up in my CPTSD healing journey...
throughout my life, I've ALWAYS gone away by myself somewhere if Im really upset and know im going to cry. I could never show people i was upset but i thought it was normal behaviour until i was working on my healing and my mom told me that from infanthood i was allergic to the formula milk and would get terrible collic and be crying in pain. But she was told by the midwives back then, that i was just looking for attention when i was crying in pain, and to park me in the buggie at the end of the garden and let me cry it out alone. Now i realised why even as an adult, i would go cry alone because i learnt that no one cared enough to help when you are in pain and you just cry alone until you cant cry anymore. And when you are quiet you get to come back to everyone else. How sad to be trained that way and how something that repeatedly happened when you were too young to even remember it, gets hardwired into your nervous system and can effect you for the rest of your life, if you dont do the healing work. That was just one of MANY things that came up. Ive come a long way. Its not easy to do this because so many things come up and have to be worked through. Sometimes it felt like a never-ending rabbit hole. But now im feeling so much calmer, more aware, emotionally attuned, able to set boundaries, seeing other peoples behaviour as just that- their behaviour, not a reflection of my worth.

yp_gfAE3XSs.author: @Marko964
yp_gfAE3XSs.text:
Id go with

1. Omega 3  1-3g of Combined EPA and DHA per day. (i take 1.1g and might go up to 1.5 or 2).

Benefits: Systemic anti-inflammation, Brain/heart health, Supports skin barrier, reduces skin stress. 

2. Astaxanthin 12-18mg per day (I take 18mg)

Benefits: MAX Anti-inflammation, Cellular protection, Muscle recovery, Anti-aging protection, Eye Health, Increase in V02 max (Mixed Results), Reduced redness and a slight "Asta Glow" not a red orange tint. 
A study has shown it increases the life span of mice by 12%. Not done on humans yet but promising result.

3. Vitamin C 100-250 mg daily (i take 1000 every 4 days. Not as optimal but still good since it has a really long half life).

Benefits: Immune function, Powerful antioxidant, Essential for Collagen synthesis (skin firmness, joint health).

4. Magnesium L-Threonate or Glycinate 300-400 per day. (Usually it says not more then 350 but its meant for lesser versions like Magnesium Oxide. If u still wanna go safe then go with 200mg).

Magnesium L-Threonate
Brain/Cognitive Function

Magnesium Glycinate
Anxiety Relief, Muscle Relaxation, Sleep Aid

5. Vitamin D with K2 2000-10000 UI (i take 5000 UI) 100-300mcg (I take 200mcg).

Benefits: Strong Immunity, Hormonal support, Calcium absorption, Indirectly supports healthy cell turnover and mood. K2 regulating and directing calcium to bones and teeth while keeping it out of arteries, supporting strong bones, cardiovascular health, and preventing arterial calcification

6. Glycine 5-10g per day(Depending on ur goals even higher up to 90g.(I use 10g)

Benefits: MAX Deep Sleep Quality, Aids detoxification, Supports muscle repair (HGH release), Primary building block for Collagen, Supports skin hydration. 

Collagen made up of 33% of glycine. Some say less and others more. 

7. Zink (Optional) 5-15mg per day

Benefits: Immune function, DNA and cellular repair, Critical for skin healing, acne control, and hair/nail health.

8. Collagen (Optional) Slightly superior then Glycine for Looks but lacks the Big Boost on Sleep. And good sleep is the most important aspect of Health. Also called Beauty Sleep.

U will have to get the benefits of it urself since im not using it😅

9. Ashwaganda (Optional) Do ur own research on the best dose for u. I do 250mg of KSM-66. Above 300 for high stress and below for moderate. If u have low levels don’t bother buying it. 

Benefits: Manages Cortisol, Calms nervous system, aids sleep onset, Reduces stress-related inflammation and breakouts.

And if i missed some benefits feel free to add them.

IlAnLWoaQNw.author: @Alderic.
IlAnLWoaQNw.text:
EXTENDED REVIEW 

Nocturne Overnight Oats:
I have finished all of them. The ‘Banana Walnut’ and ‘Blueberry’ flavors are much better than the 2 I tried in this video, unluckily enough. I want to bump it up to ‘Can Stay’ but being almost $5/ portion is a bit too steep for my SES at the moment. Will look out for sale. 

Freeze-dried Fruits & Broccoli:
Almost finished. Some of the blueberry and broccoli ended up very disintegrated perhaps because of the delivery, it became rather messy/ troublesome to eat. The peach and dragonfruit held their shape better but I prefer the flavor of the former. 

Protein Hot Cocoa:
Only had it one other time. Really kinda mid. The macros are nice though. 

Treadmill:
Working well. Previously, I was walking with socks which was how I realized the treadmill was heating up a little bit. I have since started wearing shoes. 

Chiikawa:
Still cute.

Eg6HXMaBstQ.text: Retired nurse, we used to take ventilator patients from the intensive care unit out into the fresh air with machines that could run  with the oxygen tank pressure. It took about three of us to do so. Then we were told as nurses, respiratory therapists and physio therapists we couldn't do it anymore. Plus the vent we used was replaced by a machine that couldn't go outside away from electrical outlets. Way early in my career we put babies who were jaundiced into the sunlight, the hospital I worked in used to have a windowed solarium to put people into the sunlight then that part was torn down, tuberculosis patients were put into these solariums

QSCgLMJG2zY.author: @hotsauce7124
QSCgLMJG2zY.text:
1.  Blood pressure is regulated between 5 systems in the body: electrolytes, Hormones, nervous system, muscles, blood.
2.  Electrolytes include: sodium, potassium and magnesium.  If you struggle with proper potassium and magnesium levels then you will have a sodium imbalance; leading to high blood pressure.
3. Salt raises blood pressure, salt actually changes the way your cells respond to insulin and salt distorts your thirst.
4. Don't eat fast foods, bread, restaurant food; they are high in sodium.
5. Reduce saturated fat intake: Animal fat, dairy, coconut oil, vegetable oil, seed oil.
6. A DASH diet can help lower blood pressure.
7. A Whole food plant based diet can help lower blood pressure.
8. A Mediterranean diet can help lower blood pressure.
9. A Nordic diet can help lower blood pressure.
10. A low sodium/high potassium diet can help lower blood pressure.
11. In the short term, all these diets can lower your mercury levels: 7mm per Hg 
12. Be wary of diets that promote water loss without correcting potassium and magnesium like a low carbohydrate,diets that promote calorie restriction and ketogenic diets.
13. Best way to lower your blood pressure is to move your big muscle groups for 10-15 minutes per day: walking, cycling, and squatting.  Check with a medical doctor before starting any exercise or diet.
14.  Get yourself insulin sensitive.
15. Check your blood pressure.  If your blood pressure is naturally low then check with a doctor before adopting a diet.  You do not want your blood pressure getting too low or bottoming out.
16. Eat foods rich in  potassium and magnesium: leafy greens and beans.

jvWh0PJntes.author: @NathanMorey-z2f
jvWh0PJntes.text:
Love the great insight Coach, I went pro in Hard Enduro 2 years ago in Aus, and found even being one of the fittest on the grid that it didn't translate as well as the guys that got more seat time each week, where they saved more energy, through energy saving in techniques (alot harder to get the same seat time as them due to work and location).

I then also upped my stength training and HIIT and maintained a really high VO2 in the off season, I have taken alot of my training and fueling techniques from AFL players and triathletes, a combination of getting my fueling right and not breaking into my zone 4-5 HR to early in the race, has allowed me to go from burning out at about the 3-4 hour mark to now the 12-16 hours of riding before musclar fatigue. I also worked alot on balance and plyometric exercises and found this translated directly to the bike and allowed for much more overall bike control and minimised injuries.

I would be very interested to hear what you would suggest for racers doing events such as Romaniacs/Dakar or the likes where there are 4 days in a row of hard racing, or even in Hard Enduro having a 3 hour qualifying followed by the race the day after, I tend to notice alot of racers burn out on the second day much more.

jvWh0PJntes.author: @paddydunne774
jvWh0PJntes.text: Love your work Coach. Always fascinating to read the replies in the comments. They are always well thought through and, on a human level, thoughtful. It’s much appreciated. I’m an older rider who still to this day, think Jeff Spence’s Total Training for Motocross, is one of the most groundbreaking training manuals. The last three and years I have returned to the gym, mainly for my overall health and fitness. But I find lifting heavy on a hard training day, improves my riding as long as I have managed my sleep and nutrition sensibly. I still do a hard manual job on the farm, also. Feel blessed to still have the opportunity to be able to choose that lifestyle. I love the training partner I have become. But man do I miss that 19 year old self, slinging a leg over my 81 490 Maico. Not a care in the world 😉🤘👊

jvWh0PJntes.author: @CraigHelgerson
jvWh0PJntes.text: You have brilliant insights Rob. I was going to apply for my pro license in 1980 when a low speed tip over in practice blew my acl/mcl. An experiment reconstruction failed, so I left my love at the alter and went off to college. I ignored the sport for 40 some years, and when I came  back, I saw suspension had so advanced that the riders are not experiencing the brutal pounding that we experienced in the late 70s. We always flat landed everything and you would land so brutally hard your eyes would temporarily lose focus. A few years ago I saw a European fighter punching metal with little on his fists, I was convinced he had been hit in the head too many times until I learned that hard impacts to our bones strengthen them. I am not suggesting these kids do that but I would suggest far more seat time and build there whole bodies and hearts to race their motos without severe fatigue. Also, I would have rolled my eyes a few years ago if I heard what I'm about to say but if these have been put on any antibiotics in their lives, I learned that they have leaky gut where their food is not rebuilding their muscles as God designed them. I took high priced pre and probiotics and it helped a little albut then I learned that of all things kim chi has over 900 different bacteria. Within two weeks I found everything changed in the strength I would get from my workouts.

d_e2lYnoMos.author: @lowbuckgarageMOTOplusCROSS
d_e2lYnoMos.text:
Great video!
I was having a consistent problem with the sensation of dirt in my throat after any moto. It was like I was gagging coughing and couldn’t clear my throat, a burning feeling, couldn’t even speak for several minutes. I used to believe it was only from having my mouth open to breathe, dust inhalation, so I concentrated on 100% nose breathing. That was helpful, but the problem still persisted. My doctor suggested it was induced gastrointestinal gerd from woops and jumping. Stomach contents were going up and burning my throat! 
So as long as I don’t eat anything or overdo the sports drinks 2 hrs or so before racing I’m fine. Plenty of plain water is fine. Problem solved in my case.

apTCGA-y2us.text: I'm Lebanese, we are Mediterraneans. We have a small breakfast, big lunch, small dinner, no snacks. Parsley is a staple as tabouli. Dairy is always fermented as cheese or yoghurt. Lots of garlic. Bread is a side not a main event. Our dishes are meat and veg based, not flour based, unless a special occasion. We have an Arabic saying: after breakfast work, after lunch rest, after dinner walk.

apTCGA-y2us.text: 100% Correct! My godfather was born and raised in Athens and would be horrified to learn that his beloved lamb, fish (LOTS of fish) eggs beef and chicken were actually (according to US scientists) lentils and chickpeas! The longest lived population on earth are the citizens of Hong Kong. They also enjoy the highest per-capita meat consumption of any population on earth.  That's not coincidental.

apTCGA-y2us.author: @roblloyd1879
apTCGA-y2us.text: Had severe arthritis in my right hand for many years to the point were my doctor was considering steroid injections. I have also had back pain from a road accident when I was 16. Had followed the government and NHS guidelines on diet for many years, cut out animal fats, used vegetable and seed oils, margarine and limited eggs and dairy. Red meats were very rare in my diet. After my wife passed away 5 years ago I decided to change my diet completely. Eliminated veg and seed oils, started eating lots of eggs, cheese, butter and dairy. Red meat and fish often, particularly pork as it is a major source of essential nutrients. Cook mainly in animal fats. Cut down on carbs but still plenty of fruit and vegetables. Use olive oil and occasionally 'Cold Pressed' rapeseed oil. I cook from fresh or frozen with ready meals a rarity. Mainly a pizza once every couple of months as a treat. Within a year my arthritis had gone, I lost over 2 stone and I have just realised my back pain has gone. Not bad for an old codger aged 78. I can still chase the youngest grandchildren round and play garden badminton with them. My conclusion is 90% of good health is diet!!!! Always had backache from the age of 16 when I decided to argue with a van, the van won and I had a broken leg, a back injury and some short term memory brain damage. Over time the brain learns to ignore the pain although it is always present. Suddenly realised my back pain has gone. Says it all really. A real food diet is king!

apTCGA-y2us.author: @mariecurie7491
apTCGA-y2us.text: My boyfriend lives with his mother in Lombardy. She has a cornetto with jam for breakfast. For an early lunch, they start with pasta or risotto. Have a main course of meat - osso buco, pork chop, spezzatino; or liver or a burger - or polenta with chicken livers. With tomatoes or peperonata or courgettes. Followed by a piece of fruit - orange, mandarin, peach, plum, strawberries. Followed by a sweet. Then coffee. A 4-course lunch every single day. SHE IS 93 and going strong: still cooking, washing up, doing laundry, ironing. Up and down the stairs in a 3-floor house

Always choose data over hype for your heart.

Damaged mitochondria revert to anaerobic ATP production through fermentation, which causes uncontrolled cell division, which is cancer. Free radicals also damage endothelial cells, causing clotting with cardio and peripheral vascular disease. Elevated lipoproteins are the result of our bodies inability to metabolize carbohydrates, which are then stored as subcutaneous and visceral fat. Anything that damages cells will cause disease, but dietary seed oils are among the most prevalent and toxic.

xFPHrG8Iqug.author: @PhoenixWoody
xFPHrG8Iqug.text:
Here is my quick Grok confirmation about Vitamin D: 
What part of the body triggers Vitamin D production?

The skin is the part of the body that triggers and carries out vitamin D production. When ultraviolet B (UVB) rays from sunlight (wavelength 290–320 nm) hit the skin, they convert 7-dehydrocholesterol (a cholesterol derivative naturally present in the epidermis) into previtamin D₃. This previtamin D₃ then spontaneously isomerizes into vitamin D₃ (cholecalciferol) through a temperature-dependent process in the skin itself.

Key points for accuracy:
Production happens primarily in the epidermis, especially the stratum spinosum and stratum basale layers where 7-dehydrocholesterol is most abundant. No other organ or tissue in the body can synthesize vitamin D₃ from sunlight; the liver and kidneys later convert it (25-hydroxylation in the liver → 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D in the kidneys), but the initial triggering and synthesis occur exclusively in the skin. Oral vitamin D (D₂ or D₃ from supplements/food) bypasses the skin step entirely.  So, in short: the skin is both the trigger and the site of vitamin D production in response to UVB exposure.1.1sFast

Does the retina have anything to do with Vitamin D production?
No, the retina has no role in vitamin D production.
 
Here’s the accurate breakdown:
Vitamin D₃ synthesis requires UVB photons (290–320 nm) to photolyze 7-dehydrocholesterol into previtamin D₃.
The retina is protected from UVB by multiple barriers:
The cornea absorbs almost all UVB below ~300 nm.
The lens (especially after age 25–30) filters out remaining UVB and most UVA.
By the time light reaches the retina, virtually zero UVB remains.

Experimental evidence confirms this:

Studies on animals and humans show that even intense ocular-only UV exposure (with the rest of the body shielded) does not raise serum 25(OH)D levels.
People who are completely blind or have had both eyes enucleated still produce normal amounts of vitamin D from skin exposure.
Conversely, covering all skin but exposing only the eyes to sunlight produces zero vitamin D.

There is one minor related finding (often misunderstood online):
The retina and suprachiasmatic nucleus (via the retinohypothalamic tract) detect visible blue light for circadian entrainment, which indirectly influences vitamin D metabolism by regulating time spent outdoors. But this is a behavioral/circadian effect, not direct vitamin D synthesis in the eye.

Bottom line: Vitamin D production is exclusively a skin function. The retina and eyes contribute nothing to it directly.

xFPHrG8Iqug.author: @hmsd4450
xFPHrG8Iqug.text:
Sorry - Kupfer ist ein relevantes Thema, aber Vit D ist sein Wissen mangelhaft - 9 Jahre Forschung und Anwendung widersprechen dem klar.

Die Aussage, dass synthetisches Vitamin D die Aufnahme von Vitamin A blockiert, ist nicht korrekt und basiert auf einem weit verbreiteten Missverständnis in manchen alternativen Gesundheitskreisen. Schauen wir uns die tatsächliche Wechselwirkung wissenschaftlich fundiert an:
Was wirklich passiert (evidenzbasierte Zusammenfassung):
	1	Vitamin D und Vitamin A sind Gegenspieler (Antagonisten) auf Rezeptorebene Beide Vitamine wirken über nukleäre Rezeptoren (VDR = Vitamin-D-Rezeptor, RAR/RXR = Retinsäure-/Retinoid-X-Rezeptoren). Hohe Dosen von einem können die Wirkung des anderen abschwächen – das ist seit Jahrzehnten bekannt (z. B. Studienergebnisse von Chris Masterjohn, Weston A. Price Foundation und ältere pharmakologische Studien). → Das ist aber kein Block der Aufnahme im Darm, sondern eine Konkurrenz um die gleichen Transportsysteme und Rezeptoren in den Zellen.
	2	Die Aufnahme im Darm wird nicht blockiert
	◦	Vitamin D (auch synthetisches Cholecalciferol oder Calcitriol) und Vitamin A (Retinol, Retinylpalmitat) werden über unterschiedliche Mechanismen aufgenommen.
	◦	Vitamin A wird größtenteils als Retinylester über Mizellen und CRBP II transportiert.
	◦	Vitamin D wird ebenfalls über Mizellen, aber dann über NPC1L1 und andere Transporter. → Es gibt keine relevante Konkurrenz auf Absorptionsebene im Darm bei normalen oder auch hohen physiologischen Dosen.
	3	Wo das eigentliche Problem liegt Bei sehr hohen Dosen (z. B. > 50.000–100.000 IE Vitamin D pro Tag über längere Zeit) ohne ausreichend Vitamin A und K2 kann es zu einer relativen Vitamin-A- und K2-Verarmung kommen – nicht durch Blockade der Aufnahme, sondern durch:
	◦	Stark erhöhten Bedarf an Vitamin A und K2 für die durch Vitamin D induzierten Proteine (z. B. Osteocalcin, MGP)
	◦	Verdrängung von Retinsäure an RXR-Rezeptoren durch aktivierte Vitamin-D-Formen
	4	Das kann zu Symptomen führen, die fälschlicherweise als „Vitamin-D-Vergiftung“ interpretiert werden, aber tatsächlich oft ein relativer Vitamin-A- und K2-Mangel sind.
Praktische Konsequenzen (2025-Stand der Forschung):
Situation
Risiko einer negativen Wechselwirkung?
Empfehlung
Normale Dosierung (2.000–10.000 IE D3) + normale Ernährung mit Vitamin A
Nein
Unbedenklich
Hohe D3-Dosen (> 20.000–50.000 IE/Tag) ohne extra A und K2
Ja (relativer A-/K2-Mangel möglich)
Vitamin A (ca. 5.000–10.000 IE/Tag) + K2 (MK-4 oder MK-7) ergänzen
Lebertran (natürliche Kombination A + D)
Sehr gering
Ideale natürliche Quelle
Synthetisches Vitamin D vs. natürliches
Kein Unterschied bez. A-Aufnahme
Synthetisches D3 ist genauso wirksam und sicher wie natürliches
Fazit:
Synthetisches Vitamin D blockiert NICHT die Aufnahme von Vitamin A im Darm. Die oft kolportierte „Blockade“ ist ein Mythos. Es gibt jedoch eine funktionelle Antagonismus bei sehr hohen Dosen, weshalb bei hochdosierter Vitamin-D-Therapie immer auf ausreichende Zufuhr von Vitamin A und besonders Vitamin K2 geachtet werden sollte.
Quellen (Auswahl):
	•	Cannell JJ et al. (Vitamin D Council)
	•	Masterjohn C. (2007–2024): From Seafood to Sunshine – umfangreiche Analysen
	•	Aburto et al. (2019): Vitamin A and D interactions
	•	Klinische Studien zu Hypervitaminose D und relativen Mangelzuständen
Falls du sehr hoch dosierst (> 20.000 IE D3/Tag), lass lieber Blutwerte (25-OH-D, Retinol, K2-Status wenn möglich) kontrollieren.

xFPHrG8Iqug.author: @Avagardner2608
xFPHrG8Iqug.text:
Why Vitamin D Is Your Friend
	1.	Bone Health
	•	Helps your body absorb calcium and phosphorus.
	•	Prevents bone loss, fractures, and conditions like osteoporosis.
	2.	Immune Support
	•	Regulates immune system function.
	•	Can reduce risk of certain infections and inflammation.
	3.	Mood & Brain
	•	Supports mental health; low levels are linked to depression and fatigue.
	•	May influence cognitive function as you age.
	4.	Muscle & Performance
	•	Supports muscle strength and coordination — handy if you’re active or playing golf.
	5.	Other Potential Benefits
	•	Heart health: some studies suggest benefits for blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

MoejkSJ0yGs.author: @aejiongco
MoejkSJ0yGs.text: Dr. Zoë Harcombe is correct that early diet–heart guidelines relied on weak observational data and that sugar and refined carbohydrates contribute to metabolic risk. However, modern evidence—from genetics, pathology, and clinical trials—shows that ApoB/LDL particles play a causal role in atherosclerosis, and saturated fat raises LDL in most people. Cardiovascular disease isn’t explained by a single nutrient; it reflects cumulative LDL exposure alongside insulin resistance, inflammation, blood pressure, and lifestyle factors.

MoejkSJ0yGs.author: @greensmoothieparty
MoejkSJ0yGs.text:
Note that Ancel Keys' books do not actually say what many people claim that they do. 
From Eat Well & Stay Well (1959): Cholesterol amounts to 5 or more per cent of solids in the brain and nervous tissue where it is thought it may act as an electrical insulator and where, in any case, it is effectively isolated from the cholesterol in the blood and in the arteries. Cholesterol is also prominent in the adrenal glands, where it may be a supply of material for the manufacture of adrenal hormones, and it is a main ingredient of gallstones. All in all, cholesterol is an important and remarkable substance quite apart from its unfortunate tendency to be deposited in the walls of arteries, thereby producing atherosclerosis. and later in this book... Of course, an easy solution to the problem of fat in the diet would be vegetarianism, but this would be both impractical and unnecessary in our opinion.

9fCFu21r42s.author: @SreenithiSudhakar
9fCFu21r42s.text: Good and informative video… however… there was a lot of repetition and there is conflicting info… I do intermittent fasting (16hrs fasting 8hr eating window) and I skip breakfast and finish dinner by 7/7.30pm at the latest… skipping breakfast is more sustainable (in the long term) than skipping dinner… i’ve also incorporated exercise (running/walking and gym) and have a lost a lot of weight (at a steady pace)… and have felt more healthy, have a lot more concentration, have better sleep etc… weight loss, insulin resistance etc varies between person to person… and to see results with weight loss… you need to be in a calorie deficit - i.e. eat less but also have a healthy and balanced diet during your eating window… by doing this and incorporating my workouts… I have managed to lose weight and have a healthier life

HV1z9N9Um-M.text:
Just to be very clear, this video is not saying exercise is bad or that you should stop working out. In fact, regular exercise is one of the strongest protectors of heart health.
This video exists to help people catch problems early, not to scare anyone away from a healthy lifestyle.

The goal here isn’t fear,  it’s awareness.
Train your heart. Move your body. Stay active.
But don’t rely on vibes alone. If you have risk factors or a family history, get checked and know where you stand.

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @leenakorkmaz
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: Really interesting take. Prior authorization feels less like cost control and more like a delay tactic, and the point about inflated prices and insurer consolidation makes a lot of sense. Definitely makes you rethink where the real problem is.

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @PhilGajari
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: This episode really hit home with the discussion on prior authorization and the blurred lines of ownership in healthcare! It's wild to think about how much isn't transparent to patients. Great points about residents doing surgeries too, definitely something to be aware of!

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @SerenOzturk-g5x
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: From a patient’s perspective, prior authorization is basically invisible until the moment it suddenly becomes a roadblock. Most people don’t even know what it is until they’re told they can’t get a medication, test, or procedure they urgently need. And by the time they learn the term, they’re already stuck dealing with delays, paperwork, and confusing back-and-forth calls. That kind of disconnect makes the whole system feel untrustworthy and exhausting. It’s no surprise it creates so much frustration, because patients are left feeling like they’re being punished for rules they were never even told existed.

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @kubraaydilek5667
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: Important conversation about transparency and systemic barriers in healthcare. Prior authorization and administrative processes often affect patients in ways they don’t fully see until it becomes a challenge. Raising awareness about these structural issues is essential for meaningful reform.

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @canmirza5
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: The idea that reforms are happening at the state level gives some hope. Policy adjustments around response times and reporting standards could create guardrails without eliminating oversight entirely.

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @kubraaydilek5667
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: The point about data transparency was important. Without clear reporting on denial rates and turnaround times, it’s hard to hold anyone accountable. Metrics matter here. 📊

ZE-JHr0xla4.author: @mertcerit1907
ZE-JHr0xla4.text: Prior authorization and insurer-owned systems don’t lower cost they just shift the burden to patients and doctors. Big hospitals control pricing, transparency is low, and lower-cost care often gets ignored. Eye-opening look at why healthcare stays so expensive and complicated!

38DZaNxYLD4.author: @HealthMetabolicScience
38DZaNxYLD4.text:
If you’ve ever fasted and found yourself wide awake at 2–3 a.m., read this before panicking.

You’re not losing sleep because you’re hungry.
👉 You’re awake because your brain has switched into survival mode.

Without food, the brain increases orexin, cortisol, and adrenaline to keep you alert — just like our ancestors who stayed awake to hunt, not to sleep.
So feeling wired while fasting isn’t a mistake — it’s ancient biology doing its job.

The real question isn’t “Why can’t I sleep?”
👉 It’s “How do I work with my biology instead of fighting it?”

👉 When you fast, do you usually sleep less but feel sharper, or feel restless and anxious at night?
Share your experience below — you’re not alone.

6dgc6gnMcpI.author: @theranchhand
6dgc6gnMcpI.text:
Great topic, so appreciate it.  But…. Until this question gets changed this will never be answered appropriately.  It’s not about if you are “satisfied” as one can be satisfied with an array of things they wish were better, bigger, etc.  the question needs to shift to “what is the ideal size/shape of your partner if you could have your way?” That’s a VERY different response.  I am satisfied with many things in my partner but I would still love for them to change.  We leave the room massively open for what men really want answered which is “am I big enough to meet your ultimate desire for size?”   Considering technique and love and connection are all even across the board, we are all great lovers, then what would be the fantasized size to accompany that?  Period.  As long as we keep saying “are you satisfied” or “are you happy with your partners size” we will go around in circles for ever.  Does that mean we would get honest answers, I don’t know but it is the reason the question is asked.  We also understand that the question is landing mostly on men and not for women (are you tight enough, etc) so the burden of “perfection” historically lands on the man’s shoulders (or groin).  This in itself creates even more pressure on it.  

Breast size is often used as an analogy for women but does not work as one could have sex a hundred times with their breasts completely covered and you are not having sex once with the penis covered (condoms not withstanding).  But you get the point.  It’s a unique question for men on many levels but we are afraid to form the question correctly it seems. 

Anyway, I think this is an important topic and nice to hear you speaking about it but until the polls reflect the question better, these podcasts will continue and continue and….

cgQVJ66Kudo.author: @EbruAtikk
cgQVJ66Kudo.text: hearing you explain all the misconceptions around bariatric surgery really stood out to me, because so many people genuinely think it’s some quick fix when in reality it’s a whole journey. I liked the part about how minimally invasive techniques help people get home sooner, since honestly the hospital environment really does slow recovery for a lot of folks. and the point about obesity being connected to things like diabetes, sleep apnea, depression, even changes in puberty or menopause, shows how complex it really is. The way your team works together every day with dietitians, social workers, nurses and surgeons says a lot about why long term results matter more than just the surgery itself. I’m also curious about the future of the field, especially with medications and new techniques becoming a bigger part of the process. do you think the balance between surgery and meds will shift even more over the next few years?

zM9eqJjJTow.author: @MichelleBlackChannel
zM9eqJjJTow.text:
Women in perimenopause or menopause often don’t realize that GLP-1s work differently for them.
It’s not just appetite suppression — their carb tolerance, insulin response, and cortisol patterns all shift in midlife.
So even when Ozempic is doing its job, unbalanced meals can still trigger crashes, stalled fat loss, and belly fat that won’t budge.
Once women start consistently balancing protein + carbs + fat, the plateaus disappear so much faster.

JOFFXRz4TO0.author: @CidMaik
JOFFXRz4TO0.text:
I do 20 - 22 hours fast with 2 hours to eat whatever I can find.
Usually walk around and do some stationary bicicle ever so often.

In between I do 48 hours (every 2 weeks) and monthly\bi monthly 72 hours. 

On weekends, just eat normally, eat whatever you want, avoid stressing about it, thinking to much about it. If you go in while stressing so much about it, cortisol rises and that is one of your number 1 enemies. 

I've been doing this for 48 days, lost 22 pounds so far. 
The first days are the roughest ones but after, not even your hunger signal bothers you. You'll understand your body better, will know the distinction of true hunger from "hey I need glucose... Give it to me" alerts you get. Slow and steady for sure when attempting this one.

JOFFXRz4TO0.author: @Life_style67
JOFFXRz4TO0.text: It’s important to remember that every body responds differently to fasting. What works well for one person may not feel the same for someone else. In my personal experience, I’ve been practicing fasting for about seven years, and I’ve learned that mindset plays a huge role in the process. The mental shift is often more challenging than the physical aspect. The human body is actually designed to adapt; with proper hydration and electrolytes, it can function for extended periods without solid food. I once completed 28 days consuming only sugar-free squash, unsweetened almond milk, tea, and coffee. For me, the toughest part has always been the first three to four days, when hunger feels the strongest and habits are hardest to break. After that initial phase, the intense hunger usually fades, energy stabilizes, and the routine becomes much more manageable. However, fasting should always be approached carefully and with proper medical guidance.

The only other supplement or medication or nootropic that has the same level of effectiveness of Celebrex has been tart cherry juice at bedtime.

J39S5r4G-SM.author: @clairez123
J39S5r4G-SM.text: For midlife women, the menopause transition has significant impact on pretty much everything. These recommendations make sense for the direct and indirect effects of estrogen decline. Autumn, making connections between recommendations and the realities of menopause would be great to increase in terms of content. Despite half the world's population will transition through menopause - literally BILLIONS of women around the world in/post menopause and most of us will live 40% of our lives in this state - menopause is significantly understudied and health care providers receive little to no training. Many women are struggling with muscle loss and central weight gain, using old techniques that simply fail because their bodies have changed - dramatically in some cases - without much to guide them how to change. Please consider more content that directly address menopause and strategies to improve health and strength. Thank you!

J39S5r4G-SM.text: Forgive me if you mentioned this and I missed it. I do feel it is important to mention that people with chronic kidney disease require lower amounts of protein, especially animal protein, as our kidneys have to work harder to process. It would be a good idea to know what one's kidney function is ( simple blood tests) before embarking on a regimen with a large amount of protein. A simple formula can calculate recommended daily grams of protein based on level of chronic disease.

KtmdI0r8BgY.author: @YourOrganizedAuntie
KtmdI0r8BgY.text: Holy cow, the way you said “choose ONE of these four times to drink a smoothie” had me giggling because every time someone gives me options, my brain immediately panics like “huh… why does this feel like the SATs but with fruit.” But goodness, smoothies for weight loss really do hit different depending on timing. I learned this the hard way when I used to chug one right before bed and then wonder why I woke up feeling like a blender myself. One morning I finally told myself “Luise… girl… maybe stop drinking 400 calories of berries at 10 p.m. and see what happens,” and whew, the clarity that followed. My favorite time now is mid‑morning, right when I’m about to lose the will to answer another email. I’ll take one sip and suddenly I’m a functioning adult again instead of a woman negotiating with her to‑do list like it’s a hostage situation. And ha, don’t get me started on the pre‑workout smoothie era when I thought I was unstoppable until I realized I was basically doing squats with a stomach full of blended ice. Never again. The real magic is choosing the time that keeps you from raiding the pantry like a raccoon at 3 p.m. That’s the moment smoothies save lives. And honestly, once you figure out your timing sweet spot, you feel like you’ve cracked some ancient nutritional code. Victory.

NbyAqLndwCI.author: @lincolntrewethey
NbyAqLndwCI.text:
Vitamin D3: Caplets are better than tablets or capsules. Get your D3 levels tested. Aim for around 175 - 200 nmol/L Adjust your dosage as necessary to achieve optimal levels. Necessary for testosterone production, immunity, cytokine storm prevention - look it up, and calcium absorption. Take with some fat (fish oil is perfect), and take it with vitamin K2. K2 'steers' calcium into the bones, instead of arteries.
B6, B9 (methylfolate, NOT folic acid), B12 (methylcobalamin, NOT cyanocobalamin): take in the mid-morning/lunch. Ensure you take the methylated form.
Magnesium: Glycinate - relaxing, Taurate - heart health, L-Threonate - brain health
Don't take magnesium and calcium together. I don't even take calcium supplements. If you take Vitamin D3, you will absorb enough calcium from your diet.
Zinc: Take 50mg every two days to support testosterone production. *** Take 1mg of copper every 4 days, on days you do not take zinc, to prevent a zinc induced copper deficiency.
Watch the entire video - it has LOTS more great info.

zlQRslzQOPI.text:
As a medical professional, this video provides crucial insights for seniors on circulatory health. Here are key takeaways:
1. Raw spinach in smoothies can increase oxalates, impacting blood thickness.
2. Processed oats and sugary cereals spike blood sugar, affecting vessel health.
3. Daily processed meats and cheese contribute to inflammation and thicker blood.
Small dietary shifts can significantly protect your heart.

zlQRslzQOPI.author: @BeFitest-Official
zlQRslzQOPI.text:
As someone with a medical background, I found this video extremely valuable for seniors who want to protect their circulatory health. A few important points stood out to me:

1.Using raw spinach in smoothies may raise oxalate levels, which can subtly influence blood consistency.

2.Highly processed oats or sugary breakfast cereals can cause quick blood-sugar spikes that strain your vessels.

3.Regular intake of processed meats and cheese can promote inflammation, which may lead to thicker, less efficient blood flow.
Even small adjustments to your daily diet can make a big difference for your heart and overall circulation.

Thank you so much for watching 💛
Blood clots rarely come from one big mistake — they build slowly from daily habits, especially in the morning.

DImVMNnr1Gw.author: @Ana_Ines3420
DImVMNnr1Gw.text:
I'm 82, a hip replacement and a few years ago sprained both ankles at the same time when I stepped into a shallow hole in the curb. Add to that a sedentary life and I was a mess. 
I have been doing some of these exercises for some time and they are powerful, life changing. My heart improved with the heels lifting (2 months after starting). I even replicate the movement when in bed. 
The chair squats I started 4 months aho and they are incredible. I can get up like a youngster now and my quads are beautiful mow, softly rounded, like when I was 20. 
The wall pushups are powerful but, though I had done them when I was younger I had stopped. Now I'm going to start with them again and with the band resistance. Wish me luck.

qF_06odyJR4.author: @JestonyNatindim
qF_06odyJR4.text: I spent a long time trying to think my way out of brain fog before I realized the problem was blood flow, not willpower. In severe hypothyroidism, global cerebral blood flow can drop by nearly 40 percent. The brain operates on a strict energy budget, and when metabolism drops, complex cognition and memory are the first to be cut — the abandoned neurons theory. The solution is not a nootropic. It's restoring the metabolic supply line. That shift in understanding was what finally pointed me in the right direction.

qF_06odyJR4.author: @YourOrganizedAuntie
qF_06odyJR4.text: Oh goodness, the moment you said “brain fog, fatigue, inflammation, and the mineral deficiency no one talks about,” my whole spirit perked up like “Ah yes, the plot twist of adulthood,” because whew, I once went through a season where I was so tired I thought my soul had low battery. I’d walk into a room and forget why I was there, then argue with myself like “Luise, focus… no seriously, what are we doing here?” and my brain would just shrug. And here’s the wild part: it wasn’t burnout, it wasn’t stress, it wasn’t even my questionable sleep schedule. It was a mineral deficiency so sneaky it should honestly be on the FBI watchlist. When I finally got it checked, the doctor looked at me like “Ma’am, your cells are basically running on dial‑up internet,” and I nearly fainted. And oh wow, once I started replenishing it, the fog lifted so fast I thought I had unlocked a cheat code. Suddenly I could think clearly, communicate like a functioning adult, and even my productivity came back from the dead like “Hey girl, miss me?” It taught me something hilarious and humbling: sometimes you’re not unmotivated, you’re just biochemically confused. And the moment your body gets what it’s been begging for, everything shifts. Your energy stabilizes, your mood stops doing gymnastics, and your brain finally stops feeling like a browser with 47 tabs open. Honestly, if anyone’s been feeling “off” for no reason, it might not be vibes… it might be minerals quietly screaming for help.

KMVsg9EnbcE.author: @Bill-is9lu
KMVsg9EnbcE.text: I'm 72 and started berberine with keto going on 7 years ago. After my diabetes diagnosis I changed everything. I was 65 and I had neuropathy for several years. How I wasn't diagnosed with diabetes during this time or prior to this time is a question I'll always ask. But once I threw in the towel and stopped hoping that it was a mechanical issue and that different shoes would be enough I started the Burberine, in an effort, along with my diet, to lower my A1C as much as possible. I managed to go from over 9 to 5.5 in 5 months. Most of my neuropathy is gone though I wish I got to it sooner. I took berberine for probably the first 18 months pretty regularly. I've taken it sporadically in the meantime and am in fact in the middle of another meantime. Just an aside I am so much healthier than I was 12 years ago when I was 60. The mental health alone is a miracle. At 72 I'm experiencing pretty much the best health I've ever had and I found my way back to my Navy days weight. 😊

Wow! As a stage 3 cancer survivor I was told to take Anastrozole, a devastating drug for me. I stopped taking it and told my oncologist that even if not taking it shortens my life, at least I won’t be miserable.  Then I started taking berberine years ago because studies indicate that it may be a powerful anti-cancer agent. (5+ years cancer free).

My husband was placed on statins after a MI but stopped because of the side effects. I’m sharing your video with him.
I’m blown away that it also reduces insulin resistance…..studies hint that insulin resistance is a major cause of aging, dementia and other diseases….Berberine seems to be a wonder supplement!
