BD_K4WyIY2s.author: @johnpisano1048
BD_K4WyIY2s.text:
This is simply what helped my LPR (Laryngopharyngeal reflux)  calm down — not medical advice, not a rulebook, just sharing in case it gives someone hope.
During my healing phase, I kept things very simple.
Main foods (most meals):
• Beef — ribeye, sirloin, chuck roast, ground beef (this was my base)
• Steak seemed easiest on my throat and digestion
Also included:
• Chicken — thighs, drumsticks, wings (plain, simply cooked)
• Pork — chops and loin; I avoided processed meats early on
• Eggs (when tolerated)
• Fish occasionally — salmon, sardines, white fish
Drinks:
• Water only (room temp or warm)
• Electrolytes if needed — unflavored or homemade; no citrus, sweeteners, or sugar
• No coffee, tea, alcohol, or flavored drinks during healing
What I avoided at first:
• Plant foods (vegetables, fruit, grains, beans)
• Sugar and sweeteners
• Processed meats with spices/sugars
• Sauces or seasonings beyond salt — I used salt as my only seasoning
Why I kept it simple (my personal reasoning):
Less fermentation → less gas → less abdominal pressure.
Less pressure seemed to mean less reflux mist and less throat irritation for me.
I don’t see this as forever or one-size-fits-all. Everyone’s body is different. This was just a temporary reset that seemed to give my throat a calm window to heal.
If someone feels stuck, you could consider trying something like this for about 30 days as an experiment — not a forever diet, just a short healing window to see how your body responds.

_Q4XT82yd-Q.author: @amandamittleman84
_Q4XT82yd-Q.text:
As a personal trainer with 30+ years of experience and a Master's thesis focused on exercise intensity and body composition across a lifetime — including DEXA measurements on 100+ postmenopausal women ranging from elite athletes to sedentary controls — I have enormous respect for research. AND I'll say what the data alone can't:
Reading studies is one part of exercise prescription. Working with real women — in real bodies, real hormones, real lives — is another.
If data were enough, we wouldn't have 82% of Americans metabolically unhealthy with more information available than ever before.
Women are not small men. Some of us can train with high intensity and recover well. Many cannot — especially in perimenopause and menopause, when hormonal shifts fundamentally change how we respond to stress, recover from training, and lose or gain body composition.
I say this with love and respect for the science: you cannot fully understand the nuances of perimenopause until you live it. I couldn't. And I had the data, the degree, and 20+ years of experience when I hit it.
One size does not fit all. The best exercise prescription meets the woman in front of you — not just the study population.

_Q4XT82yd-Q.author: @MathStatsMe
_Q4XT82yd-Q.text: Even with daily yoga a few years ago, as I started into perimenopause, I started getting all kinds of tendonitis issues, which require a broad range of tactics to manage, including massage, PT, and strength training. I'm kicking proverbial butt now in comparison to before gaining more muscle through strength training than yoga alone could provide. I still love the yoga, but appreciate having a broader range of targeted strength training approaches. I have a disabled son who relies on me to be physically robust, so this is a big deal to me.

SHh95zHKQKA.author: @YourOrganizedAuntie
SHh95zHKQKA.text: Thanks, Jim! Whoa, the way this topic dragged me right back into my own chaos is unreal, because goodness, the day I realized my brain was being drained at the cellular level was the same day I caught myself standing in the kitchen arguing with a banana like “ma’am, why are you bruised already, we just got home,” and honestly… that was my sign that something deeper was happening, because ha! my mind felt like an old phone stuck at 3 percent even after being plugged in all night, and I remember thinking “Luise, sweetheart, what on earth is going on up there,” so I sat myself down, right there on the floor, cross‑legged like a confused toddler, and I had this whole internal TED Talk where my brain was like “girl, you keep multitasking like you’re auditioning for a Marvel movie, no wonder your neurons are filing HR complaints,” and oh my gosh, it hit me that half my exhaustion wasn’t from life itself but from the constant background noise of overthinking, doom‑scrolling, replaying conversations from 2014, and pretending I’m fine when my mitochondria are clearly waving tiny white flags, so I started doing this tiny ritual where I pause, breathe, and ask “okay, what’s actually draining me right now,” and sweet! the clarity that comes from that is wild, because once you name the thing, it stops chewing on your brain like a raccoon in a trash can, and honestly, the moment I stopped trying to be a superhero and just let myself be a human with limits, my energy came back in ways I didn’t expect, so if your brain feels like it’s leaking battery life, trust me, you’re not broken, you’re just overdue for a reboot, and wow, isn’t it funny how the smallest shifts end up saving the whole day?

As a person of Polynesian descent (Rarotonga), I really appreciate how you have correctly recognised Micronesian traditions of navigation and thier generosity that enabled the Polynesians to learn from the Micronesian methods, in the 80s - And actually 90% of Polynesian methods are lost. The  "re-learning" that is practised by modern polynesian ocean going navigators needs to properly recognise the 'Akapapa (heritage/ lineage) of the knowledge.
The loss of knowledge is also due to system failure of how the keepers of traditional knowledge would pass history only to "the right person". Traditional knowledge was not a birthright. Furthermore only the upper class held the knowledge, passing it on through chants might skip a generation, coupled by the overwhelming London Mission Society and similar agencies influence, traditions were lost and suppressed and considered to be witchcraft

b5F3ePIGMiA.author: @shizuokaBLUES
b5F3ePIGMiA.text:
Central coastal Japan and its time to turn off my light.  Mid February and we are now in the quiet time of the year before the blossoms open in full, and the nights are still cold, clear and dry.   It feels like our lives and the stability most of us have enjoyed and thrived on are on a tight rope now. But so it goes through our eons on this planet.  
I’m afraid it’s our ego and our greed that is to blame for this. 
But for tonight, and hopefully tomorrow it’s warm and peaceful here and I’ll walk 90 minutes each way to work along the canal and take in the sunshine and bird sounds. Life is good on earth…. It’s just fleeting. 
Goodnight all,  look up at the stars and be thankful for your consciousness and forget our troubles.

ddGADGLM7ps.author: @sheila7221
ddGADGLM7ps.text: No more instant oat packets - already destroyed oats with added sugar - I was given a few boxes by son and loving DIL they ARE easy and taste good, but I buy the Steel cut real oats.NOTES: " Don't cook steel cut oats too long low-med heat 5-10 min. Not Pastey!  Soak oats 1st the night before in a GLASS/Ceramic bowl-cover with twice as much water and add 1 tsp of something acidic ie Apple cider vinegar, lemon, leave on counter overnight at room temp for enzyme activity, In am, pour out that water, rinse and strain. Cook in water or milk as usual.  Oats and enzymes are now activated!!!!  You can add chia or flax seeds, yogurt, or even a poached or fried egg on top to add the fat we need or even full TBS of peanut butter or almond butter (with NO added sugar), almonds, walnuts or pecans, pumpkin or sunflower seeds.....or even avocado. DO NOT add ANY sugar, even Honey (" it's Like pressing the gas & brake pedal at the same time) He promises our taste buds will accommodate in a day to a week. Ceylon cinnamon is Great substitute, or berries or half a banana.

3P_66JKnoT0.text:
I can’t believe how popular this Sardine Diet thing is turning out to be. Here are more resources and answers to FAQ (Mercury, Sardine Brand, etc.):

The original Sardine Diet Letter: https://staycuriousmetabolism.substack.com/p/sardine-fasting-the-ultimate-health?r=40ekz2 

Prior Video: https://youtu.be/_Th54GWd8WY 

Omega-3 and Thermogenesis Letter: Coming tomorrow 12/11/25 to StayCuriousMetabolism.com
 
Q: Aren’t Sardines high in mercury?
No. Sardines are a low mercury fish. You’d need to eat 77 servings of sardines to get the mercury in one serving of swordfish.

Q: What brand did you use for the sardine diet experiment?
Fishwife, preserved in lemon and extra virgin olive oil.

Q: What about BPA?
The brand was selected in part because it has BPA-free tins.

1F_YzKJC5os.text:
### Summary: Boost Brain Autophagy for Mental Clarity

**Core Concept:** Brain autophagy is a distinct cleaning process that removes dysfunctional components from neurons, resulting in mental clarity, focus, and reduced brain fog.

---

### 1. What is Brain Autophagy?
- **The Process:** Cellular cleanup specifically within brain neurons, clearing out old, decaying parts that create "exhaust" (oxidative stress).
- **The Feeling:** Mental consolidation, laser focus, and reduced brain fog. It feels like "slow is smooth, smooth is fast."

### 2. How to Induce Brain Autophagy
- **Fasting:** Even short fasts (e.g., 16 hours) trigger significant neuronal autophagy.
- **Ketones:** Cleaner-burning fuel for the brain that reduces oxidative stress. Can be elevated by exercise or MCT oil, even without a strict keto diet.
- **Key Supplements:**
    - **Glutamine:** Crucial for the Glutamate (focus) / GABA (calm) cycle. Deficiency leads to a "wired but tired" brain. Essential for precision focus during a fast.
    - **Spermidine:** Directly promotes neuronal autophagy. Found in wheat germ, mushrooms, aged cheeses.
    - **Urolithin A:** Specifically targets **mitophagy**—cleaning mitochondria in the energy-demanding brain.
    - **Theanine:** Promotes calm and crosses the blood-brain barrier to locally inhibit mTOR.

### 3. Autophagy in the Body & Other Methods
- **Stacking Stressors:** Exercise induces **3x more autophagy** than fasting. Combine them (fasted exercise) for maximum effect.
- **Hormonal Help (Ghrelin):** The "hunger hormone" signals autophagy. Coffee or green tea with cinnamon can **mask hunger pangs** without lowering beneficial ghrelin levels.
- **Fasting-Mimicking Protocols:**
    - **Olive Oil Fast:** Consume 1-2 tbsp of high-quality olive oil every 4-6 hours. The antioxidants (oleopein) trigger autophagic flux while keeping insulin low.
    - **MCT Oil Fast:** Same protocol. Provides clean ketones for the brain and improves neural network stability.

### 4. Action Plan & Homework
- **The Goal:** Combine regular fasting with targeted supplements (Glutamine, Urolithin A, Spermidine) and lifestyle factors (exercise, cold, sauna).
- **Immediate Task:** Start with inexpensive **glutamine** and a short fast to experience the cognitive benefits.

nMkQUlBtFlk.author: @OperatorGnosisRecords
nMkQUlBtFlk.text:
Great convo. The alcohol part at around 2hrs.23 is very important, as I think Huby is a little biased against alcohol. This guy gives a great perspective on why and how mild social drinking might be a net positive IF you're system has the resources to allocate to the cost of detoxifying it (which, for instance, many young people, and fit, happy, secure older people, will have). Superb. My Grandad lived until he was 101, and he had one small sherry every single night. I suspect the cost of the booze on his system was outweighed by the relaxing ritual of it, as stress is potentially more costly to mitochondrial health than one small drink. He was utterly a man of moderation in every aspect of life, very ordered, happily married, didn't concern himself with the stresses of the world, was a high ranking police officer, and never over ate, or drank heavily.  So his one small sherry probably never outweighed all of that. 

I think you can look at various 'indulgences' in this light. Especially as so many of them are tied to our sense of self, and our sentimental connection to our past. Determining how much you want to allocate to technically unhealthy, but subjectively fun, and potentially stress reducing behaviours, is an excellent way to look at things. With that being said, though, most people who are metabolically unhealthy (which might be a majority these days, at least in the US and the UK) are likely somewhat in that state BECAUSE they lean too much on indulgences, and not enough on improving mitochondrial health through diet, exercise, sleep optimisation etc etc. In which case, less, or no, booze is a better option.

D1XoyxPoAKw.author: @atlantaunderground
D1XoyxPoAKw.text: Something I started years ago is bi-phasic sleeping. Basically, I may sleep and then wake up 3 or 4 hours later. Instead of trying to get myself back to sleep I used to go out with my dog for an hour or so. Or these days I may read for an hour. Then I go back to sleep for maybe 2 or 3 hours. I found that that second sleep is so much more satisfying. I wake up completely rested and feel good. I started that by accident and later found out that there's evidence that people used to sleep that way. Before they had electricity, they would go to sleep early and wake up in the middle of the night and talk with their spouse or had sex or whatever. And there are old books and supposedly even the bible , that reference the first and the second sleep.

_wmjFCYfof8.author: @peterholt4806
_wmjFCYfof8.text: Chris Ramsden, the researcher who unearthed the MCE data, also unearthed data from the Sydney Heart Study. The SHS was performed as a secondary prevention study whereas the MCE was for primary prevention. The SHS (performed at the same time as the MCE) showed the same results. Swapping out SatFat for Omega 6 PUFAs was detrimental. And, the SHS results were, similarly, buried in the same way. This has all the hallmarks of Keys' influence in this area. Hiding one study can be a conspiracy theory, but hiding 2 that disprove your beliefs is a pattern.

_wmjFCYfof8.author: @julieoelker1865
_wmjFCYfof8.text:
As I'm listening to you Nick, I'm making sardine salad for dinner: water-packed sardines drained (keep the bones though), dill relish, avocado mayonnaise, and hard-boiled egg. I think I like it better than tuna salad.

7UeojKTzeHE.author: @EyepowerRedREDLIGHTGLASSES
7UeojKTzeHE.text:
Great, thanks all. Summary.
Explains how UVB, red, and near‑infrared light act as biological signals that regulate hormones, immunity, circadian rhythms, and cellular energy. It highlights how different wavelengths interact with the skin, eyes, and brain to influence health across the lifespan.

🌞 UVB Light and Hormonal / Immune Activation
UVB light from sunlight triggers major physiological responses. When UVB hits the skin, it increases production of testosterone and estrogen, improves mood, and stimulates stem cell turnover that supports healthier skin, hair, and nails. When UVB reaches the eyes, it activates a neural pathway to the spleen, prompting the release of immune cells such as T‑cells and B‑cells, effectively placing the body in a heightened defensive state. UVB exposure also increases endogenous opioids like beta‑endorphins, raising pain tolerance and contributing to feelings of well‑being.

🌙 Light, Melatonin, and the Circadian Clock
Light regulates the body’s internal timing system through the pineal gland and melatonin. Melatonin acts as a seasonal “calendar,” with higher levels in winter influencing bone density and puberty timing. Exposure to bright light—especially UVB or blue light—between 10 p.m. and 4 a.m. sharply suppresses melatonin and activates a brain region that reduces dopamine, increasing vulnerability to depression. Maintaining darkness or dim red light at night protects circadian stability.

🔴 Red and Near‑Infrared Light for Cellular Repair
Long‑wavelength red (670 nm) and near‑infrared (790 nm) light penetrate deeply into tissues and directly affect mitochondria, increasing ATP production while reducing harmful reactive oxygen species. Research from Dr. Glen Jeffery shows that viewing red light for 2–3 minutes in the early morning can improve visual acuity by about 22% in adults over 40 by restoring energy to aging photoreceptors and reducing drusen. Red light also accelerates wound healing and supports acne treatment by activating dermal stem cells. Dr Jeffery consulted on the development of our glasses.

🧭 Practical Protocols
Recommended practices include 20–30 minutes of sunlight exposure several times weekly, using red light therapy only in the morning, and relying on dim red light at night—especially for shift workers. Individuals with conditions such as glaucoma, macular degeneration, or skin cancer risk should consult specialists before increasing UVB exposure.

-aX3-QXLzoA.author: @logicalparadox2897
-aX3-QXLzoA.text:
I think the biggest problem is that modern medicine isn’t trying to treat people, INDIVIDUAL PEOPLE. Rather, they are trying to treat the population as a whole. They view you and I as essentially the same people, with the same targets. They measure things, sometimes lots of things, but my doctor only looks to see if a given value is green or red (ie, they look for things “out of range”). They seem to fully trust the lab ranges, without realizing that many of these ranges are simply based on what is “normal” (typical) for the general population. Because they are looking for acute problems, not trying to troubleshoot chronic mysterious symptoms you may be dealing with, and definitely not to optimize health and wellbeing and give advice on how well your current diet and lifestyle are doing. You’re just one of many patients, a dot on a bell curve.

This is not medicine; it’s PUBLIC HEALTH. And it’s based on the potentially false assumption that all of us are basically the same or that any differences average out. They don’t seem to allow for the possibility that what might be a very healthy diet for one person might be disastrous for someone else. They don’t seem to appreciate how interconnected things like diet and lifestyle are and how many complex dependencies and relationships there are. The advice we give is just … bad. Because it’s all generality. 

We should be screening people for signs of atherosclerosis, and once signs show up, troubleshooting what is going on with THAT person, rather than just a generic protocol they prescribe for all patients. We should be looking at not just bio markers, but genetics, Epigenetics, and looking for better diagnostics that can help ascertain what is actually going on in the body. Instead of telling someone to eat a low salt diet for hypertension, how about actually finding what is causing the hypertension in that person, and then basing treatment on targeting those systems. 

We already know that nutritionism is broken at the philosophical level. For some people, they can’t convert certain forms of nutrients to active forms efficiently (folic acid methylation, for example, or converting beta carotene to vitamin A)… yet our nutritional advice does not account for these types of things. If you’re low on iron, they are gonna give you a generic iron pill, rather than trying to see WHY is your iron low. Is it low intake? Or do you have an absorption problem? Is there a gut or digestive issue? If they are good they will at least check for internal bleeding and ask about menstruation, but that’s about it. Everything is looked at with a reductionist, absolutist, universalist mindset. 

And why wouldn’t they be? Not a lot of time in a 15 minute office visit to debug someone’s lipid metabolism or troubleshoot their RAAS system pathways to get to the root cause of their health issues…. 

We need diagnostics that can accurately assess nutrition, the health of various systems and metabolic pathways, gene expression, etc, so we can see what exactly is going on with an individual’s body given their lifestyle and genome and inputs. Don’t just say “drink less alcohol, alcohol is bad for you” (and DEFINITELY don’t say “be sure you drink a glass or two of red wine with dinner for heart health!). Say “based on X, Y, and Z testing your body is handling your alcohol intake pretty well, though we should also do some regular liver screenings”.

5. Red Dragon fruit an hour before bed. Chew the seeds dnt swallow. 
6. Dry Goji berries 2tablespoons , soak them in warm water for 10mins.. 30mins before bed.. pair these with small piece dark chocolate 
7. Papaya with a squeeze of lime..

Avoid having these with Calcium rich foods like milk , yoghurt or fortified cereal .. which blocks absorption lutein n zeazanthien 

Have it along with a little fat , lutein n zeazanthien,  carotene n lycopene are fat soluble nutrients.. a few walnuts , spoon of Flaxseed oil or a slice of avocado boosts absorption 

Wash the fruits , a gentle rinse just before eating. 

Take out your fruits from refrigerator 30_60mins before consuming.

Eat ur fruits  60_90mins before bed..

aO6lhk3hC6Q.author: @yu8mophoo329
aO6lhk3hC6Q.text:
Dr. Robert Harrison explains that vision loss in older adults is increasing, often due to damage occurring overnight when the retina doesn't receive enough oxygen and nutrients (0:56). He emphasizes that this isn't solely due to age, but rather to preventable damage (0:31).

The video highlights seven fruits that can naturally support eye health and repair overnight, along with five crucial rules for consuming them to maximize their benefits.

Key Fruits for Eye Health:

Kiwi (3:53): Rich in lutein, zeaxanthin, and vitamin C, it protects the retina from oxidative stress and reduces cataract formation. Golden kiwi is preferred for its higher lutein content and lower acidity (5:10).
Watermelon (6:13): Its high water content supports tear film, while lycopene protects retinal cells. The white rind contains citrulline, which boosts nitric oxide for better blood flow to the retina (6:59).
Black Currants (8:25): Exceptionally rich in anthocyanins, they reduce inflammation in retinal blood vessels and support night vision by regenerating rhodopsin. Standardized extract is recommended due to limited fresh availability (9:15).
Blueberries (10:49): High in anthocyanins, they protect photoreceptors from oxidative damage and reduce the risk of macular degeneration. Wild blueberries are preferred for their higher antioxidant content, and they should not be consumed with dairy (11:33).
Dragon Fruit (13:10): Red dragon fruit, rich in betalains, boosts nitric oxide production, increasing oxygen and nutrient delivery to the retina, which improves contrast sensitivity and visual field depth (13:58).
Goji Berries (15:26): A rich source of zeaxanthin, they concentrate in the macula, acting as a shield for central vision and preventing macular thinning. Soaking them before consumption is recommended (16:15).
Papaya (18:12): Contains papain, an enzyme that breaks down damaging glycation compounds in the eye, and is a good source of vitamins A, C, and E, supporting rod cells, tear production, and photoreceptor protection (19:02).

qGb7hYMlA3o.text: Wonderful episode Andrew. Terry is so right: addiction is a substitute for relationship (I am now 44 years sober).  I was fortunate enough to have been married (after getting sober) for 27 years to a man who embodied so many of the qualities Terry talks about: he not only had a capacity for being relational, he insisted on it.  He was an experienced and master therapist, and taught me everything I know about being in relationship.  Which has been essential in my being a good therapist myself for the past 30+ years. The last seven years of his life broke my heart as he slowly disappeared because of Alzheimers.  I write about it in the first chapter of my second little book: "From Here to Beyond" (Amazon) (first is "From There to Here: An Alphabet Memoir") authored at the age of 86. (Incidentally, I'm sure you know that Bob Sapolsky is adamant in maintaining that there is no free will!)

-gFhv8fC7_Y.text: I’m 74 and for years I believed more eggs and whey protein were the answer to keeping my strength. Even with light weight training, my legs were still shrinking and I felt frustrated. About three months ago I started adding quinoa, black beans, and cooked spinach to my meals instead of relying only on animal protein, and I’ve actually noticed better stamina and stronger grip. I’m not saying eggs are “bad,” but combining plant proteins seems to make a difference for me. Has anyone else tried mixing plant and animal protein and tracked their strength over a few weeks?

X4Qx2UXEQVU.text: Is there any research between a tea & toast diet and chronic dieters in general with reduced pancreatic function, and the cause being too little calories and nutrients? I'm curious because those with eating disorders or those that have been starved have reduced pancreatic function (pancreatic insufficiency) and digestion slows (bloating, constipation, etc). These people often get misdiagnosed with SIBO and leaky gut and such. I am wondering if anyone is looking at the prevalence of dieting (incorrectly in the sense of not understanding caloric and nutrient needs) and slowed pancreatic function and that domino effect into Vit D and Vit A? I think there's a lot of this going on, much more than anyone is even looking into. Would love to hear more about this as it could help bring awareness to the importance of enough calories in the diet vs a society that constantly shouts that we eat too much.

n_MVhE63ZQQ.author: @YourIdentityIsAHoax
n_MVhE63ZQQ.text:
This episode quietly dismantles a very modern illusion:

We live like we’re disembodied minds who just “think positive,” while eating ultra-processed food, nuking our microbes with antibiotics, and wondering why our mood, focus, and willpower feel broken.

The gut isn’t just about digestion, it’s a regulatory environment for the psyche. A chronically inflamed microbiome doesn’t just give you GI issues, it warps your emotional baseline,  irritability, brain fog, low resilience, overreactions in relationships.

It’s funny: people talk about “working on themselves” while completely ignoring the ecosystem that’s modulating their neurotransmitters 24/7.

Identity, behavior, even what we think is “free will” looks different when you realize how much is being negotiated by organisms you never see.

This intersection between biology, identity and behavior is exactly what I explore on my channel lately

Low estrogen and its effect on ramping up cortisol makes it very difficult to have good sleep - no one seems to have a solution, and I've watched almost every video saying to do this, that, or the other thing. Exercise, low carb, no screens for 2 hrs before bed, no blue light, supplements like berberine, GABA, and melatonin, hypnosis or meditation, reading something boring, etc etc etc.  Nothing works.  :/  Many women at the menopausal age also have significantly more stress because they're in leadership positions at work, caring for elderly parents, including dementia, or grieving the loss of a parent, or helping adult children with childcare -- or, as for some of us, all three at once.  Talk about a stressload!  Hormone therapy alone reduced my cortisol to prevent the 3 am waking and not getting back to sleep.  And with all the work and family responsibilities on my plate, visceral fat is the least of my problems - but the sleep helps me stay sane, and that's my main reason for HRT.  Don't rule it out if you're really struggling!

x3sO1MFHOGA.author: @PerAsperaAdAstra8755
x3sO1MFHOGA.text:
*Dr. Nick Norwitz's 7 Ways to Lower Blood Sugar (Without Cutting Carbs):*

1. *Post-Meal Walks:* Taking a 15–30 minute walk after eating activates glute-4 receptors in your muscles via an insulin-independent pathway. This allows your muscles to act as "glucose sponges," soaking up sugar directly from the bloodstream [01:13]

2. *Red Light Exposure:* Exposure to red and infrared light (including natural sunlight) can penetrate the skin to influence mitochondrial function and energy utilization, helping to stabilize blood glucose [03:06]

3. *Meal Sequencing:* The order in which you eat your food matters significantly. Eating *protein and fiber-rich vegetables before carbohydrates* can reduce the total blood sugar spike by as much as *73%* compared to eating the same foods in reverse order [03:42]

4. *Use a Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM):* Individual responses to food vary wildly. Norwitz notes that some people spike higher from a banana than a cookie, or from kale than chocolate, due to personal inflammatory and glycemic responses. A CGM helps identify these unique triggers [04:41]

5. *Be Aware of Medications:* Some common drugs affect glucose metabolism. For instance, **statins** have been associated with long-term increases in HbA1c and may significantly reduce the hormone *GLP-1* (which helps regulate blood sugar) [05:35].
.
6. *Allulose as a Sweetener:* Allulose is a natural sugar that has negligible calories and does not spike blood sugar. When consumed with regular sugar, it can actually blunt the glycemic spike you would otherwise experience [07:23].

7. *Stress Management (Breathing/Meditation):* Stress activates the *medial amygdala,* which has a "direct wire" to the liver. This neural connection can trigger the liver to produce sugar immediately (bypassing cortisol and adrenaline), so reducing stress stops your body from manufacturing unnecessary sugar [08:19]

*The Key Takeaway:* Blood sugar management is about more than just what you eat; it's heavily influenced by *how* you eat, your physical activity, light exposure, and even your brain's direct control over liver function [09:01].

x3sO1MFHOGA.author: @Murse_Darius
x3sO1MFHOGA.text: In my experience, the walk has to be 30+ minutes, and can't be just a casual stroll. You don't need to power walk, but it needs to be somewhat like how people walk on the sidewalks of a big city. Like you have somewhere to be. If you stop too early, though, you'll often still get a rise. If you want to completely flatten the spike, 45 min will do the trick. Also, you don't have to walk. The soleus muscle in the calf is one of the most glucose hungry muscles in the body. So even if you stand in place, like when you're washing dishes and just do calf raises, it works. (I've done this very experiment myself).

m4V95AQ6-OQ.author: @PatientAdvocateHelp
m4V95AQ6-OQ.text:
Great video.  The reason walking helps so much is that it helps move the lymphatic system. This is especially important in chronically ill patients. The lymphatic system is now being shown in new research to be an important organ in fat loss especially as it relates to systemic inflammation. 

Another issue that you should address is the chronic consumption of C15 fatty acid.  This is a product that should only cycled. The reason it helps and or can acutely improve health issues is that it polarizes your macrophages to M2. This is the anti-inflammatory pathway.  A low carb ketogenic diet and glucocorticoids also polarize the macrophages to M2. Studies now show chronic polarization of macrophages to M1 or M2 eventually develops insulin resistance even in lean fit individuals.  

Whereas, most long chain fatty acids polarize macrophages to M1, C15 polarizes the macrophages to M2. I believe future studies will eventually show chronic intake of C15 may be detrimental to health. However, acute intake and cycling said nutritional supplement is fantastic. 

There is also some early research indicating possible issues of creating endoplasmic reticulum stress. While I haven’t thoroughly investigated said issues, this could be a potential issue.

4u6L-7nf6C4.author: @dankelly2147
4u6L-7nf6C4.text: Along the Mexican border in the mountains of the Sonoran Desert in a small village in Southern Arizona at half past 0400.  A retired educator, I found this gem an extraordinary tool to share with my grandchildren. Handled tenderly, rather than fear, I will use this to build hope and a vision of possibility for my three young teens to gird themselves with in their lives. With two of the three destined for futures in the sciences, whetting their appetites for possibilities to  be created may well prepare them to shift the zeitgeist in the generations coming.  With compassion, kindness and rigor, I have every faith that humanity, in all its imperfections, can create imperfect but perfectible opportunities to adapt and overcome the effects of climate change.  At some profound level I find the human spirit irrepressible, conundrums, parchocialisms and fear notwithstanding!

4mbHKUtvA08.author: @JimsworldSanDiego
4mbHKUtvA08.text:
Read along:
Cold Plunge (0:10): Rated 4/10. Primarily beneficial for mental resilience rather than significant physiological changes like mitochondrial health or brown fat stimulation in acute settings. Prolonged exposure to moderately cool temperatures is more effective.
Sauna (1:02): Rated 9/10. High heat saunas offer substantial benefits for heat shock proteins, mitochondrial resilience, and lymphatic drainage for the brain. Infrared saunas are good, but high heat provides the most significant advantages.
High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT) (1:57): Rated 10/10. Provides metabolic benefits of both resistance training and aerobic conditioning. However, it's easy to overdo, leading to increased cortisol and sleep disruption. Keep sessions under 15 minutes of total work time, a few times per week.
Zone 2 Training (2:50): Rated 7.5/10. Excellent for building the "floor" of metabolic health and improving mitochondrial efficiency, making the body more resilient and enhancing recovery.
Detoxing (3:45): Rated 5/10. True detoxification happens internally by supporting the body's natural processes with tools like soluble fiber and antioxidants. External detoxes are largely ineffective.
Fasting (4:43): Rated 8/10. Effective for fat loss, mental clarity, autophagy, and cellular cleanup. The key is not to overdo it, as it can become mere caloric restriction.
Wearables (5:56): Rated 6/10. While providing valuable metrics and data, they can cause users to lose touch with their own body's signals.
Resistance Training (7:04): Rated 10/10. Highly beneficial for brain health, metabolism, detoxification, and overall bodily function.
Creatine (7:18): Rated 9/10. Research increasingly shows its benefits extend to anti-inflammatory pathways, helping control inflammation at systemic, brain, muscular, and joint levels.
Personalized Supplements & Gene Testing (7:59): Rated 4/10. Often expensive and not always necessary, as many people need similar basic supplements. Genetic testing can be useful for specific mutations but doesn't provide a complete health picture.
Blue Light Blocking Glasses (9:11): Rated 6/10. While blue light at night isn't ideal, reducing overall brightness and avoiding LED flicker (by switching to incandescent lights) are more crucial for brain health and sleep.
Kiwi for Sleep (10:09): Rated 3/10. Despite containing melatonin-boosting properties, kiwis are high in glucose, which can disrupt sleep for many, counteracting any potential benefits.
Raw Milk (11:02): Rated 6/10. While potentially beneficial in its purest form, mass production and lack of standardization make it risky due to potential contamination. A2 variety milk is a safer and good quality alternative.
Protein Everything (12:18): Rated 6/10. Protein-spiked foods can be good if they use quality protein sources like whey or milk protein. However, many products use minimal or less effective proteins for marketing purposes, so reading labels is essential.
Methylene Blue (13:04): Rated 8/10. Highly effective for strategic, low-dose use during periods of stress, high altitude, or increased training volume, aiding mitochondrial function and recovery. Not recommended for daily use.
Peptides (13:59): Rated 5/10. Some peptides like BPC 157 and TB500 are highly beneficial, but many others are ineffective or carry more risk than reward. Thorough research is advised.
Long-Term Keto (14:55): Rated 7/10. Can lead to oxidative stress if sustained for too long.
Intermittent Keto (15:05): Rated 9.5/10. Cyclical keto (e.g., seasonal) is highly effective for brain signaling, anti-inflammatory pathways, mitochondrial biogenesis, and autophagy, making the body more resilient.
Limiting Seed Oils (15:44): Rated 7/10. Avoiding or limiting industrialized, highly processed seed oils is beneficial due to their oxidative and chemical nature. Natural sources of mono and polyunsaturated fats are fine.

Y3NUEPNZDMA.text: Andrew, thank you for this. I have been struggling with Anxiety/panic attacks which have started last year due to a long absence from skateboarding (due to injury and other factors) and other physical activity. These have become a bit more severe in the past month and I am now on a psychological battle with myself, applying deep breathing technics as mentioned, as well as, being back on my board for the first time in 6 months. Skateboarding is once again breathing life into my routine and when I start a session, within 5 minutes my breathing gets normalised. It's that "break" in the brain that disrupts the "bad breathing" pattern. Even after 38 years of skateboarding, the benefits never fail to amaze me. Yes it has become more difficult to skate as I used to in my 20's, 30"s and even my early 40's and injury is more frequent nowadays but, the benefit is tremendous. Keep up the great work. Stay strong.

14Vh7zk9gXI.text: I have made some good changes since October first of 2025. At 65, I wanted to try something new. I stopped eating rice, pasta, and potatoes. I started eating Greek yogurt with Chi seeds and Hemp seeds, with blueberries, almond slices, and Ceylon cinnamon every day. I started fasting instead of eating breakfast. I bike indoors for 40 minutes 5-6 days a week. I also started to do a little bit of weight exercising and using a resistance tool for my arms and chest. Recently I noticed that my inflammation and joint pain have disappeared. I am so happy about that. With my inflammation gone, I hope that means my insulin resistance is improving. I even lost 6-7 pounds this fall instead of putting on some winter weight like I usually do. These major improvements have got me so motivated to keep going strong and to be in great shape for when outdoor biking starts in the spring here in Wisconsin. It is amazing how much better my mind and body is when you make some positive changes in your life.

ksV9f4jSbLQ.author: @VashtiWood
ksV9f4jSbLQ.text:
I had an unscheduled hospital visit thanks to anaphylaxis, during which point it was determined I was in a state of ketoacidosis  because I had 5.5mmol/L ketones AND my blood sugar was so HIGH (AROUND 20MMOL/L - a direct result of the intravenous adrenaline and steroid shots)

They wanted me on intravenous insulin!  

Idiots!

I told them that wouldn't be necessary, that upon withdrawal of adrenaline they'd see my blood sugars go back down to around 2.5-3mmol/L. And, yes, when that happened 17 hours later (yes, I was in intravenous adrenaline for 17 straight hours - NOT recommended) General Medicine (who accepted me into Resus before transferring my to ICU) tried to hold an intervention to have my driver's licence rescinded etc...

Thankfully, the ICU Dr understood therapeutic ketosis...


But it's such a mine field out there, with so few medical professionals understanding, and even less who want to listen to the patient! 

To think an untrained patient could know more about their own body that the trained doctor!

iJbgge9kTKI.author: @AstroSleep01
iJbgge9kTKI.text: There's something uniquely comforting about learning that the Milky Way and Andromeda are gravitationally bound — like two slow dancers that have been drifting toward each other for billions of years. The narration here turns cold astronomy into something that actually feels warm. Exactly what bedtime science should be.

iJbgge9kTKI.author: @EarthThroughTimee
iJbgge9kTKI.text: 1:59:59–2:01:07 the moment it explains that “the Milky Way and Andromeda are moving toward each other at roughly 100 km/s” and that this inward motion proves the Local Group is truly bound really stayed with me. I love how that gentle but unstoppable approach turns abstract scale into something tangible—billions of years of quiet motion shaping our future sky. It makes the Local Group feel less like scattered islands and more like a shared story in progress. Does anyone else find it calming rather than frightening that our galaxy’s biggest event is unfolding so slowly?

PGdcEL_nb88.author: @mariothepookster
PGdcEL_nb88.text: Los Angeles, CA @ 9 am. I started listening to this extraordinary video not because I needed to fall asleep but because I’ve been interested in human evolution, in this case anthropology, since my teens. What piqued my  interest was a college course in anthropology where I learned there were two branches in anthropology: physical and cultural. Around that time (late 1960s) there was a question of whether humans evolved from ancestral primates that were naturally non aggressive peaceful vegetarians or aggressive predators that hunted animals for food and fought for territorial imperative. What I like about this video was that it provided an explanation of the impact of physical and cultural aspects that created today’s humans. I didn’t realized that human didn’t change much since 50,000 years ago. If this subject matter interests you, look for YouTube videos that track the migration of modern day humans out of Africa about 100,000 years ago from a population known today as the “click” people. 👍❤

m_UcGss_9jc.author: @theClawfather
m_UcGss_9jc.text:
I'm about to speculate.
What if some forms of depression (at least those associated with visceral fat) are an evolutionary adaptation to help humans slow down and consume less food. In our distant past, if you stopped moving you'd likely stop eating, as you can no longer hunt or gather. In our modern environment it's much easier to have someone else procure and prepare the food for you. You'd still be hungry while depressed, but the depression itself would inhibit excess calorie consumption. 
Wild speculation over.

xJfJ4XfmnlE.author: @NaturalBornSupaFiend
xJfJ4XfmnlE.text:
This made me think of my little buddy Neo, I got him when I was around 12. My previous kitten had been run over in an accident a few years earlier and I blamed myself for not keeping a better eye on her and so I was scared to get another cat until I finally adopted Neo. He lived with us all the way through my adolescence and stayed at my parents when I moved out. 

He would “play soccer” with me as a kid. I would kick the ball in the backyard and he would chase the ball once it started moving, running alongside it getting ready to pounce and then at the last moment would abort his pounce - presumably realising that if he pounced on a soccer ball moving at speed he’d have got completely bowled over 😂 but he was always there, repeating this ritual - the ball triggering his ancient chase response! He was so fun to play with, almost like a dog - though I could never convince him to fetch, only chase lol.

He loved to lay down in front of the heater in the living room or on the heated bathroom floor and like me - had very little self control with food 😂 he kept eating till he would puke and we had to cover the other cat’s food so he wouldn’t eat it, silly boy. He would come and sleep on my bed purring away like a vibrating, living foot warmer at night. He also learned to call for his humans to let him in and out by running his paws down the glass rapidly making a funny, squeegee sort of noise on the glass door.

Even after I left home it always felt like he remembered and recognised me whenever I came home to visit. He passed away ~4 years ago. He was 19 I believe, my parents told me he was getting much more tired and less mobile but still holding on, curled up on the heated bathroom floor. We decided to let him pass naturally unless we had any reason to believe he was suffering. I came to see him for the last time, spoke to him, gently cuddled him and thanked him for everything and then he passed peacefully that night. It really felt like he was waiting to see me before he let go. 

We buried him in a raised bed directly under a beautiful new specimen tree. Neo’s tree has grown into a beautiful reminder of my old friend.

ojAjUKcx7p4.author: @chai7501
ojAjUKcx7p4.text: Re how viewing bright light a couple of hours before sleep disrupts the sleeping pattern. Circadian rhythm regulation is really key to all aspects of health, yet it is all too often overlooked. Many people have bright screens near their eyes at night, unaware of the disruption that is causing their body clocks. Avoiding bright light near the eyes, a couple of hours before bedtime, is essential for sleep, as is having darkness during sleep, as both allow the brain to make the sleep hormone melatonin. Viewing light close to waking up in the morning is essential for alertness and energy.  We evolved to have circadian rhythm that follows the light / dark cycle of day and night,  and these light /dark signals reach the brain via the eyes, which are actually part of the brain. For anyone living in the Northern hemisphere or who has to be up before the sun is, highly recommend getting a SAD lamp to wake the brain up in the morning, the difference it can make is amazing.

fepP7jhu0E0.author: @jmmahony
fepP7jhu0E0.text: The final chapter here- the transition from farming villages and a few scattered small cities to large city-states and kingdoms, happened roughly 4000-3000 BC, which happened to coincide with a period of drying across northern Africa and the middle east (before then, the Sahara and the Mideast was much greener than it is now). That made people  crowd into the Nile Valley and Mesopotamia (between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers). For the people already there, organizing into larger units made it easier to defend their land against these invading newcomers, and it made it possible to dig larger networks of irrigation canals, to make more land arable.

I'm sure there was some fighting, and a lot were killed, but I think most were just bred out of existence.

OTj6HEzJi_M.author: @CharlesHenson-m6r
OTj6HEzJi_M.text:
Yes, recent research suggests grounding/earthing significantly affects mitochondria, improving their function by delivering antioxidant electrons from the Earth, which reduces harmful Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS), boosts ATP (energy) production (by 5-11%), and stabilizes the mitochondrial membrane potential, essentially reducing oxidative stress and supporting cellular energy. This helps protect mitochondria from damage, potentially enhancing cellular health and combating chronic inflammation linked to aging. 
Key Effects on Mitochondria
Reduced ROS: Grounding delivers electrons that neutralize free radicals, decreasing oxidative stress (ROS production by 22-33%) within mitochondria.
Increased ATP: Mitochondria under grounded conditions produce more ATP (energy) (5-11% increase).
Stabilized Membrane Potential: Grounding decreases the mitochondrial membrane potential (by 5-6%), indicating improved bioenergetics and stability.
Antioxidant Protection: The Earth's free electrons act as antioxidants, protecting the mitochondrial matrix and DNA from oxidative damage. 
How it Works (The Theory)
Electron Transfer: The body acts as a conductor, receiving free electrons from the Earth.
Neutralizing Free Radicals: These electrons neutralize inflammatory free radicals, reducing the body's electrical tension and oxidative burden.
Restoring Natural State: Grounding helps restore the body's natural electrical potential, promoting a calmer internal environment crucial for optimal mitochondrial function, as chronic disconnection from the Earth can disrupt this balance. 
Implications for Health
By improving mitochondrial function, grounding may help combat chronic inflammation and support overall health, with implications for longevity, cardiovascular health, and recovery from stress-related conditions.

I'm from Wisconsin USA and have been living here in the Philippines for the last 15 years. I have observed coral and sea shells in the mountains at around 800 to 1200 feet above the current sea level. Not just in one area but actually at every mountain I have visited here on the big island of Mindanao. I'm curious how long ago and for how long was the sea level high enough to promote the growth of coral reefs on all the mountains here?
During such a time only the tops of mountains were exposed as well as some of the higher elevations such as Bukidnon province near the center of Mindanao island. 
I would enjoy a program about that time period in history focusing on what I have mentioned. Or comments from others that have also seen the corals at such high elevations either here in the Philippines or any other country. 🇺🇲🇵🇭✌️🌴

Since I didn't see sources for Serine mentioned other than as a supplement. A quick Google search - Serine sources include protein-rich foods like meat, fish, eggs, dairy, and soy, as well as nuts, legumes (chickpeas, lentils), and grains, but your body also produces it from glucose, glycine, and other metabolites. It's a nonessential amino acid, meaning your body can make it, but dietary intake is still important for brain health and function, especially from foods like soybeans, peanuts, almonds, sweet potatoes, and whey. 
So it would seem to be hard to be deficient in Serine to me, but I'm just a guy watching the video and not a health professional.

e77QotBNEEc.author: @HealthySeniorsHub01
e77QotBNEEc.text:
Summary (Very Short & Punchy)
- New research (2025 human study) shows Taxifolin (Dehydroquercetin) can improve metabolism safely.
- Results: 300 mg/day for 6 months → modest weight loss (+ better HDL) with zero side effects.
How it works:
- Activates brown fat (UCP1, PGC1α, PRDM16) → more calorie burn
- Boosts FGF-21 → AMPK activation, better insulin sensitivity
- Helps muscles absorb glucose even without insulin
- Strong anti-inflammatory & antioxidant effect (NRF2 ↑, NF-κB ↓)
- Why it matters: burns cleaner, stores less, and protects metabolism from inflammation.
- Best combos: cold exposure, berberine, omega-3s.
- Not a magic pill—works best with fasting, exercise, sleep, and good nutrition.

3RhS1UvSWoQ.author: @drjazz777
3RhS1UvSWoQ.text:
🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

00:02 *🧪 The study challenges standard sports nutrition by testing high-carb vs very low-carb pre-exercise meals for performance.*
00:43 *🏃‍♂️ Focus on real-world middle distances: controlled 5K and 10K performance trials.*
01:12 *🎛️ Researchers tightly controlled confounders and compared a very low-carb meal to a guideline-style high-carb meal right before exercise.*
01:52:00 *🧬 Contrary to dogma, recommended carb intakes (e.g., 60–120 g/h) didn’t show the expected performance edge when variables were controlled.*
02:07:00 *🔁 Randomized crossover design: each subject did time trials on both pre-exercise meal conditions.*
02:46:00 *⚖️ Result: no difference in 5K/10K performance between high-carb and very low-carb pre-exercise meals.*
03:16:00 *🔥 Fuel use diverged: meals produced distinct carb vs fat oxidation profiles, yet performance was identical.*
05:19:00 *🧂 Presenter plug aside, electrolytes are suggested when fasting or cutting calories for comfort and adherence.*
06:14:00 *🎯 This was an acute pre-exercise meal test, not long-term diet—aimed at the immediate effect on performance.*
06:40:00 *🌙 Related evidence: some 5K studies show fasting doesn’t impair performance, though longer events may differ.*
09:28:00 *🧪 Prior trial: after 4 weeks keto adaptation, trained runners showed no decline in a 1-mile time trial or 6×800 m at >85% VO₂max.*
11:21:00 *🚀 Record fat burning: keto-adapted athletes oxidized >1.5 g/min (up to ~1.85 g/min) fat at high intensities—previously thought impossible.*
12:33:00 *🛡️ Long-duration test: Ironman athletes completed 6-week high-carb vs low-carb phases (with washout), tightly controlling training and calories.*
15:22:00 *🧷 Nuance for athletes: success on low-carb hinges on electrolytes, protein timing/amount, calories, and possibly strategic carbs.*
16:18:00 *🩺 Health angle: in a 2023 study, ~30% on high-carb showed prediabetic glucose—raising concerns even for lean, active athletes.*
17:28:00 *⚠️ “Fit but unhealthy” risk: frequent rapid-acting carbs can drive repeated glucose/insulin spikes despite high training volumes.*
19:02:00 *📊 Broader context: metabolic dysfunction is rising (including in youth), making diet quality a central performance-and-health issue.*
20:11:00 *🎯 Goal alignment: athletes shouldn’t trade health for performance—pursue both via appropriate dietary strategy.*
21:18:00 *🏆 Case example: Ironman champion Lionel Sanders reported reversing prediabetes by cutting ultra-processed carbs and then winning a world title.*
22:14:00 *🩹 Personal note: as a type 1 diabetic, the speaker underscores how glucose/insulin management impacts daily function and long-term health.*
22:43 *🧭 Athletes often prioritize performance over long-term health, sometimes following guidance that may not benefit metabolic health.*
23:21 *🏊‍♂️ Ironman elites skew older due to time demands, potentially making them more health-aware than younger pros.*
24:02 *🏋️ Younger athletes in high-intensity sports may ignore metabolic health in pursuit of gains.*
24:46 *🔮 Prediction: many athletes in their 20s–30s today could face metabolic problems within a decade.*
25:56 *📢 Message to athletes/coaches/clinicians: peak performance need not come at the expense of health.*
26:11 *😮 In a tightly controlled study, ~30% of lean, high-VO₂max runners showed glucose levels consistent with prediabetes.*
27:07 *🧪 The study controlled calories, weight, training, diet adherence (ketones), and CGM data with dietitian oversight.*
27:35 *🧰 Standard diagnostics (fasting glucose, OGTT, HbA1c) each have important limitations for athletes.*
29:22 *⏱️ Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) every 5 minutes across 4–6 weeks provided a richer picture of glycemic control.*
31:41 *📉 Age influences risk somewhat, but BMI and VO₂max weren’t strong predictors of fasted glucose status.*
32:07 *🍚 Diet—specifically daily carbohydrate amount—best predicted fasted glucose (baseline metabolic state).*
33:01 *🧩 Fasted glucose reflects underlying metabolism by stripping away meal/exercise “noise.”*
34:10 *🔁 Metabolically flexible individuals tolerate fasted training better; insulin-resistant people struggle more.*
35:02 *🧯 Pre-exercise carbs spike insulin, suppressing lipolysis and reducing fat oxidation capacity.*
35:56 *🧬 With fat use suppressed, muscle glycogen breakdown increases to sustain effort.*
37:22 *⚖️ Insulin is anabolic but context matters; keto adaptation lowers insulin and ketones can reduce protein breakdown.*
38:45 *🎯 Insulin acts more potently at fat cells than muscle, blocking fat release and pushing reliance on muscle glycogen.*
40:34 *⛽ Glycogen duration varies: ~4–5 hours at low intensity, but only ~1–2 hours at 70–80% VO₂max.*
41:30 *🧃 Frequent gels keep insulin elevated, potentially limiting fat use when glycogen is low—implications remain uncertain.*
43:08 *🧨 Ultra-endurance events are inherently catabolic; post-race labs often look severely stressed.*
43:52 *🧠 Regardless of diet, long efforts without fuel can cause hypoglycemia and neuroglycopenic symptoms.*
44:06 *🏁 Historical data (1920s Boston Marathon): runners finished hypoglycemic; providing carbs during races improved outcomes.*
45:00 *🧠 Century of data shows carbs aid performance mainly by preventing exercise-induced hypoglycemia.*
45:44 *⏳ Even on ketogenic diets, prolonged efforts (often >2 hours) still drive glucose down.*
46:10 *🧪 Across 160+ trials, hypoglycemia—not glycogen or carb-oxidation capacity—best explained carb benefits.*
46:40 *🩸 In controls, performance fell as glucose dipped; carbs worked by averting that dip.*
47:06 *🥑 Keto athletes still benefit from small intra-workout carbs to avoid bonking.*
47:36 *⚖️ Hypoglycemia occurred in most athletes during ≥2-hour efforts on both high- and low-carb diets.*
47:49 *🧮 Despite lower glucose on keto, total brain fuels (glucose+ketones+lactate) matched high-carb, with equal performance.*
48:45 *📉 “Minimal effective dose”: ~10 g carbohydrate per hour abolished hypoglycemia without altering glycogen use.*
49:12 *🚀 That tiny dose improved performance by ~22% across diets.*
49:24 *📊 Current guidelines (60–120+ g/h, even ~200 g/h) may overshoot what’s needed for performance.*
50:06 *🛑 Chronic high carb prescriptions risk long-term metabolic harm in some athletes.*
51:01 *🍚 Some guidance suggests ~1,000 g/day carbs for heavy training—flagged here as potentially dangerous.*
51:43 *⚖️ Double standard noted: high saturated fat is criticized, yet extreme carb loads get a pass.*
53:17 *🔄 Modeling that “requires” carbs at high intensities is challenged by real-world fat-oxidation data.*
53:59 *🏆 Example: world-class Ironman showed predominantly fat oxidation for ~half the record performance.*
54:39 *🔥 Keto-adapted athletes can sustain unprecedented fat oxidation even at >85% VO₂max.*
55:21 *🧭 High fat-oxidation capacity correlates with elite performance—even in high-carb athletes.*
55:49 *🧰 Practical keto tips: avoid unintended calorie deficit; keep diet well-formulated with adequate micronutrients.*

57:53 *🧂 Electrolytes matter: early keto lowers insulin and sodium retention—supplement sodium to feel and perform better.*
01:00:11 *🎯 For long events, use the minimum carb dose that prevents hypoglycemia without suppressing fat oxidation.*
01:01:19 *🧬 Longer keto exposure may further upregulate fat-use enzymes—adaptation appears time-dependent.*
01:02:42 *🧯 Raising the “fat-burning zone”: keto can shift fat oxidation to higher intensities, aiding body-composition goals.*
01:03:39 *⚖️ After 4–6 weeks keto, fat-oxidation capacity can rise 3–4× while carb reliance falls (not to zero).*
01:05:32 *🏃 Slight, safe reductions in body mass during endurance efforts may marginally boost performance.*
01:06:01 *🧪 Plasma volume changes may influence performance, but the science remains limited and underexplored.*

3RhS1UvSWoQ.text: This is one of the biggest questions I recently facing as an ultra runner. I have been experiencing last 2 years with limiting fuel as much possible during long duration run. I recently figure out I only need start eating at 2hr mark. And then half an orange for maybe next hour depends on the weather.  Normally my heart rate flat out after 3 hrs and normally stay at low zone 3 comfortabily the rest of the day. At the zone 3 state my experience tell me that an orange every hour is all I need. I aware most of the energy problem is to do with salt deficiency instead, especially hot weather mindful with magnesium(muscle tension) and potassium(elevated heart rate) deficiency. By saying this, I also received an adviced by my friend as a experience ultra runner telling me I need 60g of carb. every hour, and that is like 4 oranges per hour. Artificial products never work on me, so I decided to stick to what I experimented. Just shared some of the discovery.

This is a truly gentle and profound video 🌌

The way you tell the story of our place in the Milky Way doesn’t overwhelm the listener. Instead, it makes everything feel intimate and easy to breathe in. I especially loved the scale comparisons, the forest, the coin, the 230-million-year orbit, scientific yet poetic, and somehow they make the mind slow down naturally.

More than astronomy, this video feels like a quiet reminder that although we are small in the universe, understanding where we are brings a strange sense of connection and calm. It’s perfect for nighttime listening, when thoughts need a wider space to soften and settle.

Thank you, Science before Sleep, for such a peaceful journey through science and serenity ✨

3A9zTXOdg24.author: @ytpah9823
3A9zTXOdg24.text:
🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

💡 Controlling insulin, the "master hormone" regulating fat storage, is key to improving diet outcomes and overall energy.
🧠 Brain fog, stubborn body fat, and energy fluctuations may be linked to a single master hormone: insulin.
⚠️ Insulin management is crucial even for lean, fit, and healthy individuals, as silent struggles can rob energy and focus.
🗺️ The video will cover four key areas: how insulin works, a mineral to "turn off" insulin resistance, how a small amount of sugar can control glucose spikes, and an actionable timeline.
⚖️ Insulin is necessary but is also the "absorptive hormone" that helps absorb both glucose and fat, which can be problematic if not managed.
🛡️ Controlling insulin spikes is essential for proper nutrient absorption, glucose entering the muscle for energy, and preventing unnecessary fat storage.
🚫 Insulin stops fat loss by turning off hormone-sensitive lipase, keeping the body in an "absorptive mode" where stored fat cannot be released into the bloodstream.
🍽️ Periods of absorption are necessary for nutrients, but too much absorption can stress the body, cause excess inflammation, and lead to feeling unwell.
🛑 Too much insulin becomes inflammatory, promotes fat storage, causes "brain fogging," and can halt mitochondria from producing energy.
🥛 Calcium, typically associated with bones and teeth, plays an arguably more critical role in metabolism.
🚨 Calcium acts as the "go signal" or trigger that causes the pancreas's beta cells to release insulin into the bloodstream when blood sugar rises.
🔬 The rush of calcium into the cell is the physical trigger that initiates insulin release, a mechanism confirmed by molecular data.
✅ Supporting the initial calcium mechanism can lead to more efficient insulin release, allowing glucose to clear faster without the pancreas overworking.
💧 A simple takeaway is to sip on high-calcium mineral water (like Gerolsteiner or San Pellegrino) or consume A2 milk or Kefir throughout the day.
🍶 Kefir is recommended because it provides calcium along with the benefits of probiotics and fermented food.
💊 Adding calcium supplements should be avoided unless a specific need is identified, as they can increase coronary artery calcium and are not recommended by groups like the American Heart Association.
🐟 Getting calcium from food sources like sardines (with bone), Kefir, and cottage cheese is preferred over supplements.
💪 The "ultimate insulin hack" that doesn't require supplements or fancy food is muscle contractions—simply moving your body.
🏃 Muscle contraction, through movement like bodyweight squats, push-ups, or even a walk, can pull glucose out of the bloodstream without requiring insulin.
🚪 Your muscles have GLUT4 transporters (glucose gateways) that typically wait for insulin to open them, but muscle contraction bypasses this.
🔥 Contracting a muscle activates the energy sensor AMPK, which signals the GLUT4 gateways to immediately move to the cell surface and let glucose in for fuel, bypassing the need for insulin.
🚶 The easiest application is to move (e.g., 10-20 push-ups or a 10-15 minute walk) immediately after a meal instead of sitting down.
🎯 Immediate post-meal movement pulls sugar into muscles for fuel, preventing it from spiking insulin and interfering with fat burning.
🤯 Consuming a specific, small dose of the sugar fructose can surprisingly help manage overall blood sugar response.
📉 Adding just 7.5 grams of fructose (less than a tablespoon of honey) to a glucose drink resulted in a 14% lower blood sugar response in adults with Type 2 diabetes.
🌊 This mechanism works by leveraging the liver's function: the small dose of fructose activates the enzyme hepatic glucokinase, which pre-primes the liver to aggressively pull glucose out of the blood and store it.
🍓 A small handful of berries is an ideal way to implement this, as they provide the specific dose of fructose to prime the liver, along with fiber to slow absorption and polyphenols that also affect blood sugar.
⏱️ The fructose trick is an acute strategy, not an all-day practice; take one teaspoon of raw honey or a small handful of berries 15-20 minutes before a planned high-carb meal.
🍯 This small fructose dose pre-primes the liver to buffer the coming glucose hit from the high-carb meal.
✨ Manuka honey (specifically Manukora brand) is highlighted as a potent source of quality fructose, with added antimicrobial benefits due to a high MGO content.
🔬 The amino acid glycine offers a long-term approach to blood sugar management by building a stronger, more resilient system.

d8LAubzZE2I.author: @YourOrganizedAuntie
d8LAubzZE2I.text: Oh wow, the way my whole brain just did a dramatic faceplant when I saw “Why a Lack of Sleep is Killing Your Mental Performance,” because hold on a second… every time I try to function on low sleep, my thoughts immediately start doing parkour while my inner voice whispers “Luise, girl, we are spiritually horizontal,” and my neurons quietly file a complaint with HR. And can you imagine, your topic unlocked the memory of the morning I tried to give a presentation after sleeping exactly 3 hours, and whew, the chaos that followed still wakes me up at night. I walked into that meeting like “I’m awake, but my consciousness is optional,” and halfway through slide two my last two brain cells started playing ping pong while my mouth said words I absolutely did not authorize. At one point I literally paused, blinked twice, and whispered “what in the world,” because my brain had fully disconnected from the WiFi. And funny enough, that disaster taught me something real: sleep isn’t a luxury, it’s the operating system. Without it, you’re basically a confused pigeon trying to do algebra. Once I stopped treating rest like a suggestion and started going to bed before my soul left the chat, my productivity stopped dragging like hell and my communication actually made sense again. Now I’m curious… what’s the funniest thing YOU’VE ever said or done while running on fumes and pretending to be a fully functioning adult?

Six years ago, my best friend was slowly dying of blader cancer. So, I visited him every week, bringing with me a bottle of Porto that we drank. When I lost him, that was the end of the Porto and the end of my alcohol consumption for good. Exit the Cognac, the Whisky, the wine, becoming in the process a dull friend for another dude who was my booze body. I don't have much merite; I don't regret it or miss it. It's just that, with the death of my best friend (and I won't have time to make another one until I die), a page had to be turned, doing something in my life other than drinking it.
Congrats to all of you who decided to sacrifice alcohol consumption to live a better life.

This really puts into perspective how much is happening in the body behind the scenes—especially during sleep. A lot of people underestimate how critical sleep is, not just for energy but for actual repair, immune function, and inflammation control.

The connection between inflammation, recovery, and things like weight gain or fatigue makes a lot of sense when you look at it this way. It’s not just about diet or exercise alone—sleep and gut health play a huge role too.

Also liked how you kept it practical, focusing on simple habits and foods instead of overcomplicating things. Definitely a good reminder that small daily habits can have a big impact on long-term health.

mCahca4wK-4.author: @SirPatrickStar
mCahca4wK-4.text:
1	Mobility that only works without load – 0:00–1:45 Good bodyweight movement breaks down when weight is added. Reverse it: Train mobility under load to build true functional range and stability.
	2	Inability to hang from a bar for sufficient time – 1:45–3:05 Tests grip strength, shoulder mobility, and core control. Reverse it: Hang daily; progress to 2+ minutes (men) or 90+ seconds (women).
	3	Can’t touch or overlap hands behind your back – 3:05–4:55 Poor shoulder internal/external rotation. Reverse it: Use broomstick-assisted stretches and doorway slides.
	4	Low VO₂ max – 4:55–6:40 Strong predictor of cardiovascular decline and mortality. Reverse it: Perform HIIT 1–2× per week (e.g., 30 sec all-out + 90 sec easy, 6–8 rounds).
	5	Chronic dehydration – 6:40–8:20 Affects cognition, energy, joints, and performance. Reverse it: Drink ~¾ of body weight (lbs) in ounces daily; use a marked bottle with rubber bands to track.
	6	Not supplementing with creatine monohydrate – 8:20–9:55 Impacts muscle preservation, strength, and emerging brain health benefits. Reverse it: Take 5–10 g daily (no cycling needed).
	7	Ignoring physical weaknesses – 9:55–11:20 Avoiding uncomfortable movements creates imbalances and limits progress. Reverse it: Dedicate part of every workout to movements you dislike or suck at.
	8	Not training to true failure – 11:20–13:40 Stopping short of real failure reduces stimulus for muscle and strength gains. Reverse it: Push sets to actual failure (while managing overall volume).
	9	Skipping corrective exercises – 13:40–15:15 Neglecting stabilizers and weak links leads to compensations and injury. Reverse it: Regularly include face pulls, rotator cuff work, hip mobility drills, etc.
	10	Relying exclusively on machines (avoiding free weights) – 15:15–17:45 Misses stabilizer engagement and real-world coordination. Reverse it: Prioritize barbells, dumbbells, and bodyweight for most training.
	11	Not prioritizing protein as you age – 17:45–20:40 Age-related muscle loss lowers metabolism and appetite. Reverse it: Aim for 1–1.2 g protein per lb body weight; include it in every meal and use shakes if needed.
	12	No direct ab/core training – 20:40–22:20 Compound lifts alone don’t fully develop the entire core. Reverse it: Train core directly every day (5–10 min) with anti-rotation, extension, and rotation control exercises.
	13	Jumping straight to pharmaceutical interventions – 22:20–26:45 Using TRT, peptides, or GLP-1 drugs as first-line options instead of optimizing basics. Reverse it: Exhaust training, nutrition, sleep, and recovery first; use meds only when truly needed and under medical supervision.
	14	Poor lateral stability (failing the elevated-leg side plank test) – 26:45–end Inability to hold a side plank with top leg raised for 30 seconds reveals weak glute medius and frontal-plane control. Reverse it: Practice elevated-leg side planks; build up to 30 seconds per side.
Implementing even a few of these changes consistently can dramatically improve both lifespan and quality of life as you age

iLCD25rR8T0.author: @MichaelJensen-g6i
iLCD25rR8T0.text: I liked and subscribed! I'm on 10 meds and have neuropathy but thought I had it due to heredity or some other reason not identifiable. I quit budesonide inhaler and got my voice back. They really tried to push gabapentin but I got the forewarning from my mother being prescribed it. I lost my mother as she was more than sleeping it was like she was unconscious. I took action and stopped it and she returned to normal. The pharmacist had a 90 day supply for me ready and at the counter I withheld and said I didn't want to waste it and take it home just reject it at the counter. Wow did she get into you must have it mode. I thought maybe if I don't take it they will label me non-compliant and put red flags around my name so now 7 years later I still have the three big bottles of it but will never use it. I still have neuropathy and need to figure out how as I didn't realize there are other meds that cause it. I'm 72 and wonder with so many meds if maybe I have shortened my life. I commute by bicycle and had one heart attack and a stent placed. Thanks for this video! I have rosuvastatin 5mg and Imdur 200mg a vaso-dialator... My phramacologist just said she sees some on 30 meds and do fine so no help there!

0XcxiwzX1MQ.author: @Dr.JonathanLucas
0XcxiwzX1MQ.text: “As a physician, I often advise patients over 60 that nerve health requires more than just rest — certain vitamins play a crucial role in repairing and protecting nerves. Vitamins B1 (thiamine), B6, and B12 support nerve regeneration, improve signaling, and reduce inflammation. Vitamin D also helps maintain nerve and muscle function, while antioxidants like vitamin E protect nerves from oxidative stress. Incorporating these nutrients through diet or clinically supervised supplements can significantly support nerve repair, reduce tingling or numbness, and improve overall mobility and quality of life in older adults.”

4KMhLlLXA48.author: @KenBarta
4KMhLlLXA48.text:
Vitamin B1, or thiamin, is a water-soluble vitamin essential for converting food into energy, supporting nerve function, muscle contraction, and heart health.

Deficiency (Beriberi): 
Causes nerve damage (neuritis), confusion, muscle weakness, and heart problems. 

Sources

Natural: Yeast, cereal grains, beans, nuts, meat (especially pork), and poultry.
Fortified: Enriched breads, cereals, and rice.
Supplements: Available individually or as part of a B-complex.

AjxVuyH1g6o.author: @drjenmd
AjxVuyH1g6o.text:
FAQ

Q: Are hormone harmony blends effective in perimenopause?
A: Usually not. They often contain too many ingredients at low doses, and some herbs like vitex may not be effective postmenopause. Focus on targeted nutrients you actually need.

Q: Should I take a multivitamin?
A: Only if you’re deficient in specific nutrients that you can’t get through food. Whole foods are preferred; supplements are for gaps or genetic limitations (e.g., vitamin D conversion issues).

Q: Are calcium supplements necessary?
A: Only if dietary intake is insufficient. Excess calcium from supplements can cause kidney stones or cardiovascular calcifications. Aim for ~1,200 mg/day, ideally from food, with vitamin D (and K2 if possible).

Q: Should I take iron supplements?
A: Only if you are iron deficient. Unnecessary iron can cause constipation, gut disruption, and other issues. Check an iron panel before supplementing.

Q: What’s the general guideline for supplementing in perimenopause?
A: Avoid generic hormone blends and unnecessary multivitamins. Supplement calcium and iron only when needed. Personalize your approach with your doctor—one-size-fits-all does not work.

I was overweight and in poor health and left the hospital with a handful of meds (12 pills) that I was told I would have to take everyday for the rest of my life.  I made some immediate and severe lifestyle changes.  Completely overhauled my eating habbits and started walking 3 miles per day.  In just the first 7 months, lost 85lbs and normalized all my lab work.  I am now a 1/2 dose of 1 med and my heart condition has actually improved a little.  Still walking 3 miles per day and feeling better than ever :-)

OHq6HLWQ-rI.author: @rebeccachurch5200
OHq6HLWQ-rI.text: I started walking in my 20's, 3 miles a day at least 5 days a week.  Now in my 70's I still walk almost every day and still do 3 miles. It takes me about an hour to walk three miles now. I have slowed down in pace over the years. So far my health has been pretty good. Hopefully it will continue.

OHq6HLWQ-rI.author: @donandcarolevans9692

OHq6HLWQ-rI.author: @ROCKMTHI

A.M. hydrate with lemon water +salt.  Morning walk for about one hour in zone 2, Main meal of day 11:00 52g.protein + broccoli and avocado. Light meal around 5:00 p.m. with another 50 +g. Protein. Goal: age 83, keep muscle, support bone repair, control blood sugar.  Fasting 18/6 or 16/8….Two protein centered meals per day. It’s been gradual to adapt to this.

90 day goal is to get to 15% bodyfat from ~23/25%. Started 1st week of Jan. I just found the satiety cheat code that sardines are. Eating two eggs with a can of sardines early in the morning keeps me full the whole day. No cravings all day long. Having a normal healthy low carb meal in the evening and half a Pomelo as an evening snack. Not thinking about food too much. 
Upped my walking goal from 8000/10000 to 14000/15000 steps per day. 

But I hit a weight loss plateau. It is frustrating even though expected. Will just plug along and ride it out. Hoping to break through soon. I just tell my self to be patient and to keep at it.

w0cesPI5tC0.author: @ARFF1Tampa
w0cesPI5tC0.text: Thank you. I stepped in to a gym for the first time in my life 12 months ago at age 63. Mostly for piece of mind (wasn't going to sit around and mope for 4 years) I hired a trainer for the first 4 months to learn, then took over on my own. I, pleasantly, got results I'd never imagined. Trainers and lifetime gym patrons came up to me with questions and compliments I'd never imagined.  "What are you doing to get those results?" ect. I went from 204 pounds 23 percent body fat, to 150 pounds and between 13 and 14.5 percent body fat. I, obviously, being inexperienced, over trained (as I went 5X a week for 3 hours a day (plus I play hockey 3X a week) but it really, made me happy, feel good every day, and was an escape from this disaste[R]. My only unfortunate result is the amount of fat you lose in your face, which changes your appearance (and, not for the better). I feel like I have the body and fitness of a 30 year old, but almost look like an emaciated cancer patient in the face. Sad that improving yourself physically and nutritionally has this negative effect. Also, I had a major setback. I had a slight rotator cuff tear from the over training and, unfortunately, had an unavoidable awkward fall in one of my hockey games that resulted in a complete supraspinatus tear. I had the hernia surgery I had been putting off in November (had just been pushing it back in for a year) followed by the rotator cuff re-attachment surgery on Dec 10th. So I'll be healing and re-habbing for the next 6 or 7 months. Guess I'll get some of the fat back in my face, hopefully. Anyway, I'll save the video and watch it. Thanks again.

w0cesPI5tC0.author: @darrylcote3971
w0cesPI5tC0.text: Had a bad snacking problem but beat it by making alternatives. Chocolate was my crutch so now i make greek yogurt mixed with chocolate protein powder with premium cocoa powder and sugar free maple syryp and freeze ice cube trays of these things they are amazing. I make several variations with fruit aswell and its helped me drop 60 pounds in just over a year maintaining about a -500 daily deficit. VK0x9sB2_l8.author: @WATTYATHINK
VK0x9sB2_l8.text: Commented before, but will again - just to show some support for your commonsense post. I'm nearly 78 now and approaching 3 years after having a hip replacement. I'm 5'8", and I weighed nearly 260lbs at the time of my operation.  I started to watch my diet, and started walking shortly before my operation and dove into it seriously after the operation. I now weigh (this morning 170lbs), and I do at least 2 hrs on the treadmill every morning. My diet was simply to cut down on my meals and walking.  Recently, I've started to do some dumbbell work and those stretching routines (tai chi). My wife of 52 years is also walking with me, and we are averaging over 25,000 steps daily. Our one vice is walking to McD's every night for a muffin and coffee (about 1 hr walk). As you have suggested, I eat everything but have cut down on the portions, I avoid adding salt to my food, and I cut out sweets. I don't count calories, but I normally cut at least one meal a couple of times a week and drink a meal replacement drink instead.
