I’m a natural competitor and my blood work is excellent.  Paul is an ectomorph body type with a naturally high metabolism.  He can get away with eating moderate to high carbs.  Nutrition needs to be tailor made to the individual.  I’m always cautious around people who speak definitively like this.

ZmZzCVHPcHw.author: @DirtyDingles
ZmZzCVHPcHw.text: I’m 57 an I’ve found it’s easy as calories in     calories out.   I intermittent fast 5 days a week and do a 24 hr fast one day a week.  On Sunday I’ll do a re feed  . For me this works the best. I don’t count calories and I even drink lite beer. You just have to find the right strategy that works for you and stay consistent.  Make it easy on yourself. If it’s something you enjoy you’ll find success in it. When I do eat I try and stick to Whole Foods.  Once or twice a week I’ll even eat some ice cream.  Just be sure to burn off more calories than you are taking in. I’ve tried every diet out there.  Im not good at following or tracking my macros.  I call it instinctive eating.

SM10oQCwB7A.author: @txhvacr1978
SM10oQCwB7A.text: Went through this myself, went from 245 down to 204 in eight months, after getting to a point where the hunger was out of control, my energy levels were too low and sleep was not great at all. SM10oQCwB7A.author: @Cody_Trading
SM10oQCwB7A.text:
Just lost 30lbs in 3 months (12 weeks) - 227 -> 197lbs. I ate 2000 cals everyday, about 175g protein, 65g fat, rest carbs. On Sundays I started adding in some more cals as a refeed in the form of a 70% cocoa chocolate bar and a large strawberry blizzard from DQ.

I worked out 3 days a week, Mon, Wed, Fri Push, Legs, Pull. On workout days I would walk 13 to 15k steps and on non workout days I would walk 20k steps. My incline bench press has remained the same so not sure if I am losing any muscle. I just had a burning desire to get under 200lbs.

Next goal is 190lbs and then 185lbs and then I will hit maintenance for a bit. I want to get a 6 pack for summer. I ate the same meal pretty much everyday, this makes things easier. Be consistent with your diet, workout, get a good amount of steps, and stop drinking for awhile, I didn't drink once in the 3 months.

SM10oQCwB7A.author: @rickybobby3043
SM10oQCwB7A.text: I’m 46 years old.  I dropped from 190lbs to 158lbs over the past year and I have 1 shot of vodka mixed into a flavored sparkling water daily.  My job is stressful and I need something to relax at the end of a shift.  But 1 hour of weight lifting at least 4 days a week and almost daily 30-60 minute walks on an inclined treadmill.  I fast everyday (only coffee) until 3:30pm(shift start) and just snack on beef jerky and gum until 8-9pm.  I usually have a 400-500 calorie homemade burrito(barbacoa, carnitas, or pastrami) for my first real meal.  Then I get home around midnight and eat a 1 lb chicken breast(pre-air fried and refrigerated, then sliced, grilled in a skillet with Pam spray, and drenched in Franks hot sauce) and 2-3 waffles covered in sugar free syrup (Mrs. Buttersworth).

KkJqjLsM0hE.author: @danielleotto121
KkJqjLsM0hE.text:
I loved it when Paula said she had to trust the process when her trainer had her go from 1200 calories to 1700 cals. She had to trust it because everything she did in the past wasn't working. 

Paul Revelia's free calculator had me eating roughly 200 grams of carbs when I first started using it over a year ago but I refused to believe that could be right. I was one of those people who was always afraid of carbs so I never went above 100 grams. Anything below 100 and I never had any energy. And guess what, I never made any progress. Never lost any weight.

Six months ago I decided well maybe I should give the 200 grams a shot since nothing I did was working. And that's when I started making progress. Lost sixteen pounds in the last 4 to 5 months and it still keeps dropping as long as I remain true to the process. Thank you Paul for all your work and encouragement helping people like us!!!

rtVzBrSzqBI.author: @ptortland
rtVzBrSzqBI.text:
There is one massively major problem with OMAD, and that is, there is almost no way that you can get your total daily protein requirements in one meal. Even if you could get 150 to 200 g of protein in one meal, your body would not likely be able to metabolize and incorporate a significant portion of that.

Therefore, since you are unlikely to get enough protein, a fairly significant portion of the weight that is lost is likely to be lean muscle mass.

rtVzBrSzqBI.author: @michaelevans9675
rtVzBrSzqBI.text: I started doing something 2 1/2 years ago and I am not a exercise science major or a personal trainer like you. But this has worked for me. I tried the one meal a day which is more of a spiritual thing for me as a Christian to pray And I also tried the keto type thing and I also tried the intermittent fasting thing. Those didn’t work for me. What I tried is what I called the chainsaw diet. I’m metabolism was like an old chainsaw that was rusty in the garage and need new spark plugs and I couldn’t get it started. So basically what I started to do at first was eat about eight meals a day every hour and a half small meals, lots of vegetables, lean proteins, nuts and fruit and then I would eat about one regular meal a day out of those seven. Now I usually do five meals a day and do one regular meal a day for the most part. This is paired with regular exercise, including going to the gym to lift weights, at least four times a week and drinking lots of water. I had tried going to the gym for three or four years, but I never really saw results because I never got my diet in Check The bottom line is you can’t outwork a bad diet. Now that I’m stuck with this plan, it really works for me. I’ve essentially jumpstarted my metabolism and I’ve kept it there. When I eat too much, drink too much beer or eat too much bad food I will start to have stomach problems and gain a little bit of weight and then I will correct. Essentially get up in the morning eat something lean drink some water. Make sure you get enough protein workout And don’t overheat. And then just listen to your body. You’ll see results I’ve seen a lot. I was always skinny and lean and I got up to 210 pounds because of bad diet and getting older and losing my metabolism at age 35. Now I’m almost 43 and I’m sitting at about 160 to 170 consistently. Plus, I’m leaner and I have more muscle. I’m not as cut as this gentleman because I don’t have a really really clean diet, but I’m certainly much better off and much healthier than I was before.

f-cXuzx9rk0.author: @twowheelsintokyo7039
f-cXuzx9rk0.text: The important thing is motivation. Losing weight while cycling is a combination of diet and exercise. If you have the motivation to get out on your bike each day, you will have more motivation to regulate your diet. I used to ride in fast groups, and spent a year racing, and these were a blast. Now I'm older, not so fast, and not so motivated to compete. But I love cycling, A good ride gives me a good workout, and it gets the endorphins flowing. Exercise regularly, your body starts getting hooked on these endorphins (secreted by your body to deal with the physical stress and pain). Pretty soon you don't have to force yourself to go out and ride, you can't help yourself, your body wants to do it. At this point it is easier to eat better. Beyond a certain level, like if you are riding 2 more more hours per day, weight loss is inevitable no matter how much you eat. But as most of us don't have that much time to spend in the saddle, changing one's diet helps. For myself, reducing or eliminating sugar, bread, rice, and such makes a huge difference, and the weight falls off.

f-cXuzx9rk0.author: @Coast-on-a-Mission
f-cXuzx9rk0.text: Great video as ever. You mention long term goals, e.g. achieving 3 watts/kg. Another useful goal might be to increase your watts/kg over time within a set heart rate, e.g. your zone 2 limit. I find this adds motivation to improve without feeling the need to smash yourself every time you ride. Indoor training is ideal for this.

iSrnI3IURTw.text:
i used to struggle with sleep alot. i have tried various supplements and they do work to a certain extend only. 
recently I made it mandatory to walk under the sun first thing in the morning for 15 minutes minimum. i do it barefoot and half naked. 

i also stopped drinking coffee and substituted with oolong tea. 

other protocols like zone 2 running, 15 minute sauna and meditating for 20-60mins before bed has helped me tremendously as well.

on super cloudy days and rain, i take vit d3(5000IU) + k2.

other supplements i take include omega 3, L carnitine, Benfotiamine, magnesium and L theanine. of which i only take omega 3 daily and the rest occasionally. 

if you have severe sleep problems i recommend L reuteri yogurt and magnesium glycinate + apigenin (at bed time).

Edit: disclaimer im not a medical doctor, each of us are unique- our enviornments, age, gender, genes may vary. we react to different substances differently. so please please please do your research and consult with your doctor before starting out any protocol. this is not to cover my back its to make sure you feel safe!

Y0VTTrOk7l8.text: I'm 70 and was called in for a senior's routine check-up. I'd walked a mile and a half to the surgery (to me, walking distance to anywhere is anything up to 5 miles). My BP was up according to the Almighty Guidelines. He took bloods - the results came back and my cholesterol was up. Of course I was called back in and he wanted to prescribe me both BP meds and statins. I asked him if he knew why the numbers were up - he didn't ! I told him that in that case, he was treating what he considered to be symptoms without asking what the cause was and all I got was a blank stare. I hike and wild camp, I lift weights, I follow the carnivore diet, no joint pain, no digestive issues, my sleep is great, - if it ain't broke, don't fix it. The next time I see him, it'll likely be for another routine check-up when we'll go through the same circus all over again and nope, I won't be taking BP meds or statins.

Y0VTTrOk7l8.author: @bigskywalking
Y0VTTrOk7l8.text:
I'm 70 years old. After bitterly complaining about dizziness and fatigue, my doctor took me off blood pressure medications. 
My quality of life increased dramatically. I was able to walk several miles a day and do light resistance training and do many things without suffering dizziness and fatigue.
Subsequently, I began taking 210 mg of magnesium glycinate first thing each morning and getting most of my protein for breakfast and lunch, with no snacks in between, and a small dinner, all within a 12 hour window. I started getting blood pressure readings like 127/76/68 while standing. Not perfection mind you, but far better than blood pressure readings like 143/63 while standing. 
Of course, what works for me may not work for other people, but medical protocols require some nuance to provide a good quality of life for people over 70 years of age.

Eventually I did join a gym, hired a trainer to get a better understanding of what I should be doing. Now I’m back at my little home gym, it doesn’t take much…although hip thrust sucks without a machine, and I love the alone time to focus on me. Black Currents 50 milligrams or as an extract daily 4. Wild Blueberries usually found frozen 1/3 cup before bed. Freeze and thaw to eat 1 hour before bed. 3.  Dragon fruit-red only. 1/2 red fruit. Chew seeds don’t swallow whole. 2. Gogi berries soak 2 tablespoon dried berries in warm water. Eat 1/2 hour before bed. Pairs with small square of 85% cacao. 1. Papaya 1 cup ripe cubes room temperature fresh papaya before bed. Squeeze fresh lime over papaya. No dairy or high calcium foods with fruits. Add a small amount of fat. 2-3 walnuts or other healthy fat. No scrubbing only Pat clean right before eating and eat at room temperature. Eat no earlier than 90-60 minutes before bed.     2.  Resistant starch: Present in foods like beans, quinoa, pharaoh, oats, and green bananas. Its content increases when starches are cooled after cooking (@30:32).

YZWyXhcCFDc.author: @readingsbycara
YZWyXhcCFDc.text:
💯  thank you, Dr. Corbin  😊 ❤  here's the TLDR:

This video features Dr. Karen Corbin, a PhD researcher, who discusses the gut microbiome and its critical role in metabolism, mood, and longevity. The conversation highlights how the gut is a "metabolism show" (1:46) with trillions of microbial species that actively metabolize undigested food, sending signals and hormones throughout the body.

Key takeaways from the discussion include:
The importance of fiber: Fiber is the preferred food for beneficial gut microorganisms (10:09). When these microbes are starved by consuming ultra-processed foods, they can become "hangry" (11:38) and produce inflammatory molecules (11:41), potentially leading to a "leaky gut" (12:28).
The gut-brain connection: Microbes communicate with the brain through pathways like the vagus nerve (7:37) and can even produce neurotransmitters (7:46), influencing overall well-being.
Diet can remodel your gut: Dr. Corbin shares research showing that specific dietary changes can remodel the gut microbiome, leading to a reduction in absorbed calories (0:06) and an increase in satiety hormones (13:17).
Four research-backed levers for gut health:
    1.  Dietary fiber: Found in vegetables, fruits, and whole grains (29:26).
    2.  Resistant starch: Present in foods like beans, quinoa, pharaoh, oats, and green bananas. Its content increases when starches are cooled after cooking (30:32).
    3.  Whole foods: These have larger pieces that reach the colon for microbial digestion and are less processed (30:49).
    4.  Avoiding ultra-processed foods: These are mechanically and chemically altered, which is not beneficial for the microbiome (31:12).
No standardized microbiome test: As of now, there isn't a reliable diagnostic test to confidently assess gut microbiome health or prescribe specific probiotics (22:58). Dr. Corbin advises against spending large amounts on expensive probiotics due to the current lack of scientific evidence (23:38).

The video emphasizes adopting an "abundance mindset" (38:05) by focusing on incorporating beneficial foods rather than restricting intake, highlighting that a well-fed gut microbiome can benefit various health profiles and lifestyle choices.

I am 79 going 80 years old was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes, had a knee replacement and two stents. I was advised by my good doctor that I was heading to the airport.he said put the handbrake on. Stop eating sugar and exercise, I stopped drinking alcohol cut out carbohydrates and exercise in gym classes every day except weekends..
I have lost 32 kilograms over four years and am no longer in the diabetic range, normal cholesterol, normal HbA1c, normal liver function and am fitter than I was at age 50
No bread, pasta, pastry, potatoes sweet Corn, no sugar drinks or fruit juicers.
I can vouch that a life style change not reliance on medication can help or fully reverse this cycle. I adopt a positive attitude to taking care of my blood sugars..it certainly made a huge difference in a very short time.
I Thank you for your most valuable advice on fruit, diabetes sufferers are in need of logic to assist with food choices, your dedication to your profession is greatly appreciated

DIBjXt49EYo.text: 3rd.attemt. I am 95 years old.. I know that I have been type two diabetic for at least seven years. I have maintained this diet plus or minus a bit ever since. I have increased my intake of fats.(olive oil and Ghee.) to replace the energy from carbohydrates.. I live alone, prepare my own food and do my own housekeeping. I am not on any medication’s and steadfastly refuse my cardiologist offer to put me on statins.. I have had a pacemaker for 30 years. I am reasonably fit, do a small amount of exercise daily and have a couple of projects which require both physical and mental activity..

L8c1tN4Ers4.author: @Catlover6363
L8c1tN4Ers4.text: I've been on high blood pressure meds since my twenties.  If one doctor had said start making life style changes or your blood arteries one day will turn hard like bone I would have made those changes.  Instead I figured that the medicine made me as good as healthy to continue unhealthy habits.  Then, almost 35 years later on those meds, it started to catch up with me, I felt awful and was prediabetic.  I found a fantastic nutritionist who got me exercising, food choices and portion control and no more processed foods.  I've been off blood pressure meds for 3 years now.  No longer prediabetic, lost 65 lbs, lab tests are always stellar and I FEEL FANTASTIC.  I hope I've caught this "hardening" of the arteries in time.  I had no idea.  I don't do the fasting component of this talk as much.  What I can say is eliminating processed foods and daily exercise has been a life changer for me

nmvHHmLeaUk.text: Here's the secret to a healthy, happy old age. Stop thinking about yourself. Stop thinking about troubles, especially the ones you can't do anything about. You will age, you will die. It's part of life. Listen to your body and eat whatever your body tells you to eat, as long as it's not junk food. When you feel sleepy, have a nap. When you feel tired take a break. Laugh.  Reflect on life, the universe, God. Life, our world, people they are all so mysterious. Celebrate them. Don't let fear guide your life. It will only shorten it. Love everything.

nmvHHmLeaUk.author: @tamiramos5873
nmvHHmLeaUk.text:
I'm 59 and my husband is 61. We have been taking the following vitamins over and above the meds were are prescribed for at least the  last 5 years: Vitamin D3 (actually prescribed to me), Vitamin K2 (but not combined into one pill), low dose aspirin, fish oil, and B-Complex. I also take collagen peptides. My husband takes NAD and I will be starting it as well soon. Besides, I was told that if you do not have a deficiency in your blood work, there's no need to take a supplement of it. Magnesium is one of them. I do not have any of the symptoms you mention that a low Magnesium level would present.

4 vitamins NOT to take:
--Vitamin A
--Iron
--Calcium
--Vitamin B6

4 vitamins to take:
--Magnesium
--Vitamin D3
--Vitamin K2
--Omega 3's

ZBFtMcTagD0.author: @thequiethealing
ZBFtMcTagD0.text:
I agree with his cautions about Metformin and PPIs, but I was disappointed that he didn’t explain how to actually reverse diabetic neuropathy. After looking into published medical journal studies on Alpha Lipoic Acid and Benfotiamine, I decided to try it myself — 600mg of ALA and 450mg of Benfotiamine each evening. I began noticing improvement after about 30 days, and by the 3-month mark, my neuropathy was completely gone.

It’s frustrating that more doctors aren’t aware of this. Even my own doctor didn’t believe me until I printed out one of the studies and showed it to him. (For context, I was already taking methylcobalamin and a full B-complex vitamin with methylfolate.)

LHJeL7-_64Q.author: @daveborchard2019
LHJeL7-_64Q.text:
I have slept primarily on my right side for many decades. I’ll try sleeping on my left side. I’ll also try raising my legs for 15 minutes before going to the bed. I already tried raising my legs before bedtime to possibly reduce the frequency of waking up to urinate overnight.

I added five type of nuts to my diet, walnuts, almonds, Brazil nuts, pecans, and pistachios. I noticed a significant decrease in the number of times that I had to wake up urinate in only 3 day and more improvement by the tenth day. I now wake up overnight one to zero times to urinate. A huge improvement! I feel much more rested with better quality sleeping. In my sleep, I dream a lot more now, or I remember the dreams. I’d prefer not to dream so much. I eat two meals a day, no snacking. Eating close to the carnivore diet. Close to zero sugar, seed oils, and vegetable oil. No highly processed foods.

1kzbMg6Lhm8.author: @healthfitness03
1kzbMg6Lhm8.text: As a cardiovascular health specialist, I can say this video highlights a method that is often overlooked but strongly supported by clinical research. Techniques like this can activate the nervous system in a way that rapidly lowers blood pressure, sometimes more effectively than traditional aerobic exercise when done correctly. The key is consistency and proper technique. This is a safe, practical approach that many patients can start using immediately alongside medical guidance.

1kzbMg6Lhm8.author: @WomanWellnessLab
1kzbMg6Lhm8.text: I’ve actually tried a very similar natural method, and I was surprised by how effective it was. I focused on simple daily habits like drinking warm water with natural ingredients, cutting down on salt, walking every day, and practicing slow breathing. Within a short time, my blood pressure readings became more stable, and I felt calmer and less tense. Of course, consistency is key, but natural approaches like this really can make a difference when done properly. Thanks for sharing this method—it aligns closely with what worked for me.

I took my treatment and exercised, in my next check up, they discovered I had a low sugar problem? Until I heard about the breathing system and I started doing them, I am now sitting at 112/98 and I am feeling strong. Writing from South Africa. ❤😅

I have been on a quest to lower my blood pressure for 2 years now. About a year after I retired it became high.  Spiking at times with systolic into 198/86.  Mostly the diastolic is not above 88.  That may be due to the vitamins I take. 
I will try this method of breathing.  I may start with 10 minutes and work up to 15 minutes.   I believe that we have blood pressure issues became of the toxins in our foods. I put an app called YUKA on my phone to check ingredients on everything I buy.  It is an absolute horror show of the crap in the foods at the grocery store. So it is back to basics!  No more canned biscuits with proplene glycol (yes anti freeze in that bisquet) will ever be in my grocery cart. Oh and it is in ice cream,  mouth wash etc. Anti freeze shuts down the kidneys.  Breathing and decent food are my new years resolution.  Thank you.

owyEr1znSVg.author: @Olivia-ko6mi
owyEr1znSVg.text: I take Warfarin so I have to limit vitamin K, or so I was told.  When I make a salad, I do use iceberg lettuce, but I add some broccoli, cucumber. fresh tomatoes, avocado, carrots. I am 76. I have never eaten a heavy meal late in the evening. In fact, I have gone many years not eating dinner at all! I developed varicose veins in my thirties and when I was close to fifty, I developed an open wound on my right leg. I went to a wound care dr. He did sonograms (or x-rays not sure which) saw these blood clots floating around in my legs. He decided to do a chest x-ray, and I had a blood clot(s) there. He put me in the hospital for a week, and I got shots of Warfarin in my stomach. The strange thing is, I had felt just fine. They said I would have passed away if they had traveled to my heart. As it was, I had ladies at the hospital praying for me. My mom had very bad varicose veins, which were never treated. The only option then was surgery, and my dad didn't trust doctors back then. Anyway, she lived until 81 years old. She didn't develop the additional complications I did. Sorry for this long story! LOL

Then I watched a Dr Berg video and he said, " if you have high blood pressure but all your blood work is normal GO ON A LOW CARB DIET ". and I did, within two weeks my blood pressure fell to below 120 sistolic 65 diastolic and it's been there ever since, Don't listen to your doctors, they want to keep you sick, they want to make a living off you for as long as they can, to them your just a file.

zkzkvmuw6Ik.author: @LongevityHeartHealth
zkzkvmuw6Ik.text: This video is a real game-changer for anyone struggling with high blood pressure! 🌿 I especially loved your breakdown of the differences between Magnesium Oxide and Glycinate/Taurate. Very few people realize that absorption rate is the key, not just the dosage on the label. 🔑 The insight on RBC Magnesium testing versus standard Serum is incredibly valuable—it explains why people can have 'normal' blood work yet still suffer from cramps and insomnia. Thank you, Dr. Claire, for bravely sharing these nutritional insights that clinical medicine sometimes misses due to big pharma pressure. This content is so detailed, scientific, and practical! I hope you'll release more videos on other micronutrients to keep educating the community. Respect! ❤🩺✨

zkzkvmuw6Ik.author: @StrongSeniors365
zkzkvmuw6Ik.text: I went down the same path for years—more pills, higher doses, and still dealing with leg cramps, poor sleep, and stubborn numbers. Learning about magnesium *forms* versus just “magnesium” was a real eye-opener, because I had tried oxide before and gave up. What stood out to me here is how one deficiency can quietly drive so many symptoms at once. Reading everyone’s experiences makes it clear this is something far more people should be talking about.

• According to a meta-analysis published in the American Heart Association journal Hypertension, magnesium supplementation typically results in a modest reduction of approximately 2.0 to 3.0 mmHg for diastolic and 3.0 to 4.0 mmHg for systolic blood pressure. Wonder where did this 26 mmHg drop come from?
•  While the RBC (Red Blood Cell) Magnesium test can provide a better look at long-term status, the medical community does not consider the serum test "useless." Serum levels are strictly regulated by the body; if serum magnesium is low, it indicates a severe clinical deficiency!

s3hzIqdpPkg.author: @TopSeniorsSecrets
s3hzIqdpPkg.text: Salt can actually help lower blood pressure in smaller amounts. As well as magnesium. To have enough water in your blood you need salt and magensium... Calcium being high can increase your blood pressure as well as getting dehydrated and the volume of your blood becoming low so your blood vessels tighten up to keep pressure to get blood to those organs that need it. Like a hose with a small amount of water flowing through it you can squeeze it to get a squirt. If you have a good blood volume your blood vessels can relax because they don't have to squeeze. You need adequate sodium and magensium. And potassium too. So lots of vegetables whole foods. Enough iron and copper and b 12 to have a good amount of healthy blood flowing. Excercise. Potassium and magnesium lower blood pressure more than avoiding salt. Because you need salt and you also need balance. Low blood pressure is also very dangerous if not worse than high bevause you can faint and have a stroke and all sorts of things like arythmias from low blood pressure. And dangerous over time as well. Salt alone can't function without the full range of nutrients and electrolytes nessiary. ... Things like beetroot juice and pomegranates with the seeds ...my mom lowered her blood pressure with changing her diet including beetroot and vegetable juices to help her have enough potassium and magnesium and sodium. She has  kindey disease so it's very important. She wasn't told she had it though so she didn't change in time. If you have diabetes and don't have kidney disease yet then look after your diabetes please. The kidneys going it so difficult and oermenetant damage. She reversed her diabetes type two in her seventees with diet too and off all medications.

ndLYgn-WBdg.author: @marcbowers9925
ndLYgn-WBdg.text: I’ve been on carvedilol for a little over 2 years.  I’ve been looking for the way off for 2 years.  I’m a retired doctor of 34 years in practice.  My cardiologist says, every time I mention getting off the beta blocker “those meds are keeping you alive”.  I am not depressed, no brain fog, minimal sexual symptoms but desperately been looking for the way off.  When my BP went down to 90/58 after exercising my cardiologists answering service got back to me two days later and told me to cut my carvedilol dose in half.  Duh!  That’s what I had already done.  I already do most of the things you mentioned, but some in supplement form.  I am going to try to do them all from food (in steps) substituting the real thing for supplements.  Some things that have also helped is drinking oolong tea and eating a clove of garlic. Oolong tea has twice the EGCG of green tea and garlic blocks the prostaglandins (PGE5) both preventing the LDL from becoming oxidized and attaching to the artery wall and causing atherosclerosis.

sI-C4tWYXh4.text:
For years Sooo many doctors prescribing medicine DO NOT WARN their patients about interactions between:
Drug to Drug
Drug to Vitamins
Drug to Food.
Also Drugists should be consulted by patients when picking up their medicines.
My best advice to everyone is to "Be Your Own Health Advocate." Ask questions even if you think you know because I've experienced how new knowledge about medicines you take comes to light. Remember you're dealing with Big Pharma and the FDA that approves drugs on the 'Fast Track' which simply means they approve drugs quickly without knowing all the side effects. So doing your own research should minimize your risk for bad side effects.
Best of health to all.
Dr Claire is awesome!

QwdG6sJn8AY.author: @Dede-d7x
QwdG6sJn8AY.text: Was prescribed to take magnesium for four years after having major cancer surgery.   Starting after surgery I had chronic fatigue and Everytime I tried to walk, even using a walker, I would get weak, dizzy, and light headed to the point of having to sit down immediately or else put my head down on a counter top.  Whichever was available to do.  Then, this past year I was given huge non-coated magnesium pills by the pharmacy.  I could not swallow them and I would literally choke,so decided to stop taking them.  Low and behold, I suddenly wasn't getting dizzy anymore and didn't need to put my head down.  Also, I regained a little bit of strength.  Now I buy magnesium glycinate gummies and take one a day although the normal dose is two gummies a day.  I very occasionally get dizzy and lightheaded but nothing compared to before.  Wish it hadn't taken me four years to discover what was causing the problem.

yaVM4mSY3qk.author: @cakehole53

I used to take Benadryl as over the counter medicine for hayfever. Haven't taken it for two plus years because I developed restless legs. 
I stopped taking atorvastatin 18 months ago, against my GPs advice. 
I took PPIs for short time (two weeks) to deal with health problem at the time, but no longer take it. 
Never taken benzodiazepines. 
I take perindopril 8mg for hypertension & CKD, and been taking it for over 10 years. 
I take tamsulosin after prostate cancer. 
I take tramadol 2 x 50mg up to three tines a day for Arthritic and lower back pain. I tapered off all doses until I stopped but actually found my pain WAS quite severe, so take two am and two pm. 
I have to take daily loperamide for chronic bowel issue following radiotherapy for prostate cancer. 
And finally, I take metformin for diabetes type 2. 
Your passion for polypharnacy is evident and I am very grateful for your channel and enthusiasm. 
Watching from 🇬🇧 🖐

I’m NOT betting the store on this.  It’s just as close as I can come with the devices I have. My goal is to burn 1800 calories a day.  This gives me a bit of a framework to decide what extra activities I want to add.

7wso8gyzwcM.author: @manjus3
7wso8gyzwcM.text: Regarding Chuck's question about the difference between nitrates in vegetables versus those found in deli meats (added as preservatives), the nitrates in deli meats become harmful nitrosamines in the stomach (nitrates to nitrites, which then react with the amino acids in the meat), increasing cancer risk (especially colorectal cancer) because meats lack protective antioxidants (like Vitamin C), and phytonutrients, and are often cooked at high heat, creating carcinogens. The key difference is the presence of protective plant compounds (phytonutrients) and cooking methods, not just the nitrate molecule itself. Vegetables contain antioxidants (like Vitamin C) and phytonutrients that block the formation of harmful nitrosamines, making the process beneficial.

fuCIY3nkIdg.author: @OTatime
fuCIY3nkIdg.text: I am a researcher who helped design, develop, test and market some of the most irresistibly delicious foods for McDonald’s, Frito Lay, TGI Fridays. I became obese and pre-diabetic with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. My doctor gave me three choices: gastric surgery, GLP-1 injections or dietary & lifestyle changes. These changes would also be required for the surgical approach and the injections. I opted for the diet and lifestyle changes alone. I lost 90 pounds in 18 months by following a Mediterranean diet with emphasis on high quality protein. All of my blood and lipid markers are now within the reference ranges. I feel immeasurably guilty and remorseful for the foods I developed that have made our society less healthy. I encourage everyone to follow the advice in this video. Eat minimally processed foods…mostly low carb fruits, vegetables and more eggs, chicken and seafood — mostly fatty fish that are low in mercury. I am so sorry.

But at 40 I did start gaining some and by 54 I was the heaviest ever at 210 pounds but I am 6 foot 2 inches so not bad. But I was feeling it and my running was starting to suffer. I went cold turkey and cut out sugar, processed foods, my only vice is a couple beers each day,. It didnt happen overnight and probably took close to a year to see real results. I think that is why people dont keep up with proper diet, they dont see results in 1 week. I am feeling very good at 175 lbs now. You cant wait.

84SH0dQdBLA.author: @Cholita.1979
84SH0dQdBLA.text: I was diagnosed with RA almost 2 years ago. I went to a naturopathic doctor and after some blood and stool tests they found I had leaky gut, c diff, h pylori, a parasite, SIBO. I also have mycoplasma pneumonia and EBV. Fast forward, I haven’t have flares for over 2 months, my body doesn’t hurt, I can go down the stairs normal. some of my fingers are deformed and I have joint damage and pain left from RA otherwise I can function like a normal person. I am grateful every day! The rheumatologist told me there was nothing I can do about it and it will just keep progressing and that I had to take meds to slow it down. Thankfully that was not the case. Of course I had to do everything on my end to help my body heal. I had to be carnivore for 18months, prioritize sleep and stress, strength training, sauna, I also get adjustments of my nervous system bc I was constantly on a fight or flight state. You name it. I did almost everything I could while working with a doctor who knows what he’s doing. There’s hope but it takes work. ❤

84SH0dQdBLA.author: @colleenjacobson8089
84SH0dQdBLA.text: I had to deal with stored trauma before I could deal with my autoimmune issues and Epstein-Barr virus which I had for years. The way I dealt with it was through a Christian counselor. She lead me to recall the issue, picture Jesus in the moment holding my hand through it (which was the actual truth as he cares for me) and then renouncing/correcting the lie I believed during and after the trauma (such as Jesus abandoned me). I then asked the Lord to heal my emotional wounds. After this my body could start to heal as I cared for it physically. Trauma, stress, unforgiveness and emotional pain leave an open door. Couple this with diet, lifestyle, nutritional and environmental issues and your immune system will misfire and suffer.

Diet is huge but people choose convenience and garbage over real, whole foods. They choose convenience like delivery or take out over real whole food and cooking at home.

We have to be active participants in our health. We have to be inconvenienced sometimes in order to make things right.
We are ultimately in control for advocating for our bodies and health.

1hKkSVXfb_s.author: @BudKash
1hKkSVXfb_s.text:
Hold on a second, Doc. I was under the impression that, although K2 is essential for D3 functionality, when taken together (as in a compound pill) they tend to conflict, because they compete for receptors. Have you heard this, and what is your opinion? I thought it's best to take D with Magnesium, but in terms of other co-factors, Zinc should not be taken simultaneously with Mag either, for instance.
You mentioned "mega-dosing", and this idea of doctors putting a patient on 50k iu daily for a brief period. Is it not true that D3 need to build up to the recommended blood levels, and to expedite this process it helps to take larger doses, especially if it was reduced to a maintenance dose thereafter? Some companies also market high-dose vit D, one brand specifically packages 50k iu caps, but as a weekly dosage, which seems reasonable if it takes time to accumulate and distribute. USRDA's are a bad joke.
All that being said, there are other important co-factors as well, especially for higher dosages of D3 (which your 10k iu daily could be considered) such as zinc, boron, vit A (beta carotene), and preferably a good B-complex also (like a methylated compound).
Thank you for an excellent, informative video.

Never have to leave the house. When the weather is nicer I sometimes walk around the corner and run hill sprints on a quiet street. Go for walks or walk inside the house on other days. Play ultimate frisbee with co-workers during the spring/summer/fall. Sometimes go for a bike ride. PC is in the loft, kitchen is downstairs, so I'm constantly going up and down stairs. No gym membership necessary.

X63SYOT1uC0.author: @liamjeffery4565
X63SYOT1uC0.text:
Good episode overall. You did well pressing Gardner on the protein recommendations, but I wish you had corrected or asked for more clarification from Ty throughout this. It felt like a few statements that misalign with the broader consensus on US public health slid by a bit too easily.

Ty brought up some great nuance that people actually need to enjoy the food they eat for a diet to be sustainable, and that incorporating some animal foods can be better than the ultra-processed junk a lot of people might eat instead. It's also fair to point out that nutrient deficiencies are an issue for certain demographics even in the US.

But he kept bringing up global data on severe undernutrition to justify higher animal product intake. That sometimes makes sense globally, but when you're talking about US guidelines where the main public health crises are calorie overconsumption, cardiovascular disease, and a massive lack of fiber it really muddies the waters. Optimizing a diet for a malnourished population is just fundamentally different than optimizing for the average American.

The talk around saturated fat and full-fat dairy too. Glossing over the established link between the saturated fat in full-fat dairy and increased ApoB leaves people with a dangerously simplified takeaway. It's frustrating to hear him use an n=1 personal anecdote about swapping eggs for oatmeal to cast doubt on the massive body of evidence showing the cardiovascular benefits of whole grains over foods high in saturated fat and cholesterol.

Also, giving that much grace to the anti-seed oil narrative without anchoring it back to the reality that replacing saturated fats with polyunsaturated fats consistently improves outcomes felt like a missed opportunity.

Same goes for the protein discussion. Pushing more protein for satiety and weight loss sounds great in clinical theory, but human behavior is messy. In practice, telling the general US population to eat more protein usually just translates to them eating more high-saturated-fat meats, rather than the fiber-rich plant proteins the population actually needs.

KDud2u4mQw0.text: Quick note: The protein targets discussed in this video are intended for healthy adults, especially men over 50 who want to maintain muscle. Muscle loss accelerates with age, and adequate protein intake combined with strength training is one of the most effective ways to slow that process. Always consult your doctor if you have kidney disease or other medical conditions.

Dinner - meat, fish, or poutry (40 g), veggies (5 g) = 45 g.  Snack - almonds or pistachios - 10 g.  Total = 40 + 45 + 45 + 10 = 140 g.  I weigh ~175 and would like to get down to ~165.  I've found trying to lose fat while increaseing muscle is difficult.  So I'm now planning on limiting workouts (from 6 to either 3 or 4 per days per week) and increasing walking/stationary bike. -NiYfdoqN2g.author: @user-je1sf6pc6q
-NiYfdoqN2g.text: For Kidney function (EGFR) blood test ask for “Cistatin C” as the marker measured vs Creatinine. This will give a more accurate estimate.

-NiYfdoqN2g.author: @sampsondiane
-NiYfdoqN2g.text: I tried creatine supplements on 3 separate occasions over a 2-year period.  In all cases I noted improved performance in the gym, better muscle definition, etc.  But I also noted two distinct side effects - increased anxiety and decreased libido.  The anxiety issues disappeared and libido returned several weeks after discontinuing the creatine supplementation.  I haven't found much related documentation in the literature, but I did stumble upon a Reddit stream in which quite a few people had similar side effects.

I told them I had done a "sprint" triathlon and was likely a normal response to have a bump in CK.

kBQhrlP33e8.author: @ryankennedy1327
kBQhrlP33e8.text: I always think of, what if gyms didn’t exist, all these fancy machines weren’t a thing. Would as many people be as into fitness as they are now? Would they allow creativity to drive their training and put the body into areas not yet discovered. I catch myself falling into a bubble of things the more I read about training philosophies and what not, but then I remember what actually got me intrigued with lifting. Got me an atlas stone sandbag and some rings, tractor tire/sledgehammer, warmer weathers here and now I’ll experiment with some different. The “basics” are still there but with added excitement, because at the end of the day, if you hate what you’re doing you’ll never enjoy this journey long term

YQagUsX6mcU.author: @jimpatek6244
YQagUsX6mcU.text: I appreciated this podcast and Simon. Nailing Alan and Brad down with specific questions designed to answer the things people really want to know was the most appreciated, not to mention Brad's and Alan's time. The one thing that troubles me and it was reinforced by your questions to Brad is the idea that you need to train ideally three days per week. I am 80 now and I train hard with five compound lifts (The Big 5) and with isolation exercises for the upper and lower body. This takes about 35 minutes. Slow reps (3-2-5), time under load 1 to 2 minutes (legs), one set. All to one rep short of failure hopefully. After I have done this, quite frankly, I am feeling it,  I have determined that recovery takes me 7-8 days. It is the aging body I guess  combined with the intensity. The results have spoken for themselves so I keep doing what I am doing. The point of all of this is that recovery was not really addressed, particularly in the aging population unless I missed it. No way would I attempt my specific protocol 3 times a week! And it is not necessary anyway.

I watch my 9-yr. old granddaughter weekdays after school for about 90-minutes.
We each either walk on treadmill for a mile or jump on rebounder.
Then we alternate between upper body exercises one day then lower the next.
We use flex bands and kettle bells .
I am hoping my example will instill this routine throughout her life.
She also knows about *fake* food, sugar, etc., which I do not have much control over, but I'm planting seeds.

j5jAm023ewQ.text: This is by far the best advice i have received in many years, i cut down my training to a push pull leg 3 times per week, sometimes i take 48h between workout and hit every muscles once per week. You need to work hard, i keep 2 reps in the tank and protect your back and knees a good training belt, knee wraps, etc. No ego lifting

When i workout i don’t go to fatigue, i leave two in the tank and give plenty of time to recover my connective tissue, nervous system and muscle. No overtraining, no fatigue, no pain. I do 8-12 reps w 4 sets each w one second concentric w a 3-4 second eccentric. I love the growth and the way things have been going w progress. Love your videos and appreciate what you do for us

Li1ZSuQDXT8.author: @goldenagecare-y8e

The explanation is clear, physiology-based, calm, and free of fear tactics or false promises. It truly feels like someone is finally explaining what so many patients experience but are never told.

Li1ZSuQDXT8.author: @seniorhealthsteps
Li1ZSuQDXT8.text: As a physical therapist, I find your WTR protocol for cellular hydration absolutely revolutionary for metabolic health. Thank you for this evidence-based guidance!

Li1ZSuQDXT8.text:
Dear Dr. Megan Foster! Thank you so much for sharing such invaluable insights. This video helped me realize a major 'missing link' I’ve had for a long time: I was so focused on strict dieting and medication that I completely overlooked the most fundamental foundation—cellular hydration.

The way you explained drinking water at the right temperature, during the 4 'golden' windows, and adding minerals to 'unlock' cells is truly a game-changer. It’s not just dry medical advice; it’s deep, compassionate understanding for seniors. I’ve started implementing the WTR protocol today, beginning with that first glass of water right after waking up. I hope you continue creating such heartfelt content to help us regain confidence in managing our health. Wishing you great health and energy! Approved!

5-6W9zGOgIs.text:
Short Summary:

REMOVE
1. Artificial Sweeteners
2. Ultra Processed Foods
3. Seed Oils
4. Chronic Under-eating
5. Eating in a stressed state

RESTORE
1. Repair the gut lining
- Collagen & gelatin rich foods (Bone broth, slow-cooked meats, oxtail, shanks, connective tissue...)
2. Reintroduce beneficial bacteria (Sauerkraut, Kefir, Yogurt, Kimchi)
3. Support digestion
- proper chewing
- warm foods over cold
- (Apple cider vinegar)

REGULATION
1. Consistent meal timing
- Regular eating windows
- No constant grazing

2. Parasympathetic dominance
- Sit down to eat
- No phone

5-6W9zGOgIs.author: @guthealer1
5-6W9zGOgIs.text:
Lucas hit the nail on the head at 8:27 about how eating in a stressed state completely shuts down digestion. That was a massive lightbulb moment for me.

However, for anyone watching who has severe IBS or a highly damaged gut, be very careful with the raw arugula/salad advice at 12:12! When my gut was at its absolute worst, eating raw greens to 'kickstart' digestion literally acted like sandpaper on my inflamed stomach and made me look 6 months pregnant instantly.

Before I could tolerate raw greens or salads again, I had to apply 'Mechanical Rest' to my meals. I temporarily stopped all raw veggies and cooked everything down to absolute mush or purees. By taking the physical breakdown work away from my stomach, my gut finally had the mechanical peace it needed to heal and actually absorb things like bone broth. You have to remove the physical friction before your gut can handle raw greens! Spot on video overall, especially the stress management.

stMa_kOedwU.author: @explorelivediscover6157
stMa_kOedwU.text:
People don't realise how much stress is fffing us up, I was living in a very high stress state for more than 30 years of my life and I never wanted to realise and admit it because I wanted to be tough, the biggest factor is how you treat yourself, though and how you talk to yourself and think about yourself.

If you might have a lot of stress but ''process it'' differently than someone else with the same situation, and it's not about so called mental toughness, it's much more mental flexibility and adaptability, tough people eventually break, flexible people are antifragile.

You can multiply stress by orders of magnitude by talking to yourself poorly and thinking poorly of yourself, the real strength doesn't come from being emotionally shut and appearing tough, it's about being the only one treating yourself with respect and love when the whole world shits on you, trust me, when in that situation, you also turn against yourself, and if that happens for long enough, you will get into some serious trouble mentally and physically.

h0TcxwAyk40.author: @anthonywilson1754
h0TcxwAyk40.text: I'm age 24 and did gymnastics for 14 years from age 4. In Ukrainian national training we trained our bodies hard most days of the week. From maybe age 7 or 8 I trained my abdominals maybe daily and sometimes more than 1 time a day. We had strength training coach and he wanted us all to have extremely strong core muscles. I don't know where exercise addiction fits in your story but I saw some gymnasts like me had exercise addiction. I was raised in the orphanage system and boys homes. Life for me was stressful--as you say about internal stresses. I used abdominal exercising to cope with stress. When adopted and raised in America at age 12 I had a lot more stresses--using English all the time was tiring. I was homeschooled to age 15 then put into high school. Life in high school was tough and in rural schools the kids didn't know what to do with a boy in gymnastics. When I was almost age 16 some older athletes found me alone and decided to have fun beating me up. They held me in a full nelson and punched the shit out of my abdominals. They saw I had very muscular abdominals but didn't know that it turns out I can take a lot of hard punches. I was lucky I trained hard in Ukraine and kept going. I didn't handle stress well until maybe age 20. I now work in Construction full time and still keep training hard but not too insane. I lift weights 4 days a week and train abdominals in some serious way 5-6 days a week. My stress has gone down a lot. I never ran growing up but have been doing more running. I'm still not loving it but I don't hate it. Life is a lot better and feel I have the results I like. I'm 5'9" and weigh 178 pounds. I have less than 10% body fat which I think is my phenotype. And also gymnast pride in me has me wanting deeply muscled abdominals with no body fat. But that's not hard to do as I don't eat crazy amounts of calories.

myUgx-eM7Ug.author: @JanaGerken
myUgx-eM7Ug.text: The guest seems to conflate compounded hormones with bioidentical hormones. Bioidentical hormones simply have the same chemical structure as those made by your own body - they can be both FDA approved (and picked up at a CVS etc) or be compounded. And some compounded meds may be made up of synthetic or animal based compounds. Great interview - just a quick clarification. Thanks!

I have friends that don’t believe everything that I’ve told him and now you said it all in this video. I’m type two diabetic and I keep it under control.   
My numbers are usually between 95 and 105 when I get my doctor recommended labs  and my A1c is always under six. I eat eat potatoes I eat rice i eat everything the only thing that I changed when knowing about refrigeration, 24 hours, I did not know about but timing when to eat it everything I tell all my friends that are diabetics, including my brother that you can have starches you can have potatoes you can have all that just make sure you eat them before 2pm and make sure to leave the skin on them and eat them with protein and vegetables.  what I’m telling them. My blood tests that the doctor does periodically. My numbers are 95 to 105 and my A1c is always under six. I’ve been doing this my entire life. It works. I just have to remember to use the vinegar more often like they do in Europe on potatoes.

byLvqBkhOyU.author: @shiftHERinc
byLvqBkhOyU.text:
Great podcast, Gary Brecka. Dr. Shepherd was a great guest.

What stood out most is how clearly the connection was made between women’s health and the realities many don’t always name… access, equity and the lived experiences of Black and brown women within the medical system. When we talk about menopause, longevity and hormone health, we can’t separate the science from the systems people are navigating to receive care.

There was also a powerful throughline here: when we begin to center those with the least access to resources, the standard of care rises for everyone.
That’s how real change happens. And conversations like this don’t just inform… they expand who feels seen, who feels included, and who feels empowered to seek answers. This is how the narrative shifts. Midlife isn’t a pause. It’s a shift.

fcZCWm3Z0G8.text:
The video mentions 5 types of fruit:
1. Avocado: Provides magnesium along with healthy monounsaturated fats that support nutrient absorption.
2. Jackfruit: Contains prebiotics that may help support gut health and improve mineral absorption.
3. Guava: Rich in vitamin C and organic acids that can support overall nutrient uptake.
4. Passion Fruit: High in fiber, pectin, and vitamin C, helping create a healthy digestive environment.
5. Figs: A good source of dietary fiber, which supports digestion and nutrient absorption.

fcZCWm3Z0G8.author: @SeniorHealth2
fcZCWm3Z0G8.text:
It turns out our bodies absorb magnesium much more effectively when it's packaged with natural fibers and healthy fats. 
Here are the Top 5 "Golden Fruits" ranked in the video:
1 Avocado: The "Trojan Horse" that delivers magnesium directly into your cells thanks to its healthy monounsaturated fats.
2 Jackfruit: Contains prebiotics that boost gut acidity, increasing absorption by 33% (don't forget to eat the seeds!).
3 Guava: Rich in citric and malic acids, which create the perfect pH to dissolve magnesium for better uptake.
4 Passion Fruit: Loaded with Pectin and Vitamin C to optimize the digestive environment.
5 Figs: High in dietary fiber, allowing for 25-30% more efficient absorption than standard pills.

P9zgIL6Xdjw.author: @jeffrey322
P9zgIL6Xdjw.text:
The 7 Practices:

Stop Eating 2-3 Hours Before Bed: Digestion keeps your core temperature elevated and cortisol high. Early dinner (6-7 PM) allows your body to finish processing food, drop temperature, and signal your brain it's time to sleep.

Take a Hot Bath (The Ofuro): Not because it relaxes you—but because when you get out, your core temperature drops rapidly. That temperature drop triggers melatonin release. Get out 60-90 minutes before bed for peak effect.

Dim the Lights: Your brain uses light to decide what time it is. Bright lights = cortisol stays high, melatonin delayed. Dim lights 2 hours before bed = melatonin rises naturally, cortisol drops, sleep mode activates.

The Digital Sunset: No screens 60 minutes before bed. Netflix isn't winding down—it's stimulation. Every cliffhanger, notification, and scroll keeps your nervous system in stress mode. Screen-free evenings reset your circadian rhythm.

The Yoru no Sanpo (Evening Walk): A 10-20 minute gentle walk after dinner helps digestion and naturally lowers cortisol. Not exercise—just gentle movement, cool air, and transition from day to night.

Reading Time (Yomu Jikan): Physical books only. Reading transfers your focus from your own anxious thoughts to someone else's world. 6 minutes of reading reduces stress by 68%—more than music, walking, or tea.

Gratitude Practice (Kansha): Before bed, reflect on three small things that went well. Gratitude activates your parasympathetic nervous system, releases oxytocin and serotonin, and drops cortisol. Not forced positivity—just shifting attention from problems to gifts.

Why This Works:

Every practice targets a different type of cortisol. You're not forcing sleep—you're removing what blocks it.

Early dinner = drops digestive cortisol
Hot bath = drops thermal cortisol
Dim lights = drops visual cortisol
Digital sunset = drops stimulation cortisol
Evening walk = drops physical cortisol
Reading = drops mental cortisol
Gratitude = drops emotional cortisol

Sleep isn't something you do. It's something you allow. When cortisol drops, sleep comes naturally. Every time.

The Bottom Line:

You don't have bad sleep because something is wrong with you. You have bad sleep because your evenings are designed to keep you awake.

Bright lights. Stimulating screens. Late meals. No transition. No stillness.

But when you give your body the signals it needs, everything changes.

You don't need all seven practices at once. Pick one. Start tonight. Let it become part of your rhythm.

Because sleep isn't a reward for being tired enough. It's a result of being safe enough.

And when your body feels safe, it rests deeply.

###

Dr. Brewer has been talking about CIMT for a while, long before it was FDA approved, and he explains the lifestyle choices that help prevent "sticky arteries." I feel like this sudden bombardment of cardiology industry trained professionals in social media is just industry "damage control." 

I watch my grandfather do what he was told for 30 years and he was waiting to die for his last 30 years of his life. 

Most of what I heard during this video, was not only should you "trust me bro, to manage your heart health," and you should also send us your kids, so we can manage their entire life of healthcare as well.

I didn't hear much in the way of self care, outside of "don't use tobacco products." We all knew that already.

So a year ago I cut Crestor 20mg a day to 10mg, then 10mg a day to every other day, now twice a week. No difference. I did go low carb & back to gym. My triglycerides dropped 40%, but nothing else except slightly higher HDL, still LDL 120-130 & ApoB steady at 104. Had a CAC score of 52 in LAD 4yr ago (others 0). Such a quandry as far as what to do as I'm afraid lowering LDL to 30 with Repatha is too low. I've heard quotes for cardiac CT with AI is more like $2500+, not $1000, and I'm on Medicare now.

aTCmZEmWILA.author: @michellemartinov6267
aTCmZEmWILA.text:
Started with weight gain/carb craving at age 61 after being on keto for years. Assumed that menopause finally hit (yes this was a reasonable assumption), went on ozempic for 8 months, lost weight and have maintained ever since and then with the weight loss I was off my BP meds. Then at age 61 my BP started going out of control. I developed constipation despite a very healthy diet. I added more fiber. Eventually I added various teas and 1.5 years later I’m stable on cascara. Then BP meds required even more. Then BP drop and then elevate. I was developing mental fog and fatigue. I was developing sinus infections 4 x a year then ended up needing surgery. My children thought I was crazy from sepsis from sinusitis. Then I was developing neuropathy hands, feet, twitching, loss of balance. Sleeping 16 hours a day. And of course I figured oral supplements would fix it once I figured out what was wrong! (Retired nurse 35 years but brain fog delayed my diagnosis!)And of course the actual blood levels are inaccurate because of oral b12! Fortunately my pcp is starting me on b12 IM supplementation which I understand will be for life. 
Yes I’ve also burning on tongue with loss of taste. And apparently as we age, we lose the ability to absorb b12. So you WON’T HAVE SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA OR JAUNDICE IF YOU ARE AN OLDER ADULT!

oci4-iAQpnw.text: heya mark, have you ever thought about doing therapy? I was a lot like you, where I put tons of energy and time into developing systems to keep me on the rails, cuz once I got off, it was a decent into binge eating and poor health. Maintaining that is fucking exhausting. I started seeing a therapist who is a certified eating disorder specialist, and I can honestly say that after a couple years, I'm in a far far better place and able to do far far more than I ever would have had all those systems I build worked exactly as I thought they would and I managed to never fell off those rails. There's also not the crushing mental overheard of maintaining all of that, and that feeling that I can't trust my body and I'm one misstep away from having it gleefully take me down the path it wants to travel and I desperately don't. Me and my body are actually buddies now, and I trust him. it's a lot nicer this way. Anyway, I just felt like I had to share that, because listening to you is like listening to my old self, and I want to help that guy, because I love him and want the best for him. Wish you the best of luck on your journey and thanks for all the great videos you've entertained and inspired me with over the years. Cheers mate.

EGunlU-RN-Q.author: @joematt98
EGunlU-RN-Q.text:
Key Takeaways:

•	Brewer's Yeast Benefits: It is rich in B complex vitamins (especially Biotin/B7, B1, B2, B3, B5, and B6) and minerals like zinc and selenium, which are essential for hair health (1:38-2:05).
•	How it Works: Hair cells in the bulb divide rapidly and need high energy. B vitamins act as co-factors to transform nutrients into energy (ATP), preventing thinner, fragile hair (2:20-3:08).
•	Types of Hair Loss: This supplement specifically helps with diffuse hair loss—a general thinning or shedding seen in showers, brushes, or pillows—rather than localized bald spots (3:45-3:57).
•	Other Important Supplements:
•	Iron: Crucial for oxygenating the hair bulb, especially for women or those with restrictive diets (6:50-7:30).
•	Zinc: Vital for cell division in the follicle, protein synthesis, and hormonal regulation (8:40-9:05).
•	Warning: Do not self-prescribe. Consult a dermatologist or nutritionist to determine proper dosages based on your specific needs (9:45-10:06).

lz-A_4s0ODQ.text:
INGREDIENTS, RECIPES AND TIPS BELOW. This milk flan is a lighter, more practical alternative for anyone wanting a creamy, high-protein, sugar-free dessert. It’s no-bake, egg-free, and still maintains that firm, soft texture of traditional flan, but with much less fat and more protein per serving. It is an excellent option for those looking to control calories, reduce sugar, or simply have a more balanced daily dessert. It’s consistent, perfectly sweet, and very easy to prepare. Let me know in the comments if you’re going to make it exactly like this or if you’ll adapt any ingredients. And check out the related videos I’ve selected below to help you.

❤  Be part of our community! Join my channel membership to get exclusive perks and support my work! Just click on the link below! https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCUU-glcsz57qVD8_xhriglg/join

🔥 Visit my other CHANNEL with HEALTH AND NUTRITION TIPS:
Weight loss, Diabetes, Cholesterol, Gut, Bones, Liver, Heart and more!
https://www.youtube.com/@PatriciaHealthandNutrition

📋 INGREDIENTS:
- 14 oz / 400g nonfat plain yogurt (1 2/3 cups) (https://youtu.be/ngyufhP9Cfs)
- 6.5 oz / 180g nonfat dry milk powder (2 1/4 cups) 
- 8 fl oz / 240mL nonfat milk (1 cup)
- 5 tablespoons water
- 0.4 oz / 12g unflavored gelatin (1 packet) (https://youtu.be/f1a3z5YgymE)
-- Sweetener to taste

Yields 10 servings, each with approximately 101 calories, 12g of carbohydrates, 12g of protein, and 0g of fat.

p9hk5h_2rhY.author: @coolcpa3321
p9hk5h_2rhY.text: I've made Greek yogurt bread with nearly the same recipe. I add seasonings and achieve breads with an Italian or Greek flavor profile and then mix-in sliced Kalamata and/or green Castelvetrano olives. You can also easily tweak into a "treat" by adding flavor to the batter and yummy mix-ins. I add lemon flavor with wild blueberries, orange flavor with cranberries or cinnamon with chopped apples and walnuts. You can also start with flavored yogurts such as vanilla. I've topped bread with poppy, flax or sesame seeds as well as chopped nuts, rolled oats and cacao nibs. Variety is the spice of life!

UwV7QXslGVk.author: @bravehome4276
UwV7QXslGVk.text:
Just to clarify:
A restart is not the same as a shutdown and start. Here are the key differences and steps to understand their behavior:

Restart

Closes all programs and processes, including the Windows kernel (core of the operating system).

Performs a cold boot, ensuring a clean start for the system.

Recommended for: Installing updates or software. Resolving system errors or driver issues. Applying group policies.

Shutdown and Start

With Fast Startup enabled (default in Windows 8, 10, and 11), shutdown saves the Windows kernel to disk (a hybrid hibernation state).

On starting, the kernel is reloaded, making the boot process faster but not fully resetting the system.

Recommended for: Turning off the computer for extended periods. Saving power when not in use.

UwV7QXslGVk.author: @AggyThomas
UwV7QXslGVk.text: I think of it as system hygiene, whether computer or phone.  Reboot every morning

UwV7QXslGVk.author: @freon_bale
UwV7QXslGVk.text: There is another option. Hibernate mode, which all Windows machines are capable of. It works like Sleep Mode, except that instead of keeping RAM powered, and everything else off, it copies everything in RAM to the hard drive, and then turns everything off. You can leave a computer in Hibernate literally forever, and it will not deplete the battery at all. The downside? When it turns back on, it must copy everything from the hard drive back into RAM, which may take a second or two. With today's modern NVMe drives, it's barely noticeable. Does it hurt your computer? No, that's ridiculous. Does it cause your system to be less stable if used over and over, debatable. I would reboot a Windows PC at least once every 3-4 weeks. With heavy use, that's about Windows' limit in terms of performance degradation, though it would still be stable.

KpcMfr5ZYa4.author: @neilgooge
KpcMfr5ZYa4.text:
I've said this in a previous video, but at 55 this series has finally got me in to the gym.

I have never been over weight, have always walked a considerable amount, I don't drink extensively, never smoked, and my diet has always been... okay, not amazing, but I am not a fast food or take out regularly kind of guy. All these things combined have definitely helped.  But... I could feel my strength fading, certainly in the last two to three years. Not to mentioning the increasingly stiffening joints.

And then late last year this series came along and gave me the push I needed.

What I find most incredible is... if you stood me next to your dad, let alone your mum, most people would instantly assume I could lift more... In some exercises, your mum can lift more than me, and in all cases your dad can lift more... by quite a lot in fact :D

I am genuinely excited to see where I will be a year from now, and this has truly opened my eyes to whats possible.

Thanks so so much for sharing.

Our age group has probably tried it

knJG_2Am7EY.author: @wfqsfg
knJG_2Am7EY.text:
I am 69 also. Will turn 70 in a few months. I started back working out after about 10-12 years of being a retired couch potato. I was quite active in my early years. Jogging, weightlifting, pickup basketball and other sports. 
I started back about 8 months ago and had plenty of muscle and joint soreness to start. Most of that is gone now. I still get muscle soreness when I pump up the volume. But I am experienced and I know when I tear down muscle I will have soreness. I walk now instead of jogging and lift weights. I will ride my bicycle when winter is over. I cleaned up my eating and try real hard to get the protein in that I need, especially when weightlifting. I've lost 30lbs since starting back with 20 more to go to reach my goal. I have plateaued and have to figure out how to get past that. 
Reading the other comments I would like to say I supplement fiber. I wouldn't want to be without it. I try to get enough in with meals but that doesn't always happen. 
I would like to tell people my own age,  just walk every day!! If you don't want to do anything else, at least walk!! Watch videos on YouTube on some tips but walking is something most can do.

knJG_2Am7EY.author: @archaeobard1

I'm trying to get my cousin's wife (who is 61) to eat like this as she is having problems with falling and her hips - she needs to start the process for hip replacement. She broke her hip when she was 13 and it's starting to cause her serious issues, but she's basically said she is getting old and this stuff just happens. For a start, 61 is nowhere near old! She's staggering around like she is 90, stooped over, and grabbing hold of things. She's rapidly losing muscle. I think I've convinced her to at least go and see her dr, but she is lacking 'the willingness', as you say, to help herself.

MYDUebYUiM0.author: @Gringabeanpole
MYDUebYUiM0.text:
Lift with Cee is my go to. I’m a 25 year Pilates Instructor and once I retired at 60, I wanted a new challenge without giving up my Pilates routine. I had several people say, why would you change workouts, you’re in great shape.
But, I now feel, after a year with Cee, is both Pilates and Lifting should be in everyone’s routine because they hit muscles in different ways.
Cee’s 3 workouts a week of just 30 minutes fit nicely in my week.
Best thing about Lift with Cee. She doesn’t jibber jabber. That makes me nuts when instructors do that.
It’s also very easy to follow her form. I never had a problem with feeling like I was doing it wrong and may injure myself. I don’t think she’s targeted to beginners though. You probably should have lifted weights at least some or have been in the workout world.

MYDUebYUiM0.author: @colettesimms9706
MYDUebYUiM0.text:
I am a “Workout with Cee aka Cheryl Coulombe” ❤ long timer here. I’ve worked out with Cee for 3 years and I’m turning 69 tomorrow!!! I’m stronger, in the best shape of my life, and still constantly challenged every day. Her information and coach training is spot on and she emphasizes dialing in your nutrition for fat loss and muscle building. This is very important to me since I have Osteopenia and am actively trying to improve my bone density naturally.

I really liked your assessment of other trainers but feel it’s also important to rate people that support both, a well rounded workout AND nutrition.🤩🌟💖

SU1YP2ijAwQ.author: @shazzy7311
SU1YP2ijAwQ.text:
I’ve been struggling with perimenopause which caused my nervous system to be hyper-sensitive. Cortisol raging!  I had tried to CUT sugar and realized all it did was make my liver dump mounds of it in my system because it was listening to my cortisol.  My first step is calming my body and allowing sugars back in, but I’m doing that through small amounts of honey in the morning and my new favourite mid morning snack at work is a date with real peanut butter!! SO GOOD!! 
I’ve been gaining weight the past 3 months, but I’m allowing it to be part of the strategy since I think my body is settling and now swinging into protective mode.  I’m starting super light weights because even a bit of exercise seems to absolutely exhaust my muscles.  And I have tried very slight rebounding after breakfast (like 50 small easy jumps).  
I’m trying not to freak out about the weight and to love my body for being protective… but also desperately want to get back to where I feel like I have a better rhythm.
Thank you for your grounded solid channel. I look forward to more. This has been a hard but enlightening three years for me!

anX_DxVQDQ0.author: @krunyanmd
anX_DxVQDQ0.text: It is often stated that metformin inhibits mitochondrial ETC complex I, however in doses used in humans (2,000 to 2,550 mg/day) this is not the case according to this article "Cellular and Molecular Mechanisms of Metformin Action," by Traci E. LaMoia and Gerald I. Shulman which points out that 1. Metformin inhibition of complex I is only observed at millimolar concentrations that are not therapeutically relevant, 2. Metformin inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis both in vitro and in vivo in a redox-dependent manner, 3. Increased cytosolic redox state, due to metformin inhibition of glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase, is observed at clinically relevant concentrations, and this is the only proposed mechanism of action that predicts substrate-selective (glycerol and lactate) inhibition of hepatic gluconeogenesis.

85qNN2BzsEY.author: @LisaS-ul6vx
85qNN2BzsEY.text: One of the questions asked was about the use of hypochlorous acid (HOCl) to clean vibrators. HOCl at skin and surface concentrations is very gentle. It is naturally produced by the white blood cells. It has multiple medical applications including intraoperative irrigation, wound debridement, preoperative prep, and post-operative care. It is my go-to household disinifectant, including piercing aftercare and cleaning my CPAP equipment. I keep a small spray bottle in my purse to use as a hand sanitizer as it also kills norovirus unlike alcohol-based ones. It is fine for cleaning silicone, glass, etc., although you would probably want to avoid it for aluminum or calcium-based stone surfaces.

PAd6VZM7qFc.author: @r-l-h7p
PAd6VZM7qFc.text: I suggest everyone actually looks up and reads the studies based on which many of these "brain-boosting" claims are based on. For instance, one of the most-cited trials on the effect of creatine on the brain by Rae et al., 2003 was in vegetarians who may start with lower creatine, and even then it’s mainly working-memory test scores and not “IQ went up and life changed.” Other meta-analyses generally find small average effects on memory and often no clear improvement in overall cognition. Having proper sleep etiquette and quality, meditating for 15-20 mins a day, and not eating processed (junk) food will already do wonders for the majority of people suffering from a mushy brain..

PAd6VZM7qFc.author: @paragsakhare6641
PAd6VZM7qFc.text:
Some alerts :
1. A combination of Alpha GPC and Citicoline (CDP-Choline) leads to acetylcholine dominance or overload. This can cause severe "choline headaches," brain fog, muscle tension (especially jaw clenching), lethargy, sudden depressive moods, and gastrointestinal distress.
2. A combination of Ginkgo Biloba, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Bacopa monnieri is fantastic for getting oxygen to the brain. Combining these increases your risk of bruising or excessive bleeding if you get a cut, and they should be completely avoided before any surgery.
3. What Works Well Together :
i. Foundation (Creatine + Omega-3)
ii.Stress Shield (L-Theanine + Bacopa + Phosphatidylserine)
iii. Neuroplasticity Engine (Magnesium L-Threonate)
main part: Hydration is non-negotiable........

PAd6VZM7qFc.author: @_Keith_
PAd6VZM7qFc.text:
Creatine (0:00-0:52): Acts as a backup battery for quick brain energy, supporting focus, memory, and clear thinking. It's especially beneficial for those who consume less meat, as meat is a primary source. Creatine monohydrate is the most effective form.

Omega-3 Fatty Acids (0:55-1:56): These fats, particularly DHA and EPA, keep brain cells flexible for smooth signal transmission and help control inflammation, promoting calmness and balanced mood. Low levels can lead to poor focus, mood swings, and impaired learning and memory. Found in fatty fish, they must be obtained through diet or supplements.

Alpha GPC (1:59-2:50): A fast-acting form of choline that helps the brain produce acetylcholine, a chemical crucial for focus and quick thinking. It can improve clarity and recall, especially when intense focus is needed. While choline is in some foods, most people fall short.

Citicoline (2:52-3:47): Another choline-based compound that boosts acetylcholine and helps build healthy cell membranes. It can improve mental clarity and recall, particularly for multitasking and learning new things. Citicoline is not found in food and must be supplemented.

Curcumin (3:49-4:21): The active compound in turmeric that reduces inflammation and oxidative stress in the brain. It also boosts BDNF, which strengthens brain connections, leading to clearer thinking, better mood, and improved focus. Supplements often include black pepper extract for better absorption.

Magnesium L-Threonate (4:23-5:23): This is the only form of magnesium that effectively crosses into the brain, helping to calm overactive pathways, strengthen neuronal connections, and support long-term memory. It can alleviate poor sleep, racing thoughts, and difficulty concentrating. While found in some foods, most people don't get enough from diet alone.

Bacopa Monnieri (5:26-6:12): Helps improve memory by reducing oxidative stress and supporting serotonin and dopamine. It strengthens neural connections, making learning smoother and enhancing recall and concentration. Its beneficial compounds are primarily found in supplements.

L-Theanine (6:14-7:13): Promotes calm alertness, especially when paired with caffeine. It boosts alpha brain waves, leading to clean focus, longer attention, and fewer jitters than caffeine alone. It's found in green tea, but supplementing provides sufficient amounts to balance caffeine's effects.

Phosphatidylserine (7:16-8:02): A fatty compound that keeps brain cells flexible and responsive, ensuring fast and accurate signal transmission. It aids in smooth task switching, information processing, and managing the stress hormone cortisol. Small amounts are found in foods, but supplements provide the form the brain absorbs best.

Ginkgo Biloba (8:04-8:42): Boosts blood flow to the brain, delivering more oxygen and nutrients to areas responsible for memory, focus, and quick thinking. It also acts as an antioxidant, protecting neurons. The useful compounds come from leaf extract, typically consumed as a supplement.

-8cPMuMm2BE.author: @Droidiphile
-8cPMuMm2BE.text: I'm an old athlete, emphasis on old ...  All of us who have tried to maintain physical capability across a lifespan know that "something is better than nothing".   You do not need to be achieving "optimum heart rate" nor "fat burning".  You do need to exercise in ways that do more good than harm.  As we age, low impact aerobics, lighter weights, and a sensible diet are needed so you don't damage your body beyond its ability to rebuild.  If you are a younger athlete, hell yeah, push the envelope and invoke as much science as you can afford !!  But for us older people, old athlete or nor, you need heavy doses of common sense.
