2b1rAhcWZXw.author: @anthonybryan7191
2b1rAhcWZXw.text:
The claims in that video are factually incorrect or seriously misleading. 
1) “Algae DHA is a triglyceride, so it’s harmful”
False.
A triglyceride is simply a chemical structure: 3 fatty acids attached to glycerol.
This is the normal, natural form of fats in:
Human blood
Human body fat
Most whole foods (including plants)
Key point:
Being a triglyceride does NOT make something harmful.
What matters is:
The type of fatty acid (e.g. DHA vs saturated fat)
The overall diet context
Reality:
DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) from algae is a long-chain omega-3 fatty acid
It is consistently associated with:
Reduced inflammation
Improved cardiovascular markers
Brain function support
There is no evidence that DHA triglycerides from algae increase cancer or heart disease risk.
2) “It’s as bad as fish oil or meat for heart disease, stroke, cancer”
False.
This is a conflation error.
Evidence-based distinctions:
Fish oil / algae DHA:
Generally linked to:
Lower triglycerides in blood
Slight reduction in cardiovascular risk (context dependent)
Not strongly linked to cancer risk
Meat (especially processed meat):
Associated with:
Increased risk of cardiovascular disease
Increased risk of colorectal cancer (classified by World Health Organization)
Important nuance:
Omega-3 fats (including DHA) are biologically protective, not harmful
The risk profile of meat has nothing to do with triglyceride structure
3) “DHA causes heart attacks or strokes”
False.
DHA and EPA are among the most studied fats in nutrition science
They are generally associated with:
Reduced blood triglycerides
Anti-inflammatory effects
Improved endothelial function (blood vessel health)
Clinical reality:
Some large trials show modest benefit, not harm
No credible evidence shows DHA causes cardiovascular events in healthy individuals
4) “The brain cannot absorb or use DHA”
Completely false.
This is the most incorrect claim.
Biological facts:
DHA is a major structural fat in the brain
~20–30% of the brain’s fatty acids are DHA
The brain uses DHA for:
Neuron membrane fluidity
Synapse function
Neurotransmission
Mechanism:
DHA crosses the blood-brain barrier via a transporter (MFSD2A)
Evidence:
DHA deficiency is associated with:
Cognitive decline
Poor visual development (in infants)
So the claim that the brain cannot use DHA is flatly wrong.
5) Is algae DHA different from fish DHA?
No meaningful difference in function.
Fish get DHA by eating algae
Algae oil is simply the original source
Practical advantage of algae:
No contaminants (e.g. mercury)
Vegan-compatible
Controlled DHA levels

_zlGkng8lqo.author: @LoevensRambles
_zlGkng8lqo.text: After losing 36 kgs my biology started working against me. I have been worse at eating healthy and craved more snacks and unhealthy foods. The number on the scales didn't move, and I was constantly cold and tired and unknowingly I had become less active. I decided to take a break from the stress of having to think about what I ate all the time, but still making sure to get my protein. But the big win for me on Wegovy is, that when I don't think of what I eat and just live my life, I don't gain weight anymore, that was unheard of before. So I decided to count that as a victory in itself. The scale didn't move for at least 4 months. I started feeling less tired, less inactive and like my body finally realized that it wasn't starving.  I became ready and motivated to give weightloss another go. So I went back to eating healthier foods and cutting down on snacks etc. And the weight started coming off almost immediately. I feel like I need to be on Wegovy for the rest of my life, because if it wasnt for Wegovy, I could easily had put those 36 kgs back on in those 4 months. Instead the weight stood still and now it's going down again. It's nice to know that I can be gentle with myself and give myself breaks every now and then, without gaining any weight. I now count  not gaining weight, as just as big of a victory as the weightloss. I am about half the way to my goal weight, and it may take longer than I first thought but I know I will get there.

It’s easy to get to hung up on the “losing weight” vs actually just maintaining what is already lost.

K5Hmgbxavls.author: @David69D

K5Hmgbxavls.author: @eddiegardner8232

K5Hmgbxavls.author: @luisviera4215

Nzg3qRNZLZQ.author: @lindaamea
Nzg3qRNZLZQ.text:
I’ve had a very critical relationship with myself since my early teens and never truly felt at home in my femininity. The way you embody and express femininity has felt like such a healing mirror for me, gently reflecting back a version of myself that feels softer and more true. Through your presence, I’ve started to soften old wounds and relate to myself with a more nurturing energy instead of continuing to fight myself.
You’ve also opened my eyes to how loud mainstream “health” narratives are and how freeing it is to step away from them and return to my own inner rhythm. The feminine energy you share feels sensual, grounded, and strong in a way that is deeply inspiring and gives me courage to trust my own body and intuition.
Thank you for creating this beautiful, honest space and for the way you honor women through your work 🦋✨💖

5ccJtbtBUSI.author: @Raazorred1
5ccJtbtBUSI.text:
Lever 1.  Yup!  If you nail the your Protein Needs, but stay at or under your Calorie goal, the rest takes care of itself.  That's why your dad could have his ice cream!!  This is huge and is very easy calculate, even if you don't have labels and scales to be exact, there are Apps or AI to fill in the gaps.  I keep track in my head but it's probably best to keep tabs on your mobile.  It helps that I only eat two meals per day with 18 hour fasts in between. 

Lever 2:  Listen to your body!  I am 60 and sometimes I do two intense workouts per week, and other times I'll do 5 light workouts.  Arms, Chest, & Abs are what I want to see, but I don't ignore back & legs.  This I do keep track of in a daily journal.  I want to see constant gains!

Levers 3 & 4... you have to get it done!  When your phone rings, the headset goes on and pace.  Do a fasting walk in the morning.  Any amount of time.  Take a walk after each meal.  Again, ANY amount of time.  Park far away and walk to the doors of the office or store.  Take stairs.  SLEEP!  There are tricks people can Google to sleep better.  8 hours, minimum!  The body needs to rest & heal.

Great video.  Subscribed. 👍👍💪💪

My breakfast is about 50 grams of whole complete proteins. Lunch is about 40 grams of whole complete protein,  dinner is about 30 grams of whole complete protein.  Notice is repeated WHOLE COMPLETE PROTEINS... Cause that's an important thing! 
Here's the problem  spiking your glucose by consuming carbs -  IS NOT A GOOD THING..  Unless you're wearing a bio sensor like stello or a CGM.... you'll have zero clue where your insulin is going after the consumption of a meal.   
Please share with us all WarriorBabe.. what DR out there would be willing to tell you that: 
1) Carbs (125 to 150 grams per day)  wont cause your insulin to rise more than 20 mg/dl after consumption like 30+ mg/dl .. 30 mg/dl is a SPIKE, 
2) That spiking your insulin is OK.. 
3) That spiking your insulin like that, doenst cause Insulin Resistance
I'll wait.but I'm pretty sure I'll get crickets.. on those questions.. right?

nXn2iM47m-c.author: @stevelenores5637
nXn2iM47m-c.text: Is the Fermi Paradox even a valid formula?  It's okay if are starting with zero information but we aren't anymore.  It can be reduced a great deal.  First planets within the habitable zone is plentiful enough to make this factor one for all stars close to the size of our sun.  The real limiting factor is a planet that has an orbit that stays in the proper orbit with an atmosphere.  Having a large moon is a plus but not absolutely necessary.  The planet is probably the significant factor and this factor is nearly zero.  Do reduce it to the right star in a friendly neighborhood and the right sized planet in a stable habitable orbit gives you a much better estimate. This reduces the number of possiblities to reasonable number.  It also tells you SETI is a complete waste one you have eliminated stars within a few lightyears away.  Even a one message sent and received a decade apart is pretty worthless.  Plus traveling even one light year is a death sentence.

NImdA45VRzQ.text: I’m 67, and for years I thought that mid-morning crash around 10 a.m. was just part of getting older. I’d start my day with oatmeal or toast and always felt hungry again within a couple of hours. A few weeks ago I switched to a simple breakfast with eggs and some vegetables, and I was honestly surprised how steady my energy became. I’m not reaching for snacks as much, and my focus feels a lot clearer through the morning. Has anyone else here tried changing just their breakfast and noticed a bigger difference than expected?

NImdA45VRzQ.author: @doctoradvised-g1l
NImdA45VRzQ.text: "The information in this video was truly a game changer for me! I tried eating eggs and vegetables before carbohydrates as the doctor advised and felt much more stable in terms of energy, no longer experiencing those gnawing hunger pangs at 10-11 am. Thank you so much to Dr. Emily Carter Senior's Health Tips channel, I hope the doctor will release more videos like this!"

NImdA45VRzQ.author: @EatSmartBeatDisease
NImdA45VRzQ.text:
Wow, Dr. Emily, this is an absolute masterclass on morning nutrition! 🤯🍳 I’ve been struggling with that 10 AM brain fog and energy crash for years, and your explanation of the 'dawn effect' and how a high-carb breakfast spikes our blood sugar completely blew my mind. 📉 The 'traffic light' analogy for protein makes so much sense! 🚦 I never realized that eating toast or drinking fruit juice before my eggs was quietly sabotaging my entire day.

Starting tomorrow, I’m officially taking your 7-day challenge! 🥑🥚 I'll be prioritizing my metabolic shield: protein, healthy fats, and fiber first, and saving any carbs for the very end of the meal. To anyone reading the comments and wondering if they should try this: pay attention to the science Dr. Emily breaks down here. It's a total game-changer for metabolic health! 💪🔥 Thank you for making such complex medical knowledge so accessible and actionable. Keep up the amazing work! 👏✨

It saved my life literally I am in early remission and cancer free right now and I still keep exercising and have learned what my body needs and doesn't need. It is just awesome!

jmDD04Siu68.author: @tennisguybri
jmDD04Siu68.text: Always love Dr Gabrielle's videos. I had widow maker heart attack 5 years ago at 40 with no warning signs and no symptoms. Ate well and was tennis pro for most of my life. Ate what I thought was well, exercised, etc. Woke up in an ICU and doctors were perplexed how my arteries were 98 percent shut. They asked how I ate, if I smoked drank etc. and I said I did everything text book according to what I was told is healthy. So this video is great because no one truly knows what is good or not good for their body. We know protein is necessary etc. but all these videos these days on the net tell us this food is good or this food is bad, so I like Dr Gabrielle's approach to science. Stay active, build muscle, eat some protein, some other food etc and don't just exercise to look good, but don't to feel strong and feel good. Keep up the great videos Dr gabrielle. You are amazing. I will keep fighting on the tennis court and try to find the right foods that are good for my body type 😂

jmDD04Siu68.author: @Westworldjunkie
jmDD04Siu68.text:
So glad to hear Layne touch on some of the "predatory marketing" around age and sex. The idea that menopausal women NEED to train differently or can't do certain things has become kind of weirdly insidious. Same thing for guys and aging, like the whole "men over 40"  thing.

Like Layne said, obviously there are considerations with age and sex - so it's not to say there's nothing there in regard to what's being marketed. But as a 36 year old guy, I constantly find my mind going to this place of "I've only got 4 years left till I'm 40 and then it's all downhill from there...I better pack on as much muscle now while I can." 

And while that may still be a good thing to do, it kinda sucks that my mind just sort of automatically defaults to 40 years old being some kind of hard cutoff age for men and building muscle. Takes away joy in the idea of simply training for life and not ascribing the bummer factor of what training should look like at certain ages.

szi-npHzS4A.author: @maireadweaver8952
szi-npHzS4A.text: I am Irish and we have a public healthcare system here which, whilst broken in many places and has a LOT wrong with it, at least people can get healthcare without bankrupting themselves. Our GP system is under huge stress with a shortage of GPs and an increasing population, HOWEVER, even though you may be waiting a couple of weeks for non-emergency appointments, you can still establish a good relationship and have regular check-ups within the system. Whenever I have travelled to the US I have been completely shocked that no-one I met actually has a GP that they see regularly. On the handful of occasions, whilst in the US, where we needed a GP/afterhours GP for mild illness like allergic reactions to insect bites or a mislaid prescription, we were funnelled into an ER situation which seemed bonkers -  and such an unnecessary waste of ER staff time (not to mention how embarrassed we were going to an ER with such relatively trivial medical issues, and the consequent cost). In the absence of regular advice and follow-up treatments from a GP, in addition to the astronomical costs charged in the US, it is no wonder people resort to Dr Google/Gwynnie/Snake-oil salesmen. IMHO.

zTKKWBbljls.text: I'm 80, healthy and an active hiker. I have live on a farm in Tobagongirbthe past 14 years, following  45 years in the USA as an active professional. I've not had a cold or any illness in 15 years or more. I regularly eat about 10 fruits and vegetables daily and fish. I also eat eggs, sardines, oats,  chick peas, garlic, onions, ginger. I add my home-grown mix of a 9-herb pesto (Basil, Tulsie, Chadon Beni, Tarragon, Turmeric, garlic, ginger Oregano, Thyme, and Rosemary) to my stir fried vegetables, vinaigrette, soups, fish-before baking. I plan to learn to make cottage cheese at home, after hearing your presentation. It's very expensive where I am. I also am surrounded by a satisfying spiritual community. Thanks for your list. I love to benchmark my wellness and your discussion was very useful. Warm regards from Eden's Door Tobago.

7tXXgqrmji0.author: @SeniorBeWell
7tXXgqrmji0.text: This is one of the most important messages seniors need to hear. Walking alone does not fix poor leg circulation after 60. As a physical therapist, I see this every week, people doing everything right, walking daily, elevating legs, wearing compression socks, yet still dealing with swelling, cold feet, and balance problems. The calf muscle pump is the real driver of circulation, and this video explains it clearly and correctly. The emphasis on controlled calf holds and time under tension is exactly what restores blood flow and reduces fall risk. If you are over 60 and worried about your legs, this is not optional information, it is essential.

7tXXgqrmji0.author: @StrongSeniors365
7tXXgqrmji0.text: This really changed how I think about leg circulation after 60. I used to believe walking alone was enough, yet the swelling and cold feet never fully improved. Adding these simple, focused movements made me feel warmer, steadier, and more confident on my feet over time. It’s encouraging to see how small, intentional exercises can protect independence and spark meaningful conversations about aging stronger together.
